text: DreamClinic GP Clinic Operator’s Framework
Complete 16-Part Operations Manual for a 4–5 FTE General Practice Clinic Victoria, Australia
Prepared for: Nova De Silva DreamClinic Acquisition Project
April 2026

Disclaimer: All content in this framework has been prepared from expert knowledge of Australian general practice clinic operations. No clinic documents were provided as part of this engagement. Items marked “Not yet reviewed” or “External verification needed” require review against actual clinic records before reliance. This document does not constitute legal, financial, or medical advice.
GP CLINIC OPERATOR’S MANUAL — PARTS 1 & 2
Executive Overview & Management Structure
An Australian General Practice Acquisition Manual (Victoria)

Document Version: 1.0
Date Prepared: April 2026
Scope: 4–5 FTE GP Clinic
Location Context: Victoria, Australia
Disclaimer: This manual is built from expert knowledge of Australian general practice operations. External verification is required for specific compliance items, legislation updates, and financial benchmarking. Consult accountants, lawyers, and accreditation bodies for current requirements.
PART 1 — EXECUTIVE OPERATOR OVERVIEW
Section 1.1 — How a 4–5 FTE GP Clinic Functions as a Business
Revenue Model & Financial Scale
A typical 4–5 FTE (full-time equivalent) general practice in Victoria operates as a small services business with highly variable revenue depending on billing model and patient demographics.

Billing Models: - Bulk-billing clinics: GPs receive payment directly from Medicare for eligible services; patient pays zero gap. Operator’s revenue = Medicare fee schedule × activity + rebates for chronic disease programs. Simpler administration but lower margins and patient volume dependency. - Mixed-billing clinics: GPs charge patient directly; patient receives Medicare rebate and pays the gap (difference). Allows higher per-consultation revenue but requires active collections and impacts some patient demographics. Higher administration overhead. - Private clinics: No Medicare; all revenue from patients. Highest margins but smallest market, requires strong reputation/location.

Typical Annual Revenue Range (4–5 FTE): - Bulk-billing, average demographics: $1.5M–$2.2M annually - Mixed-billing, average demographics: $2.0M–$3.0M annually - High-volume bulk + chronic disease programs: $2.5M–$3.5M annually - Source: Expert knowledge. External verification needed via AMA, RACGP survey data, and local practice benchmarking.

Medicare as Revenue Driver: - Medicare rebates comprise 75–85% of gross revenue in bulk-billing clinics, 60–70% in mixed-billing - Remaining revenue: private services (travel medicine, work cover, insurance medicals), allied health, rent from subtenants, ancillary (pathology, dispensing) - Source: Expert knowledge.
Cost Structure & Operating Margins
A sustainable 4–5 FTE practice typically operates on gross margins of 35–55%, with EBITDA (earnings before interest, tax, depreciation, amortisation) of 20–35%.
Typical Cost Breakdown (as % of gross billings):

Key Financial Reality: - A $2.5M clinic with 35% GP payments, 10% staff, 10% rent, and 5% other operating costs = ~40% EBITDA (~$1M before tax and owner drawings) - Cash flow timing is critical: Medicare pays on 14–21-day cycle; payroll is weekly/fortnightly; lease is monthly - Source: Expert knowledge.
Goodwill & Patient List Valuation
The value of a general practice is driven by two factors: the patient list (recurring revenue from enrolled patients) and goodwill (reputation, systems, GP relationships).

EBITDA Multiple Approach: - Typical Australian GP practice valuation: 3.5–6.0x EBITDA depending on: - Practice stability (years operating, GP tenure, patient growth) - Billing model (bulk-billing slightly lower multiple; mixed-billing higher) - Specialisation (chronic disease programs, allied health, aged care increase value) - Location (metro higher; rural lower unless isolation loading applies) - Receivables quality and collection rate - Source: Expert knowledge. External verification needed via practice valuation firms (e.g., Pitcher Partners, Grant Thornton healthcare valuations).

Patient List as Asset: - A “healthy” patient list has 85%+ active consulting rate (seen in last 12 months) - Breakup of patient list between long-term patients (low churn) vs transient (high churn) affects stability - Chronic disease programs (diabetes, cardiovascular, respiratory) improve patient stickiness and revenue per patient - Source: Expert knowledge.

Goodwill Components: - GP employment relationships and non-compete agreements - Clinical accreditation (RACGP, QIP) - IT systems and workflows - Staff relationships (especially practice manager and nursing team) - Referral relationships (specialists, allied health, pathology) - Source: Expert knowledge.
Bulk-Billing vs Mixed-Billing: Operator Perspective

Key Financial Ratios the Owner Must Track

1. Billings Ratio (per GP FTE) - Formula: Gross monthly billings ÷ FTE count - Healthy range: $40K–$60K per FTE per month (varies by bulk vs mixed) - Red flag: <$35K/month/FTE = underutilisation or patient loss
2. Collections Rate (mixed-billing only) - Formula: Patient gap payments received ÷ patient gap charges issued - Healthy range: 92–98% - Red flag: <90% = collections problem or bad-debt exposure
3. Cost-to-Income Ratio - Formula: Total operating expenses ÷ gross billings - Healthy range: 60–70% - Red flag: >75% = margin compression; operational inefficiency
4. Staff Cost Ratio - Formula: (Nurse + reception + admin wages) ÷ gross billings - Healthy range: 13–20% - Red flag: >22% = overstaffing or low utilisation
5. EBITDA Margin - Formula: EBITDA ÷ gross billings - Healthy range: 25–40% - Red flag: <20% = below-market performance; review pricing, volume, costs
6. Days Cash on Hand - Formula: (Current bank balance) ÷ (daily operating expenses) - Healthy range: 20–35 days - Red flag: <10 days = acute cash flow risk; <5 days = crisis level
7. Receivables Aging (mixed-billing) - % of invoiced gaps >60 days old - Healthy range: <5% - Red flag: >15% = collections breakdown
8. Medicare Compliance Billing Accuracy - % of claims initially accepted (not rejected/required amendment) - Healthy range: >98% - Red flag: <95% = billing leakage; potential audit risk
9. Patient Churn Rate (Active to Inactive) - Formula: Patients not seen in 12 months ÷ total patient list - Healthy range: 15–20% annually - Red flag: >30% = patient loss; investigate cause
10. GP Retention - Months/years in role for each GP - Healthy range: >3 years median tenure - Red flag: Average <18 months = high turnover risk
Source: Expert knowledge. Recommend quarterly financial review with accountant.
Section 1.2 — Main Management Domains
An effective operator must oversee eight interconnected domains. Each is critical; weakness in any one undermines the whole business.
1. Clinical Governance (Accreditation, Credentialing, Clinical Risk)
Purpose: Ensure clinical quality, patient safety, and regulatory compliance.

What This Covers: - RACGP accreditation and Quality Improvement Plan (QIP) compliance - GP registration and scope of practice (registration with AHPRA) - Credentialing of clinical staff (nurses, allied health) - Clinical protocols, guidelines, and evidence-based practice - Incident reporting and risk management - Patient safety systems (medication safety, infection control) - Peer review and GP performance monitoring - Clinical supervision (especially for junior GPs, nurses) - Mandatory reporting (child abuse, elder abuse, patient safety concerns) - Professional development and continuing medical education (CME) compliance

Why Owner Must Oversee: - Accreditation lapse = cannot bill Medicare = instant revenue loss - Clinical incident = liability, regulatory investigation, reputation damage - Non-compliance with mandatory reporting = personal criminal liability - Source: Expert knowledge.
2. Human Resources (GPs, Nurses, Admin, Contractors)
Purpose: Recruit, manage, develop, and retain clinical and administrative staff.

What This Covers: - GP contracting and employment agreements (including restraint of trade) - Nurse and admin recruitment, onboarding, probation, performance management - Contractor vs employee classification (avoiding sham contracting) - Industrial relations compliance (National Employment Standards, Awards) - Performance management and disciplinary processes - Professional development and training - Succession planning and knowledge transfer - Staff turnover management and exit interviews - Workplace culture and conflict resolution - Occupational health and safety (bullying, harassment, stress management)

Why Owner Must Oversee: - GP departure = instant 15–25% revenue loss (typically) - Unfair dismissal claim = legal costs, damages, admin burden - Award non-compliance = back-pay liability + penalties - Sham contracting = ATO liability + penalties + reputational damage - Source: Expert knowledge.
3. Finance and Cashflow (Billings, Reconciliation, BAS, Payroll)
Purpose: Manage revenue, payments, banking, and financial obligations.

What This Covers: - Weekly/monthly revenue billing and reconciliation (Medicare, patient, private) - Bank account management and cash flow forecasting - Payroll processing (wages, superannuation, tax withholding) - Business Activity Statement (BAS) compliance (quarterly GST, PAYG, superannuation) - Accounts receivable (gap invoicing, collections, write-offs) - Accounts payable (supplier invoices, rent, utilities) - GP contractor payments and GST reconciliation - Financial reporting (P&L, balance sheet, cash position) - Tax planning and compliance (income tax, GST, superannuation guarantee) - Audit and review of financial statements

Why Owner Must Oversee: - Cash flow mismanagement = inability to pay staff/suppliers (insolvency risk) - BAS non-compliance = ATO penalties, interest, potential prosecution - Payroll errors = staff disputes, ATO audits, reputational damage - Billing leakage = underreported income = lost revenue + tax audit exposure - Source: Expert knowledge.
4. Medicare and Digital Health Compliance (PRODA, HPOS, Provider Numbers)
Purpose: Maintain eligibility to bill Medicare and comply with digital health standards.

What This Covers: - PRODA (Practitioner Register) registration for all GPs and authorised representatives - Medicare provider number maintenance and annual declarations - HPOS (Health Professional Online Services) access for staff - Electronic Prescribing Systems (ePS) compliance - My Health Record (MHR) participation and data sharing agreements - Bulk-billing compliance (correct item numbers, gap declaration) - Medicare claim submission accuracy (PRODA, HPOS audits) - Shared Health Summary and Event Summary participation - Compliance with PBS restrictions (prescribing, restricted drugs) - Telehealth billing compliance (eligibility, documentation)

Why Owner Must Oversee: - PRODA lapse = cannot submit Medicare claims = instant revenue loss - Bulk-billing compliance breach = debt recovery + audit + potential suspension - Telehealth non-compliance = claim rejection + audit risk - Digital health non-compliance = increasing audit focus by Services Australia - Source: Expert knowledge; external verification needed via Services Australia, AHPRA.
5. Facilities and Property (Lease, Maintenance, Utilities)
Purpose: Maintain safe, compliant, accessible clinical premises.

What This Covers: - Lease agreement management (term, renewal, renewal dates, make-good obligations) - Rent and utilities payment (electricity, water, gas, internet, phone) - Building maintenance and repairs (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, general upkeep) - Infection control and cleanliness standards - Disability access (ramps, toilets, parking, signage) - Medical waste disposal and biohazard handling - Cold chain for vaccines (refrigerator maintenance, temperature monitoring logs) - Safety equipment (fire extinguishers, first aid, defibrillator, emergency lighting) - Pest control and hygiene standards - Building insurance and landlord relationship management

Why Owner Must Oversee: - Lease lapse/non-renewal = loss of premises (60–90 days to relocate) - Cold chain breach = vaccine spoilage = clinical incident + regulatory breach - Disability access breach = DDA complaint + remediation costs - Maintenance neglect = patient safety risk + accreditation impact - Source: Expert knowledge.
6. IT and Systems (Clinical Software, Backups, Cyber Security)
Purpose: Maintain secure, reliable clinical IT infrastructure and data.

What This Covers: - Clinical software (EHR) selection, licensing, configuration, and support (e.g., Best Practice, Medical Director, Genie) - Backup and disaster recovery (daily backups, offsite copies, recovery testing) - Cybersecurity (antivirus, firewalls, password management, multi-factor authentication) - Data encryption (in transit and at rest) - Staff IT training (phishing awareness, password hygiene, ransomware risk) - Remote access security (VPN, secure login, device management if staff work from home) - Compliance with security standards (ACSQHC, Privacy Act, Notifiable Data Breaches Scheme) - IT vendor management and SLAs (response time, availability) - Software updates and patch management - Business continuity and downtime recovery planning

Why Owner Must Oversee: - Data breach = notifiable to OAIC + patients + media (major reputational damage) - Ransomware = clinic shutdown + data loss + recovery costs + Bitcoin demand - Backup failure = loss of patient records (unrecoverable in many cases) - Cyber incident = regulatory investigation + professional indemnity claim - Source: Expert knowledge.
7. Supplier and Partner Management (Pathology, Consumables, Waste)
Purpose: Source and manage relationships with external service providers.

What This Covers: - Pathology provider contract (collection, reporting, credit terms) - Vaccine and pharmaceutical supplier relationships - Medical consumables (swabs, needles, dressings) procurement - Waste disposal and biohazard contractor - Cleaning and maintenance contractor - Telehealth provider (if using third-party service) - IT vendor and support contracts - Accountant, payroll processor, legal advisor relationships - Allied health subtenants (if applicable) - Pricing, payment terms, and service quality monitoring

Why Owner Must Oversee: - Pathology delays = patient dissatisfaction + clinical risk - Consumable stockouts = inability to deliver services - Waste non-compliance = DEC breach + reputational risk - Contractor disputes = service interruption or billing disputes - Source: Expert knowledge.
8. Business Development (GP Recruitment, Patient Growth, Services)
Purpose: Grow the clinic’s revenue and market share sustainably.

What This Covers: - GP recruitment and contractor acquisition (advertising, interviewing, onboarding) - Patient acquisition and retention strategies - New service development (chronic disease programs, women’s health, sexual health, etc.) - Marketing and reputation management (website, reviews, signage, referral networks) - Partnerships with specialists, allied health, aged care facilities - Telehealth expansion (if aligned with clinic strategy) - Performance benchmarking and improvement initiatives - Referral relationship building (local hospitals, specialists, community health services) - Patient feedback and satisfaction monitoring - Community engagement and health promotion

Why Owner Must Oversee: - Stagnant growth = declining profitability + vulnerability to competition - Poor GP recruitment = revenue gap + existing staff overload - Weak referral relationships = lost revenue opportunities - Source: Expert knowledge.
Section 1.3 — Biggest Operational Risks for an Owner-Operator
A 4–5 FTE practice is vulnerable to 12 major operational risks. Each can cause significant financial or reputational harm within weeks.
1. GP Departure (Revenue Loss)
Risk: One or two GPs generate 50–70% of clinic revenue. Sudden departure = 15–25% revenue loss + emergency workload redistribution + patient complaints.
Why It Happens: Competing practice offers better terms, GP relocates, health/burnout issues, conflict with owner/team, maternity leave.
Consequence: Immediate cash flow gap (Medicare already billed to departing GP), staff morale impact, patient loss (5–15% follow departing GP), difficulty recruiting replacement at short notice.
Mitigation: Documented employment/contracting terms with reasonable notice period; strong peer support; competitive remuneration; non-compete agreement (if enforceable); relationship management.
Source: Expert knowledge.
2. Medicare Non-Compliance / Audit

Risk: Services Australia audits billing patterns; finds non-compliant claims (wrong item numbers, ineligible services, invalid provider details, missing documentation). Clinic receives debt notice for repayment + penalties.
Why It Happens: Staff error in billing; GP unfamiliar with item restrictions; intentional/reckless billing; poor documentation.
Consequence: Repayment of claimed amounts (potentially $50K–$500K+); audit costs (legal, accountancy); reputational damage; potential provider suspension.
Mitigation: Monthly billing audits; staff training on item restrictions; PRODA compliance checks; documentation standards; professional indemnity insurance.
Source: Expert knowledge; external verification needed via Services Australia.
3. Billing Leakage (Under-Billing / Missed Items)
Risk: GPs bill at lower item number than warranted, or forget to bill ancillary items (e.g., TCAs, aged care reviews, chronic disease programs). Clinic leaves 5–15% of revenue on the table.
Why It Happens: Time pressure; unfamiliarity with current item schedule; staff error in claims processing; GP habit or preference.
Consequence: Lost revenue (e.g., $50K–$150K annually for a $2M clinic); depressed profitability; cash flow impact.
Mitigation: Regular billing audits (monthly); GP education on item numbers; clinical software configuration to prompt correct items; rebate reconciliation.
Source: Expert knowledge.
4. Key Person Dependency (Practice Manager / Single GP)
Risk: Practice manager or single high-revenue GP has no backup. Sudden absence (illness, accident, departure) cripples operations.
Why It Happens: Lack of succession planning; knowledge not documented; no cross-training of staff.
Consequence: Operational chaos; delayed billing; missed compliance deadlines; patient appointments cancelled; staff stress and errors.
Mitigation: Document all key processes; cross-train staff; have contingency plan for practice manager absence; identify backup GP or recruitment plan.
Source: Expert knowledge.
5. Cash Flow Gaps (Medicare Lag vs Payroll Obligations)
Risk: Medicare pays 14–21 days after claim submission. Payroll is weekly/fortnightly. Large unpaid gap invoices (mixed-billing) mean cash outflows exceed inflows. Clinic runs short of cash.
Why It Happens: Low opening cash balance; slow patient collections; unexpected expense; high GP contractor payments reducing net cash retained.
Consequence: Unable to meet payroll, rent, or supplier invoices; staff stress; potential insolvency; overdraft fees.

Mitigation: Cash flow forecasting (rolling 13-week forecast); maintain minimum 20–30 days of operating expenses in reserve; line of credit facility available; monitor receivables aging; negotiate payment terms with suppliers.
Source: Expert knowledge.
6. Accreditation Failure (Loss of Eligibility to Operate)
Risk: Practice loses RACGP accreditation due to non-compliance with QIP standards or failure in accreditation audit. Loss of accreditation = cannot bill Medicare = clinic becomes unviable.

Why It Happens: Non-compliance with clinical governance standards; gaps in documentation, infection control, or safety procedures; GP doesn’t hold sufficient CPD points; management neglect of accreditation obligations.
Consequence: Immediate loss of Medicare billing; 4–8 week accreditation reapplication process; interim revenue loss; reputational damage; staff concern about clinic viability.

Mitigation: Appoint accreditation/quality lead (internal or external consultant); conduct annual self-audit against QIP standards; maintain documentation; monitor GP CPD compliance; plan accreditation audit 6 months ahead.
Source: Expert knowledge; external verification needed via RACGP.
7. Privacy Breach / Cyber Incident
Risk: Patient data is accessed, stolen, encrypted by ransomware, or shared without consent. Clinic must notify affected patients + OAIC + media. Regulatory investigation follows.
Why It Happens: Weak cybersecurity (no MFA, poor antivirus, outdated software); staff phishing (click malicious link, enter credentials); ransomware attack; disgruntled staff member copying data.

Consequence: Notification costs, legal fees, regulatory fine, reputational damage (patient loss), potential clinical software downtime, cyber insurance excess/deductible, forensic investigation costs.
Mitigation: Strong IT security (MFA, antivirus, regular backups, secure offsite storage); staff training on phishing; cyber liability insurance; incident response plan; regular security audits.
Source: Expert knowledge.
8. Sham Contracting Exposure (ATO / IR Liability)
Risk: Contractor GPs are classified as employees by ATO or Fair Work. Clinic liable for unpaid superannuation, PAYG tax, and penalties.
Why It Happens: Contractor has tools provided, fixed hours, direct supervision, integration into team (control tests suggest employment relationship); no genuine independent contractor structure.
Consequence: Back-payment of super (often 11.5% of earnings for past 6 years) + interest + penalties; ATO audit; reputational damage; potential IR claim for unfair dismissal rights.

Mitigation: Proper contractor agreement (genuine independent contractor terms); GP uses own tools/equipment; flexible hours; no direct supervision; indemnity insurance; legal review of contractor status.
Source: Expert knowledge; external verification needed via ATO, Fair Work Ombudsman.
9. Cold Chain Breach (Vaccine Spoilage / Regulatory Consequences)

Risk: Vaccine refrigerator failure or improper storage leads to spoilage of stock (e.g., HPV, MMR, COVID vaccines). Clinic unaware vaccines are compromised. Patients unknowingly receive ineffective vaccines.
Why It Happens: Refrigerator malfunction; temperature logs not monitored; vaccine handling error (left out of fridge); poor staff training on cold chain.

Consequence: Patient safety incident (reputational, regulatory); clinic liable to replace vaccines; potential TGA notification; accreditation impact; staff liability; mandatory reporting of vaccine failure.

Mitigation: Quality refrigerator with alarm and temperature monitoring; daily temperature logs (electronic preferred); staff training on cold chain; regular fridge maintenance; regular stock audits; backup fridge on site.
Source: Expert knowledge; external verification needed via TGA, NACI.
10. Lease Risk (Non-Renewal / Make-Good Costs)

Risk: Lease term expires. Landlord does not offer renewal. Clinic has 60–90 days to find and fit-out new premises or close. Make-good obligations require expensive reinstatement (painting, carpet, fitouts).
Why It Happens: Landlord redevelops building; competing tenant offers higher rent; poor landlord relationship.

Consequence: Relocation costs ($50K–$200K+); business disruption (patient appointments cancelled); staff turnover (staff won’t relocate); make-good disputes with landlord; temporary revenue loss during transition.
Mitigation: Lease terms reviewed 12 months before expiry; maintain good landlord relationship; negotiate renewal option in lease; budget for make-good; have site contingency plan.
Source: Expert knowledge.
11. Staff Conflict / Unfair Dismissal Claim

Risk: Manager dismisses staff member (nurse, reception, admin). Employee claims unfair dismissal (no warnings, discriminatory reason, inadequate process). Clinic must defend claim in Fair Work Commission.
Why It Happens: Poor performance management process; manager personality conflict; disability or cultural discrimination; inadequate documentation of performance issues.
Consequence: Legal costs ($10K–$50K+); reputational damage (staff culture); management distraction; potential compensation order; appeal process (months of uncertainty).
Mitigation: Document performance issues; follow fair dismissal procedures (warnings, opportunity to respond); HR advice; ensure legitimate reason for dismissal; maintain good staff relations.
Source: Expert knowledge.
12. Payroll Non-Compliance (Award Underpayment / Super Gaps)
Risk: Clinic pays staff below Modern Award minimum rates or misses superannuation contributions. Staff or FWO investigation reveals breach.
Why It Happens: Payroll system misconfiguration; casual staff paid flat hourly rate (missing super); incorrect leave calculations; poor payroll processor understanding of Award.
Consequence: Back-payment of wages + interest; super contributions + interest + penalties; FWO investigation; reputational damage; staff grievances; potential compensation claim.

Mitigation: Payroll processed by qualified payroll service; annual payroll audit against Medical Practitioners Award; super contributions verified quarterly; staff paid slips reviewed; HR advice on leave entitlements.
Source: Expert knowledge; external verification needed via Fair Work Ombudsman, ATO.
