(2) Google reviews: monitor and analyse patient reviews on Google Business Profile monthly. Positive reviews highlight clinic strengths (thorough GP, friendly staff, quick appointment access). Negative reviews highlight issues (long wait, rude receptionist, misdiagnosis). Identify themes and address. (3) Patient complaints: analyse complaint register quarterly to identify service issues. (4) Action on feedback: if feedback highlights issue (e.g., long wait times, receptionist rudeness), take corrective action (adjust schedule, retrain staff, invest in additional reception staff if needed). Communicate improvement to patients (follow-up message: “We heard your feedback about wait times and have adjusted our schedule. Thank you for helping us improve.”). Demonstrates clinic listens and acts on feedback.

GP retention strategy (fair billing split, flexible hours, supportive culture, CPD support): GPs are clinic’s most valuable resource. Retention strategy to keep experienced GPs and attract new talent: (1) Fair billing: if GPs paid on commission basis (common in mixed-ownership clinics), ensure split is fair and competitive. Market rate: 50–70% of billing to GP (varies by location and clinic size). Transparent: GP should know exactly what percentage they receive and how calculated. Avoid opaque billing arrangements (hidden deductions, arbitrary reductions). (2) Flexible hours: offer flexible scheduling (part-time options, job share, preferred days off). GPs with families, study plans, or approaching retirement value flexibility. Strict 9-to-5 rigidity causes burnout and departure. (3) Supportive culture: foster collaborative, non-hierarchical culture. Regular GP meetings, input on clinic direction, respect for clinical autonomy (GPs not micromanaged). Positive workplace culture reduces turnover. (4) Continuing Professional Development (CPD): support GPs’ learning and career development. Allocate CPD budget ($2,000–$5,000/year per GP), allow time for training, encourage conference attendance, support fellowship training (RACGP Fellowship, specialist training). GPs who feel stagnant or undervalued leave for better opportunities. (5) Recognition: acknowledge GPs’ contributions (performance bonuses, awards, public acknowledgement). (6) Competitive remuneration: pay competitive rates relative to local market (recruit locums and new GPs at competitive rates, benchmark against other clinics). Underpaying drives departures to higher-paying clinics.

Competitive landscape monitoring: Understand local competitive environment to position clinic effectively: (1) Identify competitor clinics (other GPs, clinics in local area), assess: location, opening hours, services offered, GPs employed, online reputation (Google reviews, patient feedback). (2) Competitive advantages: what makes clinic unique? (e.g., 24/7 access, specific services like women’s health, procedural capacity, bulk-billing policy, particular GP expertise). (3) Market trends: any new clinics opening nearby (threat to patient base), any services becoming popular (opportunity to expand), patient preferences changing (e.g., telehealth increasing). (4) Benchmarking: compare clinic performance (patient volume, revenue, GP utilisation) against local and national benchmarks (industry reports from RACGP, medical management surveys). (5) Strategic response: if competitor clinic opening nearby, may need to: improve marketing, expand services, adjust pricing, invest in patient experience to retain patients. Monitor competitor clinics’ websites, Google reviews, patient feedback to stay informed.

Annual strategic plan: At minimum annually, conduct strategic planning exercise: (1) Review past year: financial performance (revenue, expenses, profit margin), patient metrics (growth, retention, satisfaction), GP utilisation, clinical performance (outcomes, complaints, incidents), staff satisfaction, accreditation status. (2) Identify opportunities: market gaps (unmet patient needs, new services), growth potential (geographic expansion, additional GPs, allied health). (3) Threats: competitor activity, regulatory changes, economic downturn affecting patient base, loss of key staff. (4) Set objectives for next 12 months: e.g., “grow patient base by 10%, expand psychology services, improve patient satisfaction NPS to 60, recruit 1 additional GP, achieve RACGP accreditation”. (5) Assign accountability: which leader owns each objective? (6) Timeline: break annual objective into quarterly milestones with accountable person. (7) Review: quarterly review of progress against plan, adjust if needed. Document plan in written document, distribute to leadership team, discuss at quarterly management meetings.

Referral network management: Cultivate relationships with referral sources (other GPs, specialists, allied health, hospitals) to ensure smooth patient pathways: (1) Identify key referral partners: specialists locally (cardiologists, orthopods, psychiatrists, dermatologists), allied health (physiotherapists, psychologists, dietitians), hospitals (emergency department for acute referrals, specific wards if clinic specialises). (2) Establish referral pathways: ensure specialists accept referrals from clinic, understand referral process (paper form vs electronic e-referral, any specific requirements). (3) Two-way communication: request specialists provide reports back to clinic after patient consultation (closure of referral loop). If reports delayed or missing, follow up with specialist admin. (4) Regular contact: maintain relationships with key referral partners (annual catch-up call, email updates, attendance at local healthcare meetings). (5) Feedback loop: if patients return from specialists with concerns (poor communication, felt rushed, unclear advice), feed back to specialist politely (aim to improve quality of referral pathway). (6) Reciprocal referrals: if allied health or other GPs refer patients to clinic, acknowledge and ensure good patient experience (may generate return referrals). (7) Referral metrics: track referral patterns (which specialists receive most referrals, patient feedback on specialist experience) — use to guide clinic partnerships and service development.
Who Usually Does It
Primary: Clinic Manager / Business Manager (data collection, metrics tracking, strategic planning) with GP Principal/Owner (strategy approval, service expansion decisions)
What Owner Must Review Personally
Patient growth and retention metrics (monthly dashboard review — identify trends, assess growth against targets)
GP utilisation rates (quarterly — ensure sessions adequately filled, investigate underutilisation)
Appointment availability monitoring (weekly — ensure <3 days for acute, take action if exceeding target)
Google Business Profile and online reviews (monthly — monitor reputation, respond to reviews)
Patient satisfaction metrics (NPS, complaints, feedback — quarterly review, identify QI opportunities)
Service expansion opportunities (annually — assess viability of new services)
Annual strategic plan (develop annually, review quarterly, adjust as needed)
GP retention strategy effectiveness (annually — assess staff satisfaction, identify retention risks, approve compensation changes)
Documents / Systems Involved
Patient metrics dashboard (Excel or BI tool: new patients, active patients, retention rate, lost patients)
GP utilisation tracking (sessions scheduled vs filled, billing per session, utilisation rate by GP)
Appointment availability monitoring (weekly report of next available appointment)
Service expansion assessment document (financial viability, regulatory check, staffing plan)
Website (clinic website with appointment booking integration, regularly updated)
Google Business Profile (clinic listing, review monitoring, response log)
HotDoc/HealthEngine listing verification
Patient satisfaction survey results (NPS scores, feedback themes)
Competitor analysis summary (competitive landscape, market positioning)
Annual Strategic Plan (objectives, accountability, timelines, quarterly review notes)
Referral network contact list (key specialists, referral pathways, contact details)
GP retention strategy documentation (billing split, flexible hours policy, CPD budget, recognition practices)
What Can Go Wrong If Missed

Patient growth not monitored: Clinic unaware patient base declining. Months pass before owner realises patient numbers down 15% (revenue impacted). By then, too late to take action. Competitor clinic captured patient base, patients switched during period of inattention. Preventable if monthly metrics reviewed.

GP utilisation poor: GP working part-time due to low patient numbers, utilisation 60%, clinic cannot justify keeping GP. GP may depart or reduce hours. If key GP departs, clinic loses revenue and reputation. If utilisation tracked, clinic would have identified issue early, taken action (marketing, adjusted schedule) to grow patient numbers.

Appointment availability excessive (>1 week wait): Patients frustrated by long wait, choose alternative provider, leave negative reviews, clinic reputation damaged. New patient recruitment suffers (read bad reviews, don’t book). Preventable if appointment availability tracked weekly.

Service expansion not financially assessed: Clinic invests $50,000 in psychology service (fit-out, staff hiring) without validating market demand or financial viability. Psychologist underutilised (insufficient referral volume), service loses $20,000 in first year, clinic forced to wind down service. Capital wasted. Would have been avoided by proper assessment (market research, financial modelling) before expansion.

Online reputation ignored: Clinic has poor Google reviews (3.5-star rating with complaints about wait times, rude staff), clinic does not respond or take action. Prospective patients read reviews, don’t book. Clinic loses new patient inquiries. Online reputation is critical for modern patients — ignoring it costs business.

GP satisfaction not addressed: Senior GP considering retirement, potential to transition retirement gradually (mentoring junior GP, reducing hours). Owner unaware of GP’s plans until suddenly receives resignation notice. Clinic loses experienced GP, patient relationships, revenue. Preventable if GP retention strategy in place and regular conversations about satisfaction.

Referral pathways not maintained: Clinic refers patients to specialist, specialist’s office never sends report back to clinic. Clinic unaware of patient’s outcome, cannot follow up appropriately, gaps in continuity of care. Patient experience poor if clinic cannot discuss specialist findings. Risk: if adverse outcome (specialist missed diagnosis), clinic liable even though didn’t directly provide specialist care (referral without closure of loop is poor practice).
CATEGORY 20 — END-OF-MONTH AND END-OF-YEAR OBLIGATIONS
What Has to Be Done

Month-end billing reconciliation (Medicare claims vs deposits — reconcile within 5 business days of month end): Upon month-end, clinic reconciles Medicare billing with actual bank deposits received. Process: (1) Extract Medicare billing report from practice software for month (all MBS items billed, amount per item, patient names, dates of service). (2) Cross-check against bank deposits: did clinic receive deposits matching billed amounts? (3) Identify discrepancies: unpaid claims (billed but not yet deposited), rejected claims (claim denied by Medicare, reversed), over/under payments. (4) Investigate gaps: if amount billed $50,000 but only $45,000 deposited, where is $5,000? (delayed payment, rejected claim, data entry error?). (5) Follow up with Medicare: contact Services Australia if claim missing or denied (may need resubmission with corrected documentation). (6) Reconciliation deadline: complete within 5 business days of month-end. If delayed, errors compound (harder to track issues retrospectively). Document reconciliation: spreadsheet matching billed vs deposited with resolution of gaps. File reconciliation report in accounting records (audit trail for accountant).

Month-end financial close (P&L draft, expense coding review): Clinic prepares draft Profit & Loss (P&L) statement for month showing: (1) Revenue: total patient billings (Medicare, private, other sources), (2) Expenses: staff salaries, rent, utilities, consumables, service contracts, insurance, depreciation, (3) Gross profit: revenue − expenses, (4) Net profit/loss: gross profit − tax provisions. Review P&L line-by-line to ensure expenses properly coded (rent coded to rent, not office expenses; staff salaries not mixed with consulting fees, etc.). Identify unusual variations: is a particular expense significantly higher than prior months? (may indicate error, theft, supplier price increase, legitimate higher usage). Approve P&L draft with accountant before finalising (accountant may have adjustments for accruals, depreciation, etc.). Retain draft P&L in accounting records.

BAS (Business Activity Statement) preparation (monthly or quarterly depending on turnover — GST collected vs input tax credits): All Australian businesses with turnover >$75,000/year required to lodge BAS to ATO (Australian Taxation Office) — typically quarterly (3-month periods) for most businesses, monthly for higher-turnover or GST-sensitive businesses. Clinic’s BAS reports: (1) GST collected (10% tax on clinic fees charged to patients), (2) GST paid on purchases (clinic can claim credit for GST paid on supplies, rent, services — called input tax credits), (3) Income tax withholding (if clinic had employees, tax withheld from salaries — reported on BAS), (4) Superannuation guarantee contributions. BAS preparation: accountant or bookkeeper prepares BAS (collects data from clinic accounting records, calculates GST position). Clinic reviews BAS before lodgement with accountant (verify amounts correct). Lodgement deadline: 21 days after quarter-end (e.g., for quarter ending 30 June, BAS due by 21 July). Late lodgement triggers ATO penalties. Online lodgement via ATO portal (Australian Business Register — ABR lookup, or tax agent portal if using tax agent).

Superannuation contributions (due 28 days after quarter end — 1 Oct, 28 Jan, 28 Apr, 28 Jul): If clinic has employees, clinic required to contribute superannuation to employees’ accounts. Contribution rate: minimum 11.5% of employee’s ordinary time earnings (as at July 2024 — rate increases annually). Contribution quarterly: employer calculates super owed for quarter, contributes to employee’s nominated super fund. Due dates: 28 days after quarter-end: (1) Quarter 1 (1 Jan–31 Mar): due 28 April, (2) Quarter 2 (1 Apr–30 Jun): due 28 July, (3) Quarter 3 (1 Jul–30 Sep): due 28 October, (4) Quarter 4 (1 Oct–31 Dec): due 28 January. Late contributions: ATO imposes administrative charge ($20) + interest. Super contribution must go to employee’s nominated super fund (employee nominates preferred fund, or employer nominates default fund if employee doesn’t nominate). Process: accountant/bookkeeper calculates super each quarter (total wages × 11.5%), clinic processes payment to super fund via EFT, obtains receipt/confirmation. Document contributions and retain for audit trail.

Payroll tax monthly lodgement (State Revenue Office Victoria — if liable): Victorian employers may be liable for payroll tax if annual payroll exceeds threshold (for 2024: $10.4 million annual payroll). Most GP clinics under $10.4 million payroll, so not liable. If clinic grows significantly (many GPs, large staff), may become liable for payroll tax. Payroll tax is charged to employer (not deducted from employee wages) at rate 4.85% of payroll above threshold. Lodgement: monthly to Victorian State Revenue Office (via online portal). If liable, accountant manages lodgement and payment.

Year-end payroll (EOFY 30 June — finalise STP, issue income statements via myGov): Australian Financial Year ends 30 June. Year-end payroll obligations: (1) STP (Single Touch Payroll) finalisation: all employers with >10 employees (or >$2 million payroll) required to use STP. STP is real-time payroll reporting to ATO (each pay day, employer lodges employee’s gross pay, tax withheld, super, HELP debt, etc. to ATO via payroll software). At EOFY, clinic must finalise STP (reconcile amounts withheld vs final tax liability, submit final STP lodge for financial year). (2) Income statements: by 30 June, employer must provide each employee with income statement (statement of income, tax withheld, super contributions, deductions). Employees use income statements to complete tax returns. Clinics typically provide income statements via myGov (ATO’s online platform — employees access via their MyTax account) or printed statements. (3) Tax agent: if clinic uses tax agent, agent assists with finalising payroll and preparing tax return (see next item).

Contractor annual payment summaries (if clinic engages contractors): If clinic engages independent contractors (e.g., locum GPs who are self-employed, cleaning contractors, IT contractors), clinic may be required to issue payment summary if contractor earned >$450 from clinic in financial year. Payment summary specifies: contractor’s ABN (Australian Business Number), amount paid, date paid, tax withheld (if any). Lodgement: to ATO by 14 July following financial year-end. Contractor uses payment summary for tax purposes.

Tax return preparation (with accountant — typically October lodgement for tax agents): Clinic’s financial year ends 30 June. Tax return (income tax return) due to ATO: (1) If clinic lodges own return: due 31 October (5 months after EOFY). (2) If tax agent lodges return: due 15 May following year (extended deadline — 10 months after EOFY). Most clinics use tax agent (accountant-prepared return). Accountant prepares return showing: clinic income (Medicare, private billings, other), deductible expenses (staff, rent, utilities, depreciation, insurance, professional fees), depreciation (equipment, fit-out), capital gains/losses if applicable. Return calculates clinic’s taxable income and tax liability. Clinic reviews return with accountant before lodgement, approves, and signs return. ATO may audit clinic’s return (especially if large deductions or inconsistencies) — retain all supporting documentation (invoices, receipts, contracts) for 5+ years.

WorkCover premium renewal (annual): WorkCover insurance premium calculated and due annually, usually around June-July (varies by policy anniversary date). Premium is based on: clinic’s payroll for prior financial year (reported to WorkCover at EOFY) and industry classification code (6211 for medical practitioners). ATO sends WorkCover premium bill, clinic pays premium before due date (usually within 30 days). Premium varies year-to-year based on: payroll size (more staff = higher premium), claims history (claims increase future premiums), industry risk rating. Clinic may receive refund or be owed money if payroll overestimated or underestimated. Review premium bill with accountant, ensure correct payroll reported, challenge if premium seems excessive (compare to prior years, benchmark against similar clinics).

PIP annual attestation (via HPOS): PIP (Practice Incentives Program) is Australian Government program providing incentive payments to general practices meeting quality and compliance standards. Clinic receiving PIP payments required to attest annually to compliance with PIP requirements. Attestation completed via HPOS (Health Professional Online Services): clinic (usually Practice Manager or GP principal) logs in, confirms compliance with PIP rules (patient enrolments, after-hours care, chronic disease management, cervical screening rates, GP immunisation status per guidelines, privacy/confidentiality, etc.). Attestation deadline: typically 31 October for prior financial year. If clinic not attesting by deadline, PIP payments may be suspended. Accountant usually reminds clinic of attestation deadline.

RACGP membership renewals: GPs maintaining RACGP (Royal Australian College of General Practitioners) membership pay annual membership fee (approximately $1,500–$2,000 depending on membership category). Renewal typically due each July. GPs responsible for own renewal, but clinic manager should track GP renewals (ensure GPs maintain membership, required for accreditation). Invoice reminder sent by RACGP. If GP membership lapses, clinic may lose accreditation or eligibility for certain incentive programs.

Asset register update: Clinic maintains asset register (list of capital items: buildings, equipment, vehicles, computers, fit-out). At year-end, update register with: (1) New assets purchased in financial year, (2) Assets disposed/sold (remove from register), (3) Depreciation recorded (accountant calculates annual depreciation for tax and accounting purposes). Asset register used for: tax depreciation (deductible each year), financial reporting (balance sheet shows net assets), insurance (contents insurance premium based on asset value). Review asset register annually with accountant, ensure completeness and accuracy.

Budget preparation for new financial year: As financial year-end approaches (June), clinic begins budgeting for next financial year (1 July–30 June). Budget process: (1) Review prior year actual performance (revenue, expenses, profit), (2) Project next year: expected patient volume growth, revenue increase, expected wage/salary increases (e.g., award increases), expected expense changes (utilities, rent increases), capex plans (equipment purchases, fit-out). (3) Set targets: revenue target (how much billing to achieve?), expense budget (staff, rent, supplies, consumables), profit margin target (desired profit as % of revenue). (4) Identify risks/opportunities: are there planned GP departures? (revenue loss). Expected new GP recruitment? (expense increase then revenue increase). New services to launch? (startup costs, new revenue stream). (5) Approve budget: management team (GP principal, clinic manager, accountant) approves budget for new financial year. (6) Use budget for monitoring: each month, actual results compared to budget (variance analysis — is clinic on track?), identify issues early, adjust operations if needed.
Who Usually Does It

Primary: Accountant / Bookkeeper (BAS, tax return, payroll, super, asset register) with Business Manager / Clinic Manager (month-end reconciliation, PIP attestation, budget preparation) | Backup: GP Principal/Owner (approval of financial statements, budget, tax return)
What Owner Must Review Personally
Month-end P&L statement (review within 5 days of month-end — identify unusual variances, approve before finalising)
Quarterly BAS before lodgement (verify GST position, income withholding accurate)
Annual tax return with accountant (review all income and deductions, approve and sign before lodgement)
Annual super contributions (verify all employees received correct contributions by due dates — spot check 3–5 employees)
WorkCover premium (review annual bill, ensure correct payroll reported, challenge if excessive)
PIP annual attestation (confirm compliance with PIP requirements, authorise HPOS attestation)
Year-end asset register (verify major assets captured, depreciation calculation reasonable)
Annual budget (develop collaboratively with accountant and management, approve before 1 July)
Documents / Systems Involved
Month-end billing reconciliation spreadsheet (Medicare claims vs bank deposits, discrepancies identified and resolved)
Draft P&L statement (monthly, reviewed with accountant)
BAS lodgement (quarterly — copy of BAS submission, ATO receipts)
Superannuation contribution records (quarterly statements from super funds, contribution confirmation receipts)
Payroll tax compliance (if liable — monthly lodgement receipts)
STP (Single Touch Payroll) — integration with payroll software, monthly STP files submitted to ATO, EOFY finalisation
Employee income statements (issued by 30 June, copies retained)
Contractor payment summaries (if applicable)
Tax agent agreement and tax return (prepared by accountant, signed by clinic owner, ATO lodgement confirmation)
WorkCover premium bill and payment confirmation
PIP attestation (HPOS record of annual attestation)
RACGP membership tracking (reminders for GP renewals)
Asset register (spreadsheet: item, purchase date, cost, depreciation, net book value)
Annual budget (document prepared at EOFY, approved by management, used for monthly variance analysis)
Monthly variance reports (actual vs budget, variance analysis and commentary)
What Can Go Wrong If Missed

Billing reconciliation not completed: Clinic bills $50,000 but only receives $45,000 deposit (month-end). If reconciliation not done, clinic doesn’t identify $5,000 shortfall. Months pass, shortfall compounded (multiple months of unreconciled billings). Accountant cannot complete accurate financial statements (revenue wrong). Tax return filed with incorrect revenue. ATO audit discovers revenue underreported, penalties assessed. Preventable if reconciliation completed monthly.

BAS lodged late (>21 days after quarter-end): ATO assesses penalty ($20–$100+). If pattern of late lodgement, ATO may pursue clinic for penalties and interest. Also affects cashflow: GST refunds (if clinic overpaid GST) delayed if BAS late.

Super contributions not made by due date (28 days after quarter-end): ATO imposes administrative charge ($20) + interest on late contributions. Employees dissatisfied (their super account not credited on time — impacts their retirement savings). If repeated non-payment, ATO may prosecute employer for failing statutory super obligation (criminal penalties possible if egregious).

Tax return not filed by deadline: If clinic lodges own return (due 31 Oct), failure to lodge triggers penalties. If tax agent lodges (due 15 May), extended deadline more forgiving. However, if clinic owes tax and doesn’t file, ATO pursues for unpaid tax + penalties + interest (debt accumulates).

Asset register not updated: Clinic purchases $30,000 equipment (autoclave), not recorded in asset register. Tax depreciation not claimed (clinic entitled to deduction, not taken). Tax return filed without depreciation deduction. Clinic pays more tax than necessary (thousands in lost deductions over time). Also, for insurance purposes, if equipment not listed, may not be covered if equipment damaged/stolen (claim denied due to uninsured asset).

Year-end payroll not finalised (STP not reconciled, income statements not issued): Employees cannot complete tax returns (don’t have income statement showing tax withheld). Employee tax return delayed. If discrepancy between STP lodged and actual tax withheld, tax return may be incorrect. ATO audit employees’ returns, discovers mismatch, penalties assessed (to employee, but clinic liable if employer error).

Budget not prepared for new financial year: Clinic starts new financial year with no targets or projections. During year, expenses not controlled (spending ad-hoc, no approval process). By end of year, clinic discovers loss or reduced profit due to unchecked spending. Preventable with budget (spending authorised against budget, variances investigated).
SUMMARY — PART 3B CATEGORIES 11–20
Categories 11–20 cover clinic’s operational, legal, and financial compliance backbone. Together they ensure:
Workplace safety (WHS, incidents, injury management)
Patient privacy (records management, privacy compliance, access rights)
Facility fitness (building maintenance, utilities, security)
Risk management (insurance coverage, complaint resolution, incident response)
Supplier management (pathology, consumables, vaccines)
Staff management (rostering, recruitment, retention, onboarding/offboarding)
Business growth (patient metrics, service expansion, competitive positioning)
Financial compliance (billing reconciliation, GST, super, tax, payroll)

Owner’s critical responsibility: personally review items flagged for owner review in each category. Delegate day-to-day execution to capable clinic manager and accountant, but maintain oversight and final approval authority on all significant decisions (insurance policies, GP hiring, budget, tax return, complaint responses, WHS incidents).
Quarterly management meetings should systematically review progress across all 20 categories — establish governance rhythm to prevent items slipping through cracks.
END OF PART 3B — CATEGORIES 11–20

rows: Part 4: Recurring Task Calendar — Australian GP Clinic Operator’s Manual

text: END OF PART 4

Note: This document provides a comprehensive recurring task calendar and event-based checklist framework for Australian GP clinic operations. All timeframes, regulatory deadlines, and processes are current as of April 2026 and specific to Victorian and Australian regulatory frameworks. Regular review and updates are recommended to align with any changes in legislation, regulatory requirements, or clinic-specific procedures. Systems, portals, and contact details referenced (e.g., Medicare, AHPRA, DHHS Victoria, Services Australia) should be verified annually for accuracy.
GP CLINIC OPERATOR’S MANUAL — PARTS 5–8

Dream Clinic Acquisition, Victoria
Built from expert knowledge | All items marked for verification
Date: April 2026
PART 5: MONEY FLOW MAP
Overview

Every dollar that enters a GP practice comes from predictable sources and must flow through documented channels. This section maps WHO pays WHAT, WHEN, HOW, and WHAT reconciles to WHAT. Operator responsibility: weekly cash review against these flows.
5.1 PATIENT BILLING & MEDICARE CLAIMING
Bulk-Billed Claims (No Gap to Patient)

Standard Consultation Item Numbers: - Item 23 – Standard GP consultation, < 10 mins - Item 36 – Standard GP consultation, 10–20 mins (most common) - Item 44 – Extended GP consultation, > 20 mins - Item 47 – Home visit (time-based variants exist)

How money flows: 1. Receptionist registers patient, records item number (based on GP time in room) 2. GP provides service 3. Claim submitted to Medicare (via Best Practice/MD system or direct batch) 4. Medicare pays practice directly (100% of MBS fee) 5. Patient pays nothing

MBS rebate rates (April 2026):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed (exact rates update quarterly) - Item 23: ~AUD $45 (example) - Item 36: ~AUD $60 (example) - Item 44: ~AUD $90 (example)
Cash timing: - Claims submitted daily or batched - Medicare ECLIPSE payment cycle: 5–7 business days typically - Bank receipt: 7–10 days after submission (processor delay)

Operator control points: - Weekly: reconcile items billed vs items claimed (match count/value) - Monthly: Medicare bank statement vs claims register (gap = claim failure or delay) - Red flags: - Unbilled services (patient seen, no claim recorded) - Item code errors (e.g., Item 23 claiming when Item 36 was appropriate) - Rejected claims (submitted but denied by Medicare – causes 2–3 week investigation)
Medicare Claims – ECLIPSE & PRODA

System overview: - ECLIPSE: Medicare’s claims processing system; practices submit via approved software (Best Practice, MD, Hotdoc, Pracsoft, etc.) - PRODA: Practice Registration and Disclosures Authority – practice delegates user accounts; tracks who can submit claims

Claim submission process: 1. Practice staff log into PRODA portal, confirm delegations to practice manager + bookkeeper 2. Practice staff log into ECLIPSE via software (Best Practice, etc.) 3. Claims submitted (batch daily, or real-time in modern systems) 4. Medicare responds with acceptance/rejection within 24 hours (usually) 5. Accepted claims queued for payment
Payment deposit: - Typically into business bank account designated for Medicare - Often separate account to practice operational account (for reconciliation)

Operator responsibility: - Confirm PRODA delegations annually (who has submit rights? who can receive payment details?) - Reconcile ECLIPSE reports weekly (what was sent vs what was accepted) - Investigate rejected claims immediately (often due to missing/incorrect provider number, item code mismatch, or patient non-eligibility) - Ensure software integrates with bank feeds (so Medicare deposits auto-reconcile)
Mixed Billing (Bulk-Bill + Private Gap)

How it works: - Patient bulk-billed for consultation (e.g., Item 36 = AUD $60 Medicare rebate) - GP also charges private gap fee (e.g., AUD $40 additional) - Patient pays gap; practice receives Medicare + gap
Cash timing: - Medicare: 5–10 days (as above) - Gap fee: usually same-day (EFTPOS at reception) or invoiced (30 days)

Operator control points: - Daily: EFTPOS reconciliation (gap fees collected vs EFTPOS receipts) - Weekly: gap fee invoices sent and follow-up on unpaid - Monthly: gap fee bank deposits vs billing register - Red flags: - Gap fee charged but not recorded in billing system - Gap fees collected but not deposited - Patient claims “wasn’t told about gap” (staff training issue)
Private Billing (No Medicare Claim)
When used: - Patient not eligible for Medicare (e.g., overseas visitor, incorrect Medicare number) - Patient choice (may occur for cosmetic/non-core services) - Some allied health

Typical fees (Victorian practice benchmark):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Standard consultation: AUD $120–$180 - Extended: AUD $180–$250 - Home visit: AUD $200–$350 + travel
Cash timing: - Usually EFTPOS same-day - May be invoiced (30 days) – higher default risk

Operator control points: - At registration: confirm patient Medicare status; if not eligible, inform of private fee - Daily: EFTPOS reconciliation - Monthly: invoice tracking and debt collection follow-up
Chronic Disease Management (CDM) Item Numbers
Items: - Item 721: Initial health assessment GP (>45 mins, plan created) - Item 723: Follow-up CDM GP visit - Item 732: Nurse-led review
Value: Higher than standard consultation; often AUD $100+ per item
Eligibility: Patient must have chronic condition, documented plan, usually referred by GP
How they differ: - Requires detailed documentation (plan must be in record) - Often bundled with specialist referral - Can be bulk-billed or private (if claimed, must be correct item number)

Operator control points: - At claim time: verify plan is documented (auditors will check) - Monthly: CDM claims vs plans created (should be 1:1 or close match) - Red flag: claiming CDM without documented plan = compliance risk
Mental Health Items (Session-Based)
Items: - Item 2700: Initial assessment (up to 60 mins, AUD ~$120+) - Item 2701: Follow-up (up to 30 mins, AUD ~$90+) - Item 2702: Group (AUD ~$65+)
Key difference: Often referred to allied health (psychologist, social worker), not just GP

Operator control points: - Referral tracking: patient referred, session attended, claim lodged - Item coding: practitioners must be qualified (e.g., psychologist, counsellor); GP-referred mental health items have specific requirements - Red flag: mental health claim without documented referral or session record
Health Assessment Items (71-Year-Olds, etc.)
Items: - Item 701–707: GP health assessment (varies by age/context; AUD ~$100+)
Key requirement: Specific assessment form completed, Medicare eligible

Operator control points: - Recall: practice should proactively contact eligible patients - Documentation: assessment form filed in record before claiming - Timing: typically annual opportunity per patient
5.2 PRIVATE FEES & THIRD-PARTY BILLING
WorkCover (Victorian Workplace Injury)
How it works: - Employee injured at work → referred to WorkCover-approved provider - Provider claims directly to WorkCover insurer (or patient bills WorkCover) - No gap to patient
Typical item value: AUD $100–$150 per consultation
Cash timing: - WorkCover insurer pays practice directly (or patient reimbursed) - Often slower than Medicare (2–3 weeks)

Operator control points: - At registration: confirm WorkCover claim number and insurer details - Claim submission: use correct WorkCover item codes (not Medicare) - Monthly reconciliation: WorkCover claims lodged vs payments received
Red flags: - Claiming Medicare AND WorkCover for same visit (double-dipping) - Patient treated but claim not lodged (patient waits for reimbursement)
DVA (Department of Veterans’ Affairs)
Eligibility: War widow/er, veteran, or dependent on DVA gold/white card
How it works: - Similar to Medicare bulk-billing - DVA pays practice directly - Some DVA patients bulk-billed; others private with gap

Operator control points: - At registration: confirm DVA card type (affects billing) - Claim submission: use DVA provider number + DVA batch process (separate from Medicare) - Monthly: DVA payments vs claims
TAC (Transport Accident Commission – Victoria)
Eligibility: Person injured in motor vehicle accident covered by TAC
How it works: - TAC claim number issued - Provider claims TAC directly - TAC pays practice
Operator control points: - Registration: confirm TAC claim number - Item coding: use TAC-appropriate item numbers - Monthly reconciliation: claims vs payments
Third-Party Private Insurance
Common: Overseas visitor insurance, accident insurance, etc.
How it works: - Patient pays upfront; claims reimbursement from insurer - OR practice claims insurer directly (requires pre-approval)
Operator control points: - Policy check: confirm insurance active and covers GP services - Payment terms: typically 30 days after submission - Red flags: patient uninsured or insurance lapsed
5.3 MERCHANT SETTLEMENT & EFTPOS
Daily EFTPOS Reconciliation

Process: 1. Each EFTPOS terminal (Tyro, Square, Commonwealth Bank, etc.) processes transactions throughout day 2. End-of-day batch settled 3. Funds deposited to nominated bank account (typically 1–2 business days)

Operator control points: - Daily: print EFTPOS settlement reports; reconcile to cash register/POS system - Discrepancies: gaps between POS and EFTPOS (indicate cash register errors, stolen transactions, or system failures) - Weekly: EFTPOS bank deposit vs daily settlements (chase delays)
Typical merchant fee: 1.5–2.5% of transaction value (varies by provider)
Reconciliation tools: - Bank feeds (software integrations with Best Practice, etc.) auto-import EFTPOS deposits - Manual monthly reconciliation still recommended
5.4 CONTRACTOR GP PERCENTAGE PAYMENTS
Typical Arrangement
Structure: - GP is contractor (not employee) - GP receives percentage of billings or fixed service fee - Practice retains remainder
Common split: - GP receives: 65–70% of billings - Practice retains: 30–35% (covers rent, staff, utilities, admin)
Example: - Item 36 bulk-billed: AUD $60 Medicare receipt - GP receives: AUD $42 (70%) - Practice retains: AUD $18 (30%)
Payment frequency: Weekly, fortnightly, or monthly (varies by agreement)

Operator control points: - At hire: confirm percentage split in signed contract - Weekly: run GP billings report (Best Practice > Reports > GP Billings) - Reconcile: items claimed this week vs GP payment due - Monthly: GP payment invoice vs total billings - Red flags: - GP payment doesn’t match billings × percentage (indicates missing claims or data-entry error) - GP claims underpaid (check contract and confirm no disputed claims)
Service Fee Alternative
Structure: - GP paid flat fee (e.g., AUD $2,500/week) + percentage of excess billings - Practice retains first $X in billings, then splits excess 50/50 (example)
Reconciliation: same as above but with fixed fee component factored in
5.5 EMPLOYEE GP WAGES
Employment vs Contracting
Employment (salaried GP): - PAYG tax withheld - Superannuation paid (11.5% from 1 July 2025) - Workers compensation insurance required - Entitled to leave
Contracting: - No tax withheld (GP pays own tax) - No superannuation obligation - No workers comp (contractor covers own insurance) - No leave entitlements
Operator responsibility: - Confirm structure with accountant - Ensure correct tax treatment (ATO scrutiny on sham contracting in GP sector – see Prince Developments case law)
5.6 NURSE WAGES
Award Coverage
Applicable award: Nurses Award 2020 (Fair Work Ombudsman)
Classification: - RN (Registered Nurse, Level 1-6) - EN (Enrolled Nurse, Level 1-4) - Immunisation Nurse (often RN but specialist rate may apply)

Minimum rates (April 2026):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed – check Fair Work Ombudsman - RN Level 1: ~AUD $65,000–$70,000 pa (example) - EN Level 1: ~AUD $55,000–$60,000 pa (example) - Penalty rates apply for evenings, weekends, public holidays

Operator control points: - At hire: confirm AHPRA registration (RN/EN number) - Payroll check: wage rate matches Award (run quarterly benchmarking) - Timesheets: record hours worked; ensure no unpaid overtime - Superannuation: 11.5% mandatory contribution - Red flags: - RN paid below Award rate - AHPRA registration lapsed (nurse cannot legally practice) - Unrecorded casual shifts
5.7 RECEPTIONIST & ADMIN WAGES
Award Coverage
Applicable award: Health Professionals Award 2020 OR Clerks Award 2020 (varies by role classification)

Typical rates (April 2026):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Receptionist Level 1: ~AUD $48,000–$52,000 pa - Senior receptionist/admin Level 2–3: ~AUD $55,000–$65,000 pa

Operator control points: - Classification: confirm role classification matches Award level - Payroll: weekly reconciliation against timesheets - Superannuation: 11.5% - Red flags: - Receptionist paid below Award - Casual staff denied rostered hours (informal flexible arrangement = risk)
5.8 PRACTICE MANAGER WAGES
Typical structure: Salaried employee or contractor

Typical rates (Victorian practice):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Practice manager: AUD $65,000–$85,000 pa (varies by clinic size/complexity) - Senior manager: AUD $85,000–$110,000 pa
Operator control points: - Same as nurses/admin (Award compliance, superannuation, timesheets) - Ensure practice manager has delegated authority (defined in job description)
5.9 SUPERANNUATION
Employer Contribution Rate
Current (1 July 2025 onwards): 11.5%
Applies to: - All employees (full-time, part-time, casual) - Contractor GPs: NO contribution required
Calculation: - Employee annual salary AUD $65,000 × 11.5% = AUD $7,475/year = ~AUD $620/month

Operator control points: - Monthly: payroll system auto-calculates and deposits to employee SuperFunds - Reconciliation: payroll summary should show total super paid vs total salary - Annual: obtain ATO SuperFund payment receipts (reconcile to payroll records) - Red flags: - Super not paid (breach of superannuation guarantee) - Underpayment (common error: wrong percentage or salary base)
5.10 PAYROLL TAX (VICTORIA)
Threshold & Obligation
Payroll tax applies if: - Victorian practice payroll > ~AUD $900,000/year (2026 threshold)
Rate: ~4.85% of payroll above threshold

Operator control points: - Quarterly: calculate whether threshold exceeded - If threshold exceeded: register with State Revenue Office Victoria; lodge quarterly returns - Red flags: - Payroll crossing threshold but not registered (penalty interest accrues) - Contractor arrangements used to avoid payroll tax (ATO scrutiny)
5.11 RENT & PREMISES COSTS
Lease Structure

Common arrangements: 1. Fixed rent: AUD $3,000–$8,000/month (varies by location, practice size) 2. Percentage of turnover: 5–8% of gross revenue (less common in VIC) 3. Hybrid: fixed base + percentage of excess

Operator control points: - At lease signing: confirm lease term, renewal rights, break clauses, make-good obligations - Monthly: rent invoice matches lease terms (value, payment date) - Annual: review rent increases (usually CPI-linked) - Red flags: - Rent paid but not invoiced (indicates informal arrangement = tax/legal risk) - Make-good costs at lease end not budgeted
Landlord Outgoings
Typically included in rent or charged separately: - Building insurance - Council rates - Common area maintenance - Security/cleaning of common areas

Operator control points: - Monthly/quarterly: outgoings invoice reconciled to lease - Red flags: - Outgoings increasing unexpectedly (may indicate building maintenance) - No breakdown of outgoings (request itemised statement)
5.12 UTILITIES
Electricity, Gas, Water

Typical monthly cost (Victorian practice):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Electricity: AUD $500–$1,500/month (depends on clinical use, air conditioning) - Gas: AUD $150–$400/month - Water: AUD $100–$250/month

Operator control points: - Monthly: utility bills reconciled to budget - Quarterly: usage trend review (spike = leak/equipment fault) - Red flags: - No meter readings (estimates may be incorrect) - Sudden cost increase (investigate usage/rate change)
5.13 SOFTWARE SUBSCRIPTIONS
Common Clinic Management Systems
Best Practice (IBIS): - One-time license + annual subscription - Cost: AUD $3,000–$8,000/year (varies by practice size, modules) - Includes: patient records, billing, reporting
MD (Medical Director): - Subscription model - Cost: AUD $2,500–$7,000/year - Includes: patient records, billing, telehealth
Hotdoc (by Healthtech): - Subscription - Cost: AUD $2,000–$5,000/year - Includes: template library, document management
Pracsoft: - Subscription - Cost: AUD $3,000–$6,000/year

Other tools: - HealthEngine (patient portal): AUD $500–$2,000/year - Tyro (EFTPOS + integrations): merchant fee (see 5.3) - Zoom/Microsoft Teams: minimal or included - Practice website hosting: AUD $100–$300/year

Operator control points: - Annual: audit all subscriptions; cancel unused ones - Monthly: verify subscriptions are invoiced correctly - Red flags: - Duplicate subscriptions (e.g., two practice management systems) - Unused module subscriptions
5.14 CONSUMABLES
Medical/Clinical Consumables
Categories: - Gloves, swabs, gauze, dressings - Needles, syringes, blood collection tubes - Swabs for pathology - Vaccines (if on-site storage) - Creams, lotions, medications (for in-room use)

Typical monthly spend:
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Small practice (2–3 GPs): AUD $1,000–$2,000/month - Medium practice (4–6 GPs): AUD $2,500–$5,000/month
Suppliers: - Medical supply wholesalers (Medismart, McKesson, etc.) - Pathology collection kits (supplier-provided, often free or included in collection fee)

Operator control points: - Ordering: establish approved supplier list; negotiate volume discounts - Receipt: stock check against invoice (ensure delivery complete) - Usage: track expiry dates; rotate stock (FIFO) - Budget: monthly spend vs prior year (flags waste or usage changes)
5.15 PATHOLOGY ARRANGEMENT
Typical Model (Victorian Practice)

Arrangement types: 1. Lease/Licence: Practice provides space to pathology lab (e.g., Pathology Australia, collection centre); lab pays rent 2. Service fee: Pathology company pays practice commission per collection or per patient 3. Revenue share: Practice shares in test revenue (less common) 4. No arrangement: Collections done on-site; pathology company picks up and pays nothing
Typical pathology companies: - Pathology Australia - Australian Pathology - Local diagnostic providers - Hospital-based services

Operator control points: - At agreement: confirm terms (rent paid to practice? commission structure? equipment provision?) - Monthly: if revenue-share or commission, reconcile collections vs payment - Red flags: - Collection kits not restocked (supplier responsibility) - Pathology company not remitting agreed rent/commission
5.16 INSURANCE PREMIUMS
Professional Indemnity Insurance (PII)
Required for: GPs, nurses (often grouped with clinic)

Typical cost (Victoria):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Solo GP or small clinic: AUD $3,000–$6,000/year - Larger clinic: AUD $6,000–$15,000/year
Operator control points: - Annually: review policy renewal (confirm coverage limits adequate, no gaps) - At hire: ensure every practitioner has PII (contractor GPs must provide proof)
General Liability & Management Liability Insurance
Covers: slip/fall, injury on premises, employment claims, etc.
Typical cost: AUD $2,000–$5,000/year
Operator control points: - Annual review; ensure landlord/mortgagee is notified insurer
WorkCover Insurance (Victoria)
Required if: clinic has employees
Premium calculation: based on payroll + risk class (medical practice = low-moderate risk)
Typical cost: 0.5–1.5% of payroll
Operator control points: - Ensure premiums paid on time (late payment = cover suspended) - Notify insurer of new staff
5.17 ACCOUNTING & BOOKKEEPING
Typical Engagement
Bookkeeper: - Processes payroll weekly/fortnightly - Reconciles bank accounts monthly - Lodges GST/BAS quarterly - Maintains general ledger
Accountant: - Reviews financial statements monthly - Prepares tax return (annual) - Advises on structure, tax planning - Audits (if required by lenders)

Typical cost (Victoria):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Bookkeeping: AUD $300–$600/month - Accountant: AUD $200–$400/month + annual fee (AUD $2,000–$5,000)
Operator control points: - Monthly: review financial summary (profit/loss, cash position) - Quarterly: BAS reconciliation (GST paid vs claimed input tax) - Annual: tax return and financial statements
5.18 LOAN REPAYMENTS
Acquisition Financing
Common structure: - Bank loan for practice purchase + fitout - Term: 5–10 years - Interest rate: bank variable or fixed (RBA-linked) - Repayment: monthly or quarterly

Operator control points: - Monthly: verify loan repayment deducted from bank account - Quarterly: reconcile loan balance statement vs general ledger - Annual: confirm no missed payments (affect credit rating/covenant)
5.19 WEEKLY OPERATOR CASH REVIEW CHECKLIST
Every Monday morning (or start of week):
Bank balance: check main account (should match last Friday close)
Medicare deposits: verify amount and count (should align with billings last week)
EFTPOS deposits: verify amounts and dates
Payroll cleared: confirm wages/super paid (should reflect timesheets)
Rent paid: confirm monthly rent cleared
Outstanding invoices: check any major suppliers not yet paid
Cash register reconciliation: (if using cash) daily float vs receipts
Aged debtors: identify invoices unpaid >30 days (private fees, gap fees)
Pathology payments: if applicable, confirm commission/rent received

Red flags for investigation: - Missing deposits (claim not processed?) - Delayed EFTPOS (fraud risk?) - Payroll overdue (compliance issue) - Large EFTPOS fees (renegotiate merchant terms?) - Unpaid invoice from supplier (budget impact)
5.20 MONTHLY CASH RECONCILIATION
End of each month:
Bank reconciliation: all transactions matched to bank statement
Clinic revenue summary:
Total Medicare claims lodged
Total Medicare received (should lag by ~1 week)
Total private/gap fees invoiced
Total private/gap fees received
Total EFTPOS deposits
Contractor GP payment: calculate due amount and verify paid
Payroll register: total wages + super vs bank payments
Supplier invoices: all material invoices logged and categorised
Loan/rent: verify payments cleared
Accounts receivable aging: identify any debtors >60 days
Profit & loss statement: compare to budget (variance analysis)
Documentation retained: bank statements, reconciliation schedule, copies of major invoices
5.21 QUARTERLY CASH REVIEW
Every quarter (end of Mar, Jun, Sep, Dec):
Tax obligation: BAS/GST due? Confirm amount and lodgement
Financial statement: compare to budget (revenue, expenses, cash)
Loan covenant compliance: confirm with accountant (if required)
Superannuation reconciliation: verify all employer contributions made
Payroll tax: if over threshold, confirm quarterly return lodged
Budget review: adjust forecast if revenue/costs trending differently
END PART 5

text: 6.2 IMMEDIATE PRIORITY: MISSING DOCUMENTS REQUEST LIST
Send to seller/previous owner IMMEDIATELY:

CRITICAL (within 2 weeks): 1. Lease agreement + all landlord correspondence 2. All GP/nurse/staff contracts (employment & contractor) 3. All insurance policies (PII, general liability, WorkCover) 4. PRODA registration & delegations list 5. All software licences (Best Practice, HealthEngine, etc.) 6. Bank account details & loan agreements (if any) 7. Accreditation certificate (AGPAL or QPA) 8. My Health Record registration documents 9. Pathology agreement (if applicable) 10. IT support agreement

IMPORTANT (within 4 weeks): 11. Payroll tax registration (if over threshold) 12. GST registration 13. All utility contracts (electricity, gas, internet, phone) 14. Waste disposal agreement 15. Cleaning/maintenance contracts 16. Merchant agreement (EFTPOS/Tyro) 17. All HR policies (privacy, security, WHS, infection control, vaccination, code of conduct, complaints) 18. Accountant & bookkeeper engagement letters 19. Equipment leases (if any) 20. Sublease agreements (pathology, allied health)

OPTIONAL (within 8 weeks): 21. Dated credentialing files for all staff 22. Last 3 years of financial statements 23. Last 3 years of BAS/tax returns 24. Staff training records (CPR, infection control, privacy) 25. Incident/complaint logs (previous 2 years)
6.3 HIGH-RISK CONTRACT REVIEW LIST
Recommend legal review BEFORE signing or within 30 days of acquisition:
Lease agreement
Risk: Make-good clause could be $30K–$50K+ at end of term
Review: Break clauses, renewal rights, landlord’s maintenance obligations, assigned vs sublicensed
Advice: Negotiate landlord consent to sublease (pathology, allied health)
Contractor GP agreement
Risk: Sham contracting classification; ATO audit
Review: Independent contractor declaration, tax withholding, superannuation, restraint of trade
Advice: Use RACGP template; ensure contractor has own ABN, insurance, tax file number
Employee contracts (GPs, nurses, staff)
Risk: Wrongful dismissal claims; wage disputes
Review: Award compliance, notice periods, termination clauses, confidentiality, restraint
Advice: Ensure modern awards applied; templates updated post-2020
Loan agreement
Risk: Covenant breach; personal guarantee
Review: Interest rate, repayment schedule, covenants (e.g., debt-to-equity ratio), early repayment penalties
Advice: Negotiate removal of personal guarantee if possible
Software licences (practice management system)
Risk: Vendor lock-in; data portability unclear
Review: License term, data export rights, termination provisions, ongoing support
Advice: Negotiate data migration rights; ensure backup access
Pathology agreement
Risk: Unclear rent/revenue split; unplanned termination
Review: Who owns collection kits? Who manages consumables? Notice to terminate?
Advice: Document in writing; clarify revenue/rent reconciliation process
Insurance policies
Risk: Underinsurance; exclusions not understood
Review: Coverage limits, deductibles, exclusions, notice of cancellation terms
Advice: Ensure PII covers all practitioners; management liability covers employment claims
6.4 DOCUMENTS REQUIRING LEGAL REVIEW BEFORE EXECUTION
Do NOT sign without legal advice:
Lease (if modified or first time reviewing)
Loan agreement (if modified or refinancing)
Contractor GP agreement (all new hires)
Pathology agreement (if new or modified terms)
Buy-sell/succession agreement (high value; multi-owner)
Privacy policy / Security policy (compliance-critical)
Confidentiality/NDA agreements
Estimated cost: AUD $200–$500 per document (depending on complexity)

Recommended practitioners: - Law firm specialising in healthcare/medical practice (e.g., Lander & Rogers, HopgoodGanim, Herbert Geer) - Alternatively: General commercial lawyer with healthcare sector experience
