PART 7: STAFF, HR, PAYROLL & EMPLOYMENT CONTROL
Overview

A GP practice’s reputation, compliance, and financial performance depend entirely on staff. This section covers ALL employment control points from hiring through offboarding, with emphasis on Victorian/Australian legal obligations.
7.1 CONTRACTOR GP VS EMPLOYEE GP: THE SHAM CONTRACTING RISK
Legal Framework

Contractor classification (at risk for ATO challenge): - Independent contractor GP (works for themselves, provides service to clinic) - Receives percentage of billings or flat fee - No tax withheld by clinic - No superannuation - No leave entitlements

Sham contracting red flags (ATO guidance & Prince Developments Pty Ltd v K Hazel 2021): - Clinic controls how, where, when services provided - Clinic controls scheduling (roster system) - Clinic dictates which patients GP sees - Clinic supplies all equipment/software - GP not free to refuse work - GP cannot engage substitute - High degree of supervision - Clinic controls departure terms

ATO approach (2021 onwards): - Increased scrutiny of medical practice contractor arrangements - Prince Developments case established: doctor working clinic hours under clinic direction = likely employee (PAYG tax + super required) - Non-compliance penalty: back tax + super + interest + 75% penalty
Determining Status: Employee vs Contractor

Contractor MUST have: 1. Own ABN registered with ATO 2. Own tax file number (separate from clinic) 3. Own professional indemnity insurance (separate from clinic PII) 4. Written independent contractor agreement (RACGP template recommended) 5. Freedom to refuse work or request time off 6. Freedom to work elsewhere simultaneously 7. Own equipment/software (or clear lease arrangement) 8. Own business entity (sole trader or company) 9. Own superannuation fund 10. Invoice clinic for services (not placed on payroll)
If ANY of above missing = likely employee classification (ATO perspective)
Employee GP MUST have:
Employment contract (not contractor agreement)
PAYG tax withheld (ATO-registered as employer)
Superannuation contributions (11.5%) to employee’s fund
Leave entitlements (annual, personal, long service, parental)
Workers compensation insurance
Award compliance (if applicable – usually custom arrangements in medical)
Operator Responsibility

At hire: 1. Decide: contractor or employee (with accountant advice) 2. If contractor: - Request ABN + ABN lookup confirmation (abn.business.gov.au) - Obtain signed RACGP contractor agreement - Request proof of PII (insurance certificate) - Confirm independent contractor declaration signed - NO superannuation contributions - NO tax withholding 3. If employee: - Obtain signed employment contract - Set up PAYG withholding in payroll (calculate based on tax scale) - Set up superannuation deduction (11.5%) - Ensure WorkCover insurance covers - Register with WorkSafe Victoria (if not already)
Monthly (both types): - Verify contractor invoices vs billings (reconcile percentage or fee) - Verify employee PAYG withheld correctly (check payslips) - Verify superannuation contributions processed
7.2 NURSE EMPLOYMENT & CREDENTIALING
Registration & Credentials

Registered Nurse (RN): - Must be registered with AHPRA (Nursing and Midwifery Board of Australia) - Check registration: ahpra.gov.au > Find a Health Practitioner - Annual renewal required - Scope of practice: administers medications, performs clinical procedures
Enrolled Nurse (EN): - Also AHPRA-registered (different scope) - Can assist RN, limited independent scope - Check registration: AHPRA website
Immunisation Nurse: - Often RN with vaccination certificate (separate from AHPRA) - Must have current vaccination/immunisation certificate - Check certificate expiry annually
Award Coverage
Applicable: Nurses Award 2020 (Fair Work Ombudsman)
Minimum rates (April 2026 — external verification needed): - RN Level 1 (entry): ~AUD $65,000–$70,000 pa - RN Level 2: ~AUD $72,000–$78,000 pa - EN Level 1: ~AUD $55,000–$60,000 pa
Applies to: - Full-time nurses - Part-time nurses (pro-rated) - Casual nurses (slightly higher hourly rate to compensate for no leave)
Penalty rates: - Evening shift: ~15% loading - Weekend: ~50% loading (Saturday) / ~100% (Sunday) - Public holiday: ~150–250% loading

Operator control points: - At hire: obtain AHPRA certificate number; create reminder for annual check - Payroll: ensure Award rate applied (benchmark quarterly against Fair Work) - Timesheets: record all hours; flag unrecorded/unpaid work - Superannuation: 11.5% contributions mandatory
Credentialing File (Nurse)

Must contain: - AHPRA certificate (photocopy; verified against website) - Employment contract (signed) - References (2–3, from previous employers) - Police check (if required by clinic policy) - Immunisation record (if administering vaccines) - Hepatitis B immunity - MMR immunity - COVID-19 vaccination status - Influenza vaccination (annual) - Vaccination certificate (if immunisation nurse) - Training records - CPR (annual, must be current) - Infection control training (annual) - Privacy & confidentiality (annual) - PRODA/My Health Record (if applicable) - Bloodborne pathogens / sharps safety - Working With Children Check (if applicable – depends on role, varies by state) - Signed code of conduct / confidentiality agreement - Performance review records (annual)
Retention: 7 years after employment ends (Fair Work Act minimum)
7.3 RECEPTIONIST & ADMIN STAFF
Award Coverage
Applicable: Health Professionals Award 2020 OR Clerks Award 2020
Check classification: - Receptionist (front desk, patient registration) → typically Health Professionals Award - Admin/Data entry (back office) → typically Clerks Award

Minimum rates (April 2026 — external verification needed): - Receptionist Level 1: ~AUD $48,000–$52,000 pa - Receptionist Level 2 (senior): ~AUD $54,000–$60,000 pa - Data entry/Admin Level 1: ~AUD $48,000–$52,000 pa

Operator control points: - Classification review: annually confirm correct Award applied - Benchmark: quarterly check against Fair Work rates - Timesheets: record all hours; ensure minimum hours met (if contracted part-time)
Credentialing File (Reception/Admin)

Must contain: - Employment contract (signed) - References (2–3) - Police check (optional but recommended) - Training records - Privacy & confidentiality (annual) - PRODA/My Health Record training (if access to systems) - Patient registration/billing systems (initial + refresher) - Phone etiquette / patient communication (initial) - WHS/infection control (annual) - PRODA security (annual, if applicable) - Signed code of conduct / confidentiality agreement - Performance reviews (annual)
7.4 PRACTICE MANAGER EMPLOYMENT
Role Definition

Typical responsibilities: - Daily clinic operations - Staff rostering & timesheets - Payroll approval - Supplier management - Compliance & accreditation liaison - Patient communication (non-clinical) - Incident/complaint response - Budget oversight
Award & Wages
Not typically Award-covered (management role).
Custom arrangements common: - Salary-based (AUD $65,000–$85,000 pa for small–medium practice) - Senior manager: AUD $85,000–$110,000 pa - Plus superannuation (11.5%)

Operator responsibility: - Define role in employment contract - Specify delegated authority (who approves what spending?) - Include confidentiality & restraint clauses - Annual performance review & remuneration review
Credentialing File (Practice Manager)
Employment contract (signed)
References
Training records (privacy, PRODA, MyHR, WHS)
Performance reviews
7.5 ONBOARDING CHECKLIST
Before first day in clinic:

Documentation (5 days before): - [ ] Employment contract signed (or contractor agreement) - [ ] Tax file number declaration form (TFN) completed - [ ] Superannuation fund details collected (employee only) - [ ] Bank account for salary/payment collected - [ ] Emergency contact details - [ ] References verified

System access (day 1): - [ ] Email account created - [ ] Practice management system (Best Practice/MD) access granted with appropriate permissions - [ ] PRODA access (if applicable; practice manager only) - [ ] My Health Record access trained (if applicable) - [ ] Phone extension assigned - [ ] Car park / access card issued - [ ] IT passwords set (securely transmitted, changed on first login)

Training (week 1): - [ ] Induction video / handbook provided - [ ] Clinic tour - [ ] Emergency procedures (evacuation, medical emergency, security) - [ ] Privacy & confidentiality training (documented) - [ ] Infection control / hand hygiene training - [ ] Patient registration system walkthrough (reception/admin) - [ ] Billing system walkthrough (if applicable) - [ ] PRODA/My Health Record training (if applicable) - [ ] Code of conduct explained & signed - [ ] Confidentiality agreement signed - [ ] Workers compensation insurance explained (employees)

Credentialing (within 2 weeks): - [ ] For clinical staff (GPs, nurses): AHPRA registration verified online - [ ] For all staff: police check results received (if required) - [ ] References received & documented - [ ] Training certificates (CPR, infection control) verified - [ ] Immunisation records reviewed (nurses/clinical) - [ ] Credentialing file created & stored securely

Payroll setup (day 1–3): - [ ] If employee: PAYG withholding set up (ATO tax scale tables) - [ ] If employee: superannuation fund details entered in payroll system - [ ] Timesheet system explained - [ ] First payment scheduled (weekly/fortnightly/monthly)
7.6 LEAVE MANAGEMENT
Annual Leave
Entitlement (Award/National Employment Standards): - Full-time: 4 weeks per year - Part-time: pro-rata (based on hours worked) - Accrues month-by-month or weekly (depends on agreement)
Loading: 17.5% penalty loading if not taken (or paid out at termination)

Operator control: - Maintain leave register (date, duration, balance) - Approve leave requests 2–4 weeks in advance - Ensure minimum staffing maintained during leave periods - Pay out untaken leave on termination (rarely withheld)
Personal Leave (Sick & Carer’s Leave)
Entitlement: - Full-time: 10 days per year (can be used for own illness or caring for family) - Part-time: pro-rata - Casual: no entitlement (can request unpaid leave)

Operator control: - Maintain separate leave register - Request medical certificate if >2 consecutive days absent (or as per policy) - Cannot refuse reasonable personal leave request - Unused personal leave does NOT accrue or pay out (expires each year in most cases)
Long Service Leave

Entitlement: - Accrues after 10 years of service (1.3 weeks per year, capped at 13 weeks for ongoing employees) - Can be taken as week-long breaks or saved - Rarely used in small GP practices (high staff turnover)
Operator control: - Maintain record of accrual - Discuss options near 10-year mark - Pay out on termination after 10 years of service
Parental Leave
Entitlement: - 12 months unpaid (per National Employment Standards) - Some employers offer paid (discretionary)
Operator responsibility: - Confirm in writing within 4 weeks of notification - Discuss return-to-work arrangements - Hold role open (or equivalent) for 12 months
7.7 ROSTERING & TIMESHEETS
Rostering System

Good practice: - Roster planned 4–6 weeks in advance - Published on staff notice board + online system (if available) - Staff can request days off (reasonable notice required) - Clinic can refuse if impact on operations too high - Changes to roster: minimum 2 weeks notice (unless emergency)

Operator control points: - Monthly: review roster against staffing levels (no understaffing) - Avoid: regular overtime (indicates understaffing); if used, ensure paid fairly - Fairness: rotate unpopular shifts (evenings, weekends) fairly across team
Timesheets
For all staff (except GPs, who invoice separately):

Process: - Staff record daily hours (start time, finish time, breaks) - Fortnightly/weekly signature/confirmation by practice manager - Cross-check against roster (flag discrepancies) - Feed into payroll system - Retain 5+ years

Operator control: - Daily: monitor timesheets for obvious errors (e.g., 12-hour shifts without overtime recorded) - Weekly: sign off timesheets; ensure total hours match contracted hours - Monthly: total hours payroll vs hours paid (variance investigation) - Red flags: - Staff working unpaid hours (indicate systemic understaffing or informal arrangement) - Timesheet “ghosting” (staff forget to record, estimate at end of week)
7.8 CONTRACTOR GP PAYMENT PROCESS
Weekly/fortnightly process:
Billings report: run from Best Practice (or practice management system)
Filter by contractor GP name
Sum all items billed (Medicare + private) for the period
Calculate payment:
Billings × percentage (e.g., 70%) = amount due
Or: flat fee + (excess billings × percentage)
Cross-check:
Compare this week’s billings to previous weeks (unusual spike = investigate)
Ensure items claimed to Medicare are included (cross-ref Medicare receipt list)
Verify no duplicate entries
Generate invoice/payment schedule:
Create payment slip showing calculation (GPs appreciate transparency)
Schedule payment to GP’s bank account
Record in general ledger:
GP contractor expense account
Match payment to invoice (audit trail)

Red flags: - GP payment > 75% (losing too much to contractor) - GP payment < 60% (GP may claim underpayment; verify contract) - Sudden drop in GP billings (investigate: patient transfers? items coded incorrectly?) - GP disputes calculation (maintain clear records to defend)
7.9 PAYROLL APPROVAL & CONTROLS
Payroll Process (Weekly or Fortnightly)
Timesheet approval: practice manager signs off all timesheets
Payroll run: bookkeeper/payroll software calculates gross pay, PAYG, super, deductions
Payroll report: bookkeeper provides summary (who’s paid, how much, deductions)
Owner approval: owner reviews and approves before payment
Payment: salary processed to staff bank accounts
Documentation: payslips issued to staff; retained for 7 years
PAYG Withholding Accuracy

Common errors: - Wrong tax scale (check ATO update quarterly) - Incorrect salary basis (e.g., forget to include super in gross pay) - Tax-free threshold not claimed (if applicable) - Multiple job withholding not adjusted

Operator check: - Monthly: spot-check 2–3 payslips (gross, tax withheld, net) - Quarterly: compare total tax withheld to BAS; should be close match - Annual: staff can claim refund if over-withheld, so small surpluses acceptable
Superannuation Processing
Mandatory (11.5% from 1 July 2025): - Calculate 11.5% of gross salary - Deposit to employee’s SuperFund (employee nominates) - Within 28 days of end of pay cycle (or monthly)
Operator check: - Monthly: verify super deposited to employee funds - Quarterly: BAS/reconciliation shows total super paid - Annual: obtain donation receipts from SuperFund (matches total paid)
Common issues: - Super paid late (employees can complain to ATO; clinic faces penalties) - Wrong amount (under-contribution) - Paid to wrong fund (employee must verify)
7.10 CREDENTIALING: MASTER CHECKLIST
For ALL practitioners & staff:

Storage: - Individual credentialing file per staff member - Stored securely (password-protected if digital, locked if paper) - Backup copy (encrypted external drive, off-site) - Access limited to practice manager + owner
7.11 PERFORMANCE MANAGEMENT & DISCIPLINE
Informal Process (for minor issues)
Conversation: manager addresses issue privately, same day if possible
Documentation: manager notes conversation (date, issue, outcome)
Improvement: agree on improvement and follow-up date
Re-check: manager confirms improvement at follow-up
Record: file in staff member’s performance file
Examples: missed timelines, minor errors, attitude issue
Formal Performance Management (for serious issues)
Step 1: Informal warning (if first offence) - Verbal discussion documented in writing - Copy provided to staff member - 2-week improvement period - Re-check and document outcome

Step 2: Formal warning (if issue persists or serious breach) - Written warning letter - State exact issue, expected standard, consequences of further breach - Right to respond in writing - Improvement plan (2–4 weeks) - Re-check and document

Step 3: Disciplinary action or termination - Final written warning or dismissal (depending on severity) - Follow Fair Work requirements (no harsh, unjust termination) - Provide all documentation to staff member - Reasonable opportunity to respond

Operator responsibility: - Document EVERY performance issue in writing (date, issue, outcome) - Act quickly but fairly - Don’t gossip about performance issues (confidentiality) - If dismissal is likely, consult legal counsel
7.12 TERMINATION & OFFBOARDING
Notice Periods

For employees: - Minimum notice (Fair Work Act): 1–4 weeks depending on tenure - Most employment contracts specify longer (2–4 weeks common) - Immediate termination only for serious misconduct (consult legal counsel first)
For contractors: - Per contractor agreement (typically 2–4 weeks) - Check contract before terminating
Operator action: - If terminating: provide written notice (minimum 2 weeks is standard) - State last day of work and final pay date - Arrange handover - Plan for backfill
Final Pay

What’s included: - Salary to last day of work - Accrued annual leave (paid out in full) - Personal leave (not paid out unless agreement allows) - Long service leave (paid out if >10 years service) - Superannuation (any final contributions)
What’s deducted: - Any equipment not returned (if agreed in contract) - Any overpayment (if agreed with employee) - Debt to clinic (rare and contentious; legal advice needed)

Operator check: - Within 2 weeks: issue final payslip - Before last day: collect all keys, access cards, equipment - On last day: obtain signed equipment return form - After departure: remove system access (email, PRODA, Best Practice, etc.)
Offboarding Checklist

Before departure: - [ ] Handover meeting (identify in-progress work, patient handover, etc.) - [ ] Training for replacement begun - [ ] Patient files organised (ensure continuity) - [ ] GP/nurse: identify which patients transfer to which practitioner

At departure: - [ ] All equipment returned (stethoscope, laptop, ID badge, etc.) - [ ] Keys & access cards surrendered - [ ] System access disabled (email, PRODA, Best Practice, My Health Record) - [ ] Exit interview conducted (feedback on role, workplace) - [ ] Final payslip issued - [ ] References provided (if requested, at clinic discretion)

After departure: - [ ] Credential file archived - [ ] Employment record retained (7 years minimum) - [ ] AHPRA registration no longer relied upon (patients transferred) - [ ] Tax & superannuation finalised with ATO
7.13 INCIDENT REPORTING & INVESTIGATION
Duty to Report

Incidents requiring documentation: - Patient safety incident (near-miss, harm) - Staff injury or near-miss (WorkSafe obligation) - Infection control breach (e.g., needle stick, spill) - Data security breach (patient data exposure) - Medication error - Staff conflict or complaint - Equipment failure

Process: 1. Incident report form: completed by person present (or manager if not available) 2. Date, time, location, people involved: key details 3. Description: what happened, what was noticed 4. Immediate action taken: (first aid, isolation, reporting to supervisor) 5. Investigation: manager interviews involved parties, reviews records 6. Finding: what was the root cause? 7. Corrective action: what will be done to prevent recurrence? 8. Documentation: report filed, reviewed by owner/manager
Retention: 7 years minimum (relevant to WorkSafe, Privacy Commissioner investigations)
WorkSafe Victoria Reporting Obligation

Must notify WorkSafe if: - Staff member injured (even if minor) during work - Serious injury (serious bodily injury, serious illness, death) - Dangerous occurrence (collapse of structure, exposure to chemical, etc.)
Timeline: - Serious injury: notify WorkSafe ASAP (phone 1300 366 779, then written notification) - Non-serious injury: notify within 10 days (written form)

Operator responsibility: - Know which injuries require notification - Report promptly (late reporting incurs penalty) - Cooperate with WorkSafe investigation - Implement recommendations from investigation
7.14 BULLYING, HARASSMENT & COMPLAINTS
Fair Work Act Obligations

Clinic must prevent: - Bullying (repeated, unreasonable conduct creating risk to health/safety) - Harassment (conduct based on protected attribute: age, sex, disability, etc.) - Discrimination (unfair treatment based on attribute) - Sexual harassment

Operator responsibility: - Have written anti-bullying, anti-harassment policy - Make policy known to all staff - Train staff on what constitutes bullying/harassment - Investigate complaints seriously & impartially - Support complainant (no victimisation) - Disciplinary action against perpetrator (if substantiated)
Complaint Handling Process
Informal complaint: staff member raises issue with manager verbally
Manager listens, documents (date, issue, person’s account)
Manager investigates (interview other parties, review records)
Manager resolves (if possible) and documents outcome
Formal complaint: if informal not resolved or serious issue
Written complaint lodged with owner/practice manager
Owner acknowledges within 5 business days
Owner investigates (separate from manager, if manager involved)
Owner interviews all parties
Owner provides written response within 10 business days (minimum)
Outcome documented, filed
Escalation: if not resolved, staff member can lodge with Fair Work Commission (FWC)
Clinic has 10 business days to respond
FWC conciliation or arbitration
Red flags: - Complaint not documented (defensible position lost) - Delayed response (indicates lack of seriousness) - Complaining party moved/demoted (victimisation claim)
7.15 WORKERS COMPENSATION & WORKPLACE INJURY
WorkCover Victoria Insurance
Mandatory if: clinic has employees (even 1 part-time employee)
Coverage: - Medical treatment (doctor, hospital, surgery, therapy) - Wages replacement (60% weekly for up to 26 weeks; then can review) - Rehabilitation - Return-to-work assistance
Premium calculation: - Based on payroll + risk class (medical practice = low-moderate risk, ~0.5–1.5%) - Annual review & adjustment if payroll changes

Operator responsibility: - Maintain current certificate of currency - Notify insurer of new staff, payroll changes - Report injuries to insurer within 10 days - Cooperate with rehabilitation provider - Keep injured worker informed
Injury Notification & Documentation
If staff member injured at work:
Immediate: first aid/medical treatment
Report to manager: incident report form completed
Notify WorkCover: within 10 days (written form or online)
Cooperate with insurer: they manage claim, treatment, wages
Rehabilitation: comply with return-to-work plan
Retainage: file incident report, worker statement, doctor’s reports (7 years minimum)
7.16 STAFF TRAINING & DEVELOPMENT
Mandatory Training (Annual)
All staff: - [ ] Privacy & confidentiality (1–2 hours) - [ ] Infection control (1 hour) - [ ] WHS/SafeWork (1 hour) - [ ] Bullying & harassment awareness (30 mins) - [ ] Code of conduct (30 mins)
If applicable: - [ ] PRODA security training (IT manager, reception, admin) - [ ] My Health Record training (reception, clinical staff) - [ ] Patient registration/billing system refresher (reception)

Clinical staff: - [ ] CPR certification (1 day, annual renewal) - [ ] Bloodborne pathogen / sharps safety (1 hour) - [ ] Vaccination protocols (if administering vaccines) - [ ] AHPRA/professional development (varies by scope)
Training Records
Maintain register: - Staff member name - Training topic - Date completed - Trainer/provider - Certificate number (if applicable) - Due for renewal date
Documentation: certificates filed in staff credentialing file
7.17 HR POLICIES & TEMPLATES
Essential Policies (Written & Communicated)
Code of Conduct
Professional standards
Confidentiality
Conflict of interest
Social media use
Dress code
Privacy & Confidentiality Policy
Patient data protection
OPAL Act obligations
My Health Record security
Breach response process
Anti-Bullying & Harassment Policy
Definition of bullying/harassment
Complaint procedure
Confidentiality
No victimisation
Leave Policy
Annual leave accrual & taking
Personal leave (sick/carer’s)
Long service leave
Parental leave
Other (compassionate, special)
WHS Policy
Hazard identification & control
Incident reporting
Emergency procedures
First aid & AED
Ergonomics
Infection Control Policy
Hand hygiene
Personal protective equipment (PPE)
Needle stick/sharps protocol
Spill management
Vaccination requirements
Recruitment & Selection Policy
Advertising standards
Interview process
Reference checks
Credentialing requirements
Offer of employment process
Performance Management Policy
Induction process
Performance expectations
Feedback & development
Formal discipline process
Termination process
Payroll & Superannuation Policy
Pay cycle
Deductions
Superannuation contributions
Leave loading
Final pay
IT & Data Security Policy
System access & passwords
Data backup & recovery
Cybersecurity standards
Incident response
Personal device use
Recommended Template Source
Fair Work Ombudsman: free templates & guidance (www.fairwork.gov.au)
RACGP: member resources (if practice is member)
Legal counsel: custom policies for practice size/structure
7.18 STAFF FILE RETENTION
What to Keep

For every employee/contractor: - Employment contract (signed) - References - Credentialing documentation (AHPRA, police check, training certs) - All payslips (or annual tax documents) - Tax file number declaration - Superannuation fund details - All performance review forms - All incident/complaint records (if any) - Proof of training (privacy, CPR, etc.) - Final payslip & separation documents
Retention Period
Minimum 7 years after employment ends (Fair Work Act, tax law, Privacy Act)
Best practice: 10 years
Some records (e.g., incident reports involving potential injury) kept longer
Confidentiality & Access
Filed securely (locked filing cabinet or password-protected digital)
Access limited to practice owner, practice manager, HR manager
Discuss only on need-to-know basis
Do not share with staff (other than employee’s own file)
END PART 7
PART 8: REGISTRATIONS, IDENTIFIERS, ACCESS & COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST
Overview

A GP practice operates across multiple government, regulatory, and private systems. Each requires separate registration, identifiers, and access management. This section is a complete checklist of what exists, what doesn’t, what needs to be verified, and what goes wrong if neglected.
8.1 CLINIC/BUSINESS-LEVEL REGISTRATIONS
ABN & ACN
What it is: - ABN (Australian Business Number): 11-digit unique identifier for all business activities - ACN (Australian Company Number): if structure is Pty Ltd (company)
Why it matters: - Required for Medicare claiming - Required for tax (PAYG, GST) - Required for business bank account - Required for PayPal, merchant services, etc.
Who controls access: - ABN: registrations open (public); no password needed - ACN: ASIC register (public); structure details (shareholding) may be private or public

Evidence it exists: - ABN lookup: abn.business.gov.au (search by name or ABN number) - ACN lookup: asic.gov.au > Company Search (if company) - ABN notification letter (from ATO when business registered)
Renewal/maintenance: - ABN: permanent (no renewal, but must notify ATO if name/address changes) - ACN: annual fees to ASIC; company must lodge financial statements annually

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] ABN lookup online confirms correct business name, address, structure - [ ] If company: ACN lookup confirms director names, shareholding, status (active)
Business Name & Structure
What it is: - Business trading name (may differ from ABN name) - Structure: sole trader, partnership, trust, company, or franchise
Why it matters: - Determines tax treatment - Determines liability (sole trader = personal liability; company = limited) - Affects superannuation obligations - Affects buy-sell/succession arrangements
Evidence it exists: - ABN notification (shows structure) - Trust deed (if trust structure) - Partnership agreement (if partnership) - ASIC company details (if Pty Ltd)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] ABN name matches trading name (or authorised alias registered) - [ ] Structure confirmed (sole trader? trust? company?) - [ ] If trust: trust deed obtained and reviewed by accountant - [ ] If partnership: partnership agreement exists and reviewed by legal counsel
Medicare Location Registration
What it is: - Practice is registered with Medicare as a healthcare provider location - Required to claim Medicare benefits - Separate from individual provider registration
Why it matters: - Without location registration, clinic cannot lodge Medicare claims - Affects practice incentives, grants, and funding eligibility - Must match to PRODA & HPI-O
Who controls access: - Medicare (Department of Health) - Delegate: practice manager, bookkeeper, or designated staff member
Evidence it exists: - Medicare location identifier (8-digit code, visible in Best Practice / MD system) - Medicare registration letter (from Department of Health, may be archived)
Renewal / maintenance: - Once registered, no formal renewal - But must notify Medicare if details change (location address, GPs on site, etc.)

What goes wrong if neglected: - Claims rejected (claims software won’t process without location ID) - Practice cash flow interrupted (no Medicare deposits) - Accreditation affected (auditors require proof of Medicare location status)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] Medicare location ID exists and matches to clinic address - [ ] Location ID entered correctly in practice management software - [ ] Test claim submitted to verify acceptance by Medicare
Practice Incentives Program (PIP) Registration
What it is: - Australian government program providing financial incentives for meeting quality/service targets - Administered by Services Australia - Practice must be registered to receive payments

Why it matters: - PIP payments: typically AUD $10,000–$50,000+ per year depending on targets achieved - Missing PIP registration = lost income - Targets cover: patient safety, chronic disease management, mental health, Indigenous health, etc.
Evidence it exists: - PIP registration letter (from Services Australia) - PIP portal access (Services Australia online) - Quarterly PIP payments visible in bank statement/practice management system
Renewal / maintenance: - Annual renewal (must re-confirm eligibility) - Quarterly reporting of performance metrics (may be automatic if PRODA linked)
What goes wrong if neglected: - PIP payments delayed or lost - Quarterly reporting missed = payment suspension - Targets not met = no payment for that quarter

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] PIP registration letter obtained - [ ] PIP portal access tested (login successful, current practice details visible) - [ ] Last 4 quarters of PIP payments visible in bank account - [ ] Quarterly performance targets reviewed & understood
Workforce Incentive Program (WIP)
What it is: - Government funding for rural/remote practices or practices meeting specific workforce goals - Administered by Services Australia - Provides grants for hiring nurses, allied health, etc.

Why it matters: - If clinic in eligible area: potential AUD $50,000–$200,000+ in annual funding - Attached to specific staffing requirements (must hire RN, psychologist, etc.) - Miss requirements = repayment of grant
Eligibility: - Practice in rural or remote area (by postcode), OR - Meets specific service provision goals
Evidence it exists: - WIP grant agreement (Services Australia) - WIP portal access
Renewal / maintenance: - Annual; must confirm ongoing eligibility & staffing

What goes wrong if neglected: - Grant payments lost - If already received: may have to repay if staffing requirement not maintained - Clinic loses competitive advantage in recruitment (WIP funding often used to attract staff)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] Determine if clinic eligible (by location or service model) - [ ] If eligible: WIP registration obtained; payments received - [ ] WIP staffing requirements understood & documented - [ ] Compliance with staffing requirements confirmed
HPI-O (Healthcare Provider Identifier – Organisation)

What it is: - 16-character code issued by Services Australia (HPOS system) - Unique identifier for the practice as a healthcare provider - Required for integration with My Health Record, PBS, e-referrals, etc.

Why it matters: - Enables secure electronic communication with hospitals, specialists, pathologists - Required for PBS (Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme) access - Required for eReferral systems - Identity verification for secure messaging (NASH)
Evidence it exists: - HPI-O code issued by Services Australia / HPOS - Letter or email confirming HPI-O - May be visible in Best Practice or practice management software
Renewal / maintenance: - Assigned once; no renewal - Must update if practice details change (address, ownership, etc.)
What goes wrong if neglected: - Cannot integrate with My Health Record (eHealth services broken) - Cannot access PBS online - eReferral systems may not recognize practice

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] HPI-O code obtained from HPOS - [ ] HPI-O code matches to practice name & address - [ ] HPI-O loaded into Best Practice/practice management system - [ ] Test integration with My Health Record successful
NASH Certificate (National Authentication Service for Health)

What it is: - Security credential (digital certificate) issued by Services Australia - Enables secure communication with My Health Record, PBS, eReferral, etc. - Installed on clinic server or accessed via web portal
Why it matters: - Essential for secure clinical messaging - Enables two-factor authentication to government health systems - Protects patient data in transit
Evidence it exists: - NASH certificate details (certificate number, expiry date) - Visible in Best Practice or medical software (if integrated) - HPOS system shows active NASH certificate
Renewal / maintenance: - Expires typically every 2–3 years; must renew before expiry - Renewal done via HPOS or Services Australia
What goes wrong if neglected: - Certificate expires = lose secure access to My Health Record, PBS, eReferral - Clinical workflow interrupted - Electronic prescribing blocked

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] NASH certificate obtained & installed - [ ] Expiry date noted; calendar reminder set for renewal (3 months before expiry) - [ ] Certificate tested (login to HPOS successful)
My Health Record Participation & Policy

What it is: - Practice enrolled in My Health Record (patient health records stored in national system) - Practice commits to contributing patient data to My Health Record - Requires My Health Record participation policy

Why it matters: - Enables care coordination across practitioners & hospitals - Improves patient safety (medication reconciliation, allergy alerts) - Required for accreditation (AGPAL, QPA) - Required for some government funding (PIP, WIP)

Evidence it exists: - My Health Record registration confirmation (from Services Australia / HPOS) - My Health Record participation policy (written document) - Practice can access My Health Record portal (test access)
Policy must cover: - How practice accesses & uses patient records - Confidentiality & security measures - Patient privacy rights - Breach notification process - Data retention & deletion
Renewal / maintenance: - Registration is ongoing (no formal renewal) - Policy review: annual minimum - Compliance audit: as per accreditation cycle
What goes wrong if neglected: - Practice cannot participate in My Health Record (integration broken) - Accreditation at risk - Funding (PIP/WIP) at risk - Patient safety concerns

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] My Health Record registration confirmation obtained - [ ] Participation policy exists (written, communicated to staff) - [ ] Test access to My Health Record portal successful (can view patient records) - [ ] Staff training on My Health Record completed
PRODA Organisation Registration

What it is: - Practice Registration and Disclosures Authority registration - Enables practice staff to use government health systems (HPOS, NASH, Medicare, PBS, eReferral, etc.) - Practice manager typically holds organisation admin account

Why it matters: - Without PRODA registration, staff cannot submit Medicare claims, access HPOS, or use PBS - PRODA tracks who has access to which systems (audit trail) - Security-critical (must restrict access to authorized staff only)
Evidence it exists: - PRODA organisation account active (can log in) - Delegations list showing authorized staff members - Delegation notification letters (one per staff member)

Delegations: - Practice manager: typically has admin rights (can add/remove other users) - Bookkeeper: can submit Medicare claims, access bank reconciliation - GP: can access HPOS, prescriber details, PBS - Reception: can view/update patient details, submit claims - Others: as appropriate to role

Renewal / maintenance: - PRODA account active as long as practice is registered - Annual review of delegations (remove departed staff, add new staff) - If staff member leaves: immediately revoke access in PRODA

What goes wrong if neglected: - Staff member leaves but retains PRODA access (security risk; could fraudulently claim Medicare) - New staff cannot submit claims (waiting time for access = cash flow delays) - Auditors cannot verify who submitted claims (compliance failure) - Departed staff submit fraudulent claims (practice liable)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] PRODA organisation account exists & active (test login) - [ ] Current delegations list obtained (who has what access?) - [ ] Cross-check delegations against current staff list (remove inactive users) - [ ] Test staff member access (e.g., reception logs in successfully) - [ ] Document change log: when were delegations last reviewed/updated?
HPOS Access & Delegations
What it is: - HPOS (Health Provider Online Services): platform for accessing NASH, PBS, eReferral, etc. - Requires PRODA account to authenticate

Why it matters: - GPs use HPOS to check PBS eligibility, prescriber status, patient authority numbers - Nurses use HPOS for immunisation records (if registered) - Reception/admin use HPOS for referral tracking
Evidence it exists: - HPOS login credentials (username/password) - PRODA delegations enable HPOS access - Test login successful (staff can access HPOS portal)
Renewal / maintenance: - HPOS access maintained as long as PRODA account active - Passwords should be secure (changed regularly) - Access revoked when staff member leaves (via PRODA)

What goes wrong if neglected: - GPs cannot check PBS eligibility (prescribing blocked or non-subsidised) - Referrals cannot be tracked (patient workflow broken) - Staff use weak passwords (security breach risk)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] HPOS access confirmed for GPs & nurses (test login) - [ ] HPOS password policy established (strong passwords, no sharing) - [ ] HPOS access revoked for any departed staff (verify in HPOS)
Accreditation (AGPAL or QPA)

What it is: - AGPAL (Australian General Practice Accreditation Ltd): accredits practices against RACGP Standards 5th Edition - QPA (Quality Practice Accreditation): alternative accreditor - Voluntary but required for many grants (PIP, WIP, etc.)

Why it matters: - Accreditation demonstrates quality & safety standards - Required for some government funding - Enhances reputation & patient confidence - Audits identify operational gaps & compliance risks
Scope: - RACGP Standards 5th Edition cover: clinical governance, patient safety, risk management, staff qualifications, information management, etc.

Evidence it exists: - Accreditation certificate (issued by AGPAL or QPA) - Accreditation valid until date (typically 3 years from issue date) - Recent accreditation visit report (post-visit documentation)

Renewal / maintenance: - Accreditation cycle: every 3 years - Pre-accreditation planning: 6–12 months before expiry - Accreditation visit: 2–3 days on-site; auditors review policies, records, staff, premises - Post-accreditation: corrective action plan for any non-compliances
Cost: - Application & accreditation: AUD $3,000–$8,000 (varies by practice size) - Staff time: significant (preparing documentation, hosting visit)

What goes wrong if neglected: - Accreditation lapses (ineligible for PIP, WIP, many grants) - Audits identify serious compliance gaps too late (regulatory risk) - Patient confidence affected - Staff morale (accreditation demonstrates commitment to quality)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] Accreditation certificate obtained & current (within 3 years of issue date) - [ ] Accreditation audit report reviewed (any non-compliances addressed?) - [ ] Accreditation renewal scheduled (6 months before current expires) - [ ] RACGP Standards 5th Edition reviewed by staff (key changes understood)
Payroll Tax Registration (Victoria)
What it is: - Registration with State Revenue Office Victoria (SRO) - Required if payroll > ~AUD $900,000 per year (2026 threshold) - Rate: ~4.85% of payroll above threshold
Why it matters: - If payroll over threshold but not registered: penalties, interest, audit risk - Missed registrations often caught during tax audits - Quarterly lodgement & payment required
Evidence it exists: - Payroll tax registration number (issued by SRO) - Registration letter or certificate - Quarterly payroll tax returns (BAS-style)
Renewal / maintenance: - Ongoing; no formal renewal - Quarterly lodgement & payment mandatory

What goes wrong if neglected: - Payroll tax owed accumulates (with interest & penalties) - Audit triggers full investigation (includes prior years) - Cash flow impact (large catch-up payment required)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] Estimate annual payroll (sum all employees + GPs if employed) - [ ] If over AUD $900,000: confirm payroll tax registered - [ ] If registered: last quarterly return obtained & reviewed - [ ] If not registered but over threshold: immediate registration required (consult accountant)
WorkSafe Victoria Registration
What it is: - Registration with WorkSafe Victoria as employer - Required if clinic has employees (even 1 part-time) - Employer must hold valid WorkCover insurance

Why it matters: - Required by law; failure to register = prosecution risk - WorkCover insurance mandatory (lapsed = employees uninsured) - WorkSafe audits compliance (WHS policies, incident reporting, etc.)
Evidence it exists: - WorkCover certificate of currency (issued by insurer) - WorkSafe employer number
Renewal / maintenance: - WorkCover insurance renewed annually - WorkSafe registration ongoing (no formal renewal) - Incident reporting to WorkSafe (within 10 days of serious injury)
What goes wrong if neglected: - Staff injury uninsured (clinic liable for all costs) - WorkSafe prosecution & fines - Reputational damage & loss of insurance

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] WorkCover certificate of currency obtained (valid until date) - [ ] WorkSafe employer number confirmed - [ ] Last certificate reviewed (renewal due within 6 months?)
8.2 PRACTITIONER-LEVEL REGISTRATIONS
AHPRA Registration (Medical & Nursing)
What it is: - Regulation by Australian Health Practitioner Regulation Agency - Mandatory for GPs, nurses, psychologists, etc. - Registration number published on AHPRA website

Why it matters: - Legal requirement to practice medicine/nursing in Australia - Patient safety & quality assurance - If registration lapsed: practitioner cannot legally practice - Hiring unregistered practitioner = patient safety risk + legal liability
How to verify: - AHPRA website: ahpra.gov.au > Find a health practitioner - Search by name; confirm registration status, provider number, any restrictions
Renewal / maintenance: - Renewal cycles vary (typically annual for GPs; 2-yearly for nurses) - Renewal reminder sent by AHPRA before expiry - Non-renewal = automatic suspension

Continued professional development (CPD): - GPs: minimum CPD hours required annually (varies by state; ~40 hours common) - Nurses: similar requirements - Must be documented (college of GPs, nursing body)
What goes wrong if neglected: - Lapsed registration = practitioner cannot practice legally - Patient safety concern - Hiring/retaining unregistered practitioner = regulatory breach + liability

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each GP & nurse: - [ ] AHPRA lookup shows active registration - [ ] Registration number noted & recorded in credentialing file - [ ] Renewal date noted; calendar reminder set (3 months before expiry) - [ ] Any restrictions or conditions on registration noted - [ ] CPD requirements & evidence of compliance documented
Medicare Provider Numbers

What it is: - Location-specific provider number issued by Medicare - Each practitioner must have separate number at each practice location - Required to claim Medicare on behalf of patients (bulk-billing)

Why it matters: - Without provider number: claims rejected by Medicare - Cash flow interrupted (no rebates if not claimed correctly) - Each location requires separate number (if GP works at multiple clinics)

Evidence it exists: - Medicare provider number letter (from Department of Health) - Provider number visible in Best Practice or practice management software - Provider number used in Medicare claim headers
Renewal / maintenance: - Issued once; no formal renewal - Must notify Medicare if GP leaves or changes location
What goes wrong if neglected: - Claims rejected (Medicare cannot match to provider) - Delayed or missing payments - Accreditation auditors require proof of valid provider numbers

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each GP: - [ ] Medicare provider number obtained & recorded - [ ] Provider number matches to clinic location - [ ] Test claim submitted using provider number (accepted by Medicare) - [ ] Provider number entered correctly in practice management software
Prescriber Numbers
What it is: - National Prescriber Number (NPN) issued by Therapeutic Goods Administration (TGA) - Unique identifier on all PBS prescriptions - Required for legal prescribing in Australia
Why it matters: - Without NPN: scripts not processed by pharmacy - Patients cannot access subsidised medicines - Regulatory requirement
Evidence it exists: - NPN issued by TGA / Prescriber Registration system - NPN printed on prescriptionpads or digital prescribing system - NPN visible in Best Practice or e-prescription software
Renewal / maintenance: - Issued once; no formal renewal - If practitioner relocates: may need new NPN for new location (check TGA)
What goes wrong if neglected: - Scripts cannot be processed (patient cannot get medications) - Clinical workflow interrupted - Regulatory non-compliance

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each prescriber (GP, nurse practitioner): - [ ] NPN obtained & recorded - [ ] Test script written & verified by pharmacist (accepted) - [ ] NPN entered in e-prescription software (if used)
HPI-I (Healthcare Provider Identifier – Individual)
What it is: - 16-character code issued by Services Australia (HPOS system) - Unique identifier for the individual practitioner - Required for integration with My Health Record, PBS, eReferral, etc.
Why it matters: - Enables secure electronic communication with other providers - Required for ePrescribing to PBS - Identity verification for clinical messaging
Evidence it exists: - HPI-I code issued by Services Australia / HPOS - Letter or email confirming HPI-I - HPI-I visible in practice management software (if integrated)
Renewal / maintenance: - Issued once; no formal renewal - Must update if practitioner name changes
What goes wrong if neglected: - Cannot integrate with secure clinical messaging (eHealth broken) - Cannot access PBS online (ePrescribing blocked) - eReferral systems may not recognize practitioner

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each practitioner: - [ ] HPI-I code obtained from HPOS - [ ] HPI-I code matches to practitioner name - [ ] HPI-I loaded into practice management software - [ ] Test ePrescribing or secure message: successful
PRODA Individual Accounts

What it is: - Individual PRODA accounts for each staff member who accesses government health systems - Enables auditing of who accessed what (accountability) - Requires individual credentials (username/password)

Why it matters: - Security: can revoke access immediately if staff member leaves - Audit trail: Medicare Compliance can see who submitted each claim - Fraud prevention: can identify unauthorized access
Evidence it exists: - Individual PRODA login credentials (username/password) - PRODA account exists & active (test login) - Delegation notification letter (per staff member)
Renewal / maintenance: - Accounts active as long as staff member employed - Immediate revocation when staff member leaves (critical) - Password changes every 6–12 months

What goes wrong if neglected: - Departed staff retains access (security breach; fraudulent claims possible) - Cannot revoke access (Medicare still processes claims under that account) - Audit trail compromised

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each staff member with system access: - [ ] PRODA individual account exists & active - [ ] Test login successful (staff can access HPOS, Medicare, PBS) - [ ] Delegation letter obtained & filed - [ ] Offboarding procedure: PRODA access revoked immediately on departure - [ ] Audit of PRODA account activity (review quarterly who logged in, what they accessed)
My Health Record Individual Setup
What it is: - Individual healthcare practitioners register their professional credentials in My Health Record system - Enables practitioners to view & contribute to patient records

Why it matters: - GPs & nurses must register individually to access/contribute to My Health Record - Each practitioner has individual audit trail (accountability) - Practice cannot contribute without practitioner registration
Evidence it exists: - Individual registration confirmed in My Health Record (search practitioner name) - Test access: practitioner can log in & view patient records
Renewal / maintenance: - Registration ongoing (no formal renewal) - Must update if practitioner name or credentials change
What goes wrong if neglected: - Individual practitioner cannot access My Health Record (workflow broken) - Practice cannot document care in My Health Record - Patient safety coordination compromised

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each clinician (GP, nurse): - [ ] Individual registration in My Health Record confirmed - [ ] Test access: practitioner logs in successfully - [ ] Practitioner trained on contributing to My Health Record
PBS Authority Prescriber Status

What it is: - Prescriber authorization status for Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme - Some medicines require prescriber authority (e.g., restricted antibiotics, expensive biologics) - Authority checked via HPOS / PBS online

Why it matters: - Without authority: certain prescriptions cannot be dispensed - Patient access to subsidised medicines delayed or denied - Regulatory requirement for certain high-cost or restricted medicines
Evidence it exists: - HPOS PBS Authority list shows prescriber’s authorizations - TGA Prescriber Registration system shows authorities
Renewal / maintenance: - Authorities are ongoing (no formal renewal) - May require additional training for some authorities (e.g., high-cost biologics)
What goes wrong if neglected: - Prescriber cannot prescribe restricted medicines - Patient access to necessary medicines delayed - Clinical workflow interrupted

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each prescriber: - [ ] Log in to PBS online (HPOS) and review available authorizations - [ ] Identify any high-use medicines in practice requiring authority - [ ] If authority needed: apply via HPOS / TGA Prescriber Registration
DVA Provider Approval (if applicable)
What it is: - Department of Veterans’ Affairs provider registration - Required if practice treats DVA card holders (veterans, war widows/widowers)
Why it matters: - Enables DVA billing (separate from Medicare) - DVA patient access to subsidised GP care
Scope: - Can practice bulk-bill DVA patients? - Can claim service fees from DVA? - Which DVA card types accepted?
Evidence it exists: - DVA provider registration letter - DVA provider number
Renewal / maintenance: - Annual review by DVA - Must notify DVA if details change (address, services offered, etc.)

What goes wrong if neglected: - DVA patients cannot use practice (must attend different provider) - Lost revenue (DVA subsidises care; patients often bulk-billed) - Practice reputation (not accessible to all patient groups)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] Determine if practice has DVA patients (review recent billing) - [ ] If yes: DVA provider registration obtained & current - [ ] DVA provider number recorded & available to billing staff
WorkCover Provider Approval (Victoria)
What it is: - Registration as approved WorkCover provider - Required if practice treats WorkCover patients (work injuries)
Why it matters: - Enables WorkCover billing (separate from Medicare) - Access to workers compensation patients
Evidence it exists: - WorkCover provider registration letter - WorkCover provider number
Renewal / maintenance: - Annual; WorkCover sends renewal notice - Must maintain professional indemnity insurance
What goes wrong if neglected: - Cannot treat WorkCover patients (must refer elsewhere) - Lost revenue (WorkCover commonly provides 2–5 patients per week in many practices)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - [ ] Review recent patient list for WorkCover attendances - [ ] If yes: WorkCover provider registration obtained & current - [ ] WorkCover provider number recorded
Professional Indemnity Insurance
What it is: - Mandatory insurance for clinical practice - Covers negligence claims, patient harm claims, complaints investigations
Why it matters: - Legal requirement to practice - Protects personal & business assets - Covers legal defense costs & damages awards

Coverage elements: - Clinical negligence (wrong diagnosis, treatment error, adverse outcome) - Claims-made basis (covers claims made during policy period, not necessarily when care given) - Tail cover (optional; covers claims after retirement)
Evidence it exists: - Insurance certificate (proof of currency) - Policy document (terms, limits, exclusions) - Premium payment receipts

Typical cost (Victoria):
Source: Expert knowledge / External verification needed - Solo GP: AUD $3,000–$6,000/year - GP in clinic: AUD $2,000–$4,000/year (shared clinic PII cheaper) - Nurse: AUD $500–$1,500/year (often included in clinic PII)
Renewal / maintenance: - Annual; insurer sends renewal notice - Premium may increase with claims history - Coverage limits should be reviewed annually (adequate for practice size?)

What goes wrong if neglected: - Practitioner uninsured; personal assets at risk - Cannot practice legally (professional colleges require evidence of insurance) - Claim comes in with no insurance (catastrophic)

Confirmed from docs / needs verification: - For each clinician: - [ ] Insurance certificate obtained & reviewed (valid until date) - [ ] Coverage limits adequate (recommend minimum AUD $5M per claim) - [ ] Renewal date noted; calendar reminder set - [ ] For contractors: proof of insurance sighted (not clinic’s responsibility, but verify)
8.3 FULL COMPLIANCE CHECKLIST
Clinic/Business Level

Registration & Identifiers: - [ ] ABN obtained & verified (abn.business.gov.au) - [ ] ACN (if company) obtained & verified (asic.gov.au) - [ ] Medicare location registration confirmed (can claim Medicare) - [ ] HPI-O obtained (Services Australia / HPOS) - [ ] PRODA organisation account active (staff can submit claims) - [ ] NASH certificate installed & valid (expiry date noted) - [ ] My Health Record registration confirmed & policy written
Government Funding & Incentives: - [ ] PIP registration confirmed (receiving quarterly payments) - [ ] WIP registration confirmed (if eligible; receiving grants)
Accreditation: - [ ] Accreditation certificate current (within 3 years) - [ ] Accreditation audit report reviewed; corrective actions completed - [ ] Next accreditation renewal scheduled

Insurance: - [ ] Professional indemnity insurance (clinic PII) current & adequate coverage - [ ] General liability insurance current - [ ] Management liability insurance current - [ ] WorkCover certificate of currency valid - [ ] Cyber liability insurance current

Compliance Documentation: - [ ] Privacy policy written & communicated (OPAL Act / Privacy Act compliant) - [ ] Security policy written (My Health Record, data protection) - [ ] WHS/SafeWork policy written & reviewed annually - [ ] Infection control policy written & reviewed annually - [ ] Vaccination policy written (staff & patient) - [ ] Cold chain policy (vaccine storage) - [ ] Code of conduct written & staff signed - [ ] Anti-bullying & harassment policy written - [ ] Complaints procedure written - [ ] Incident reporting procedure written

Practitioner Level (Per GP, Nurse, Clinician)

Special Qualifications (if applicable): - [ ] DVA provider status (if treating DVA patients) - [ ] WorkCover provider status (if treating work injury patients) - [ ] GP training / fellowship recognised (RACGP, AGPAL, etc.)
Insurance: - [ ] Professional indemnity insurance current (own or covered by clinic PII) - [ ] Insurance certificate provided to clinic & filed

Credentialing File: - [ ] AHPRA certificate (photocopy) – signed original verification - [ ] Employment/contractor contract (signed) - [ ] References (2–3, verified) - [ ] Police check (if required) - [ ] Immunisation records (if clinical staff) – current - [ ] CPR certification (current; annual renewal) - [ ] Training records (privacy, infection control, PRODA, My Health Record, etc.) - [ ] Signed code of conduct & confidentiality agreement - [ ] Signed performance review (annual)
IT & Systems
