DREAMCLINIC GP ACQUISITION MANUAL
Parts 9–12: Insurance, Suppliers, Documents, and Retrieval Systems
PART 9 — INSURANCE AND RISK MAP
9.1 Insurance Cover Requirements and Checklist
9.1.1 Public Liability Insurance

What It Covers: - Third-party bodily injury or property damage occurring at the clinic - Patient sues clinic for injury (e.g., slip on wet floor, allergic reaction to vaccine) - Visitor injury (contractor, supplier, family member) - Damage to building/fixtures caused by clinic operations - Legal defence costs - Excludes: gross negligence, intentional acts, professional negligence (covered under PI)
Typical Cover: - $10–20M aggregate (most GPs carry $10M minimum, $20M preferred) - Excess typically $500–$2,500 per claim - Annual premium: $800–$2,500 depending on claims history and clinic size

Who Should Hold It: - Clinic entity (Pty Ltd or partnership) must be named insured - If subletting rooms, pathology may hold separate cover for their area - Individual GPs do NOT hold clinic PL cover (clinic entity does)

Evidence Document Needed: - Certificate of Currency (CofC) showing: - Clinic name and address - Policy number - Effective dates (from/to) - Coverage limits ($10M, $20M) - Excess amount - Insurer name and contact - Original policy document (kept with insurance file) - Claims history declaration (completed during quote process)

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually (often in calendar months: Jan, Feb, Mar) - Insurance broker should send renewal 30–45 days before expiry - Review claims history each renewal (impacts premium) - If clinic has made claims, expect premium increase - Shop around every 2–3 years; brokers often offer discounts for first-year new customers - Document any changes to clinic (staff, procedures, seating capacity) that affect underwriting

Operational Risk If Missing: - CRITICAL: Uninsured exposure. Single patient injury claim could bankrupt clinic. - Unable to lease space (landlord typically requires evidence of PL) - Unable to obtain professional indemnity (insurer will ask for PL proof) - Professional bodies (AMA, RACGP) require PL as condition of membership - Reputational damage if patient discovers clinic uninsured
9.1.2 Professional Indemnity Insurance (Clinic-Level)

What It Covers: - Clinic’s liability for professional negligence, misdiagnosis, incorrect treatment - Example: patient sues because GP missed cancer diagnosis - Patient privacy breach (confidentiality violation) - Breach of duty of care - Legal defence and settlement costs - Excludes: GP’s personal PI policy (see below), intentional misconduct

Clinic-Level vs. Individual GP PI: - Clinic-level PI: covers clinic entity and all GPs working at clinic (provided they are employees or approved contractors) - Individual GP PI: each GP holds personal cover (typically required by indemnity insurers) - Both are necessary; they work together

Typical Cover: - $5–10M cover per claim, $10–20M aggregate (clinic-level) - Individual GP PI: $1–5M (employer will specify minimum) - Excess: $500–$5,000 (clinic-level) - Clinic premium: $3,000–$8,000 p.a. (depends on claims history, practitioner numbers, specialties)

Who Should Hold It: - Clinic entity (Pty Ltd or partnership) - Certificate of Currency must list clinic, all employed GPs, and any contractor GPs - Each contractor GP must show their personal PI certificate on file

Evidence Document Needed: - Clinic-level Certificate of Currency (as for PL) - Individual GP PI certificates (one per GP, must be current) - Policy document (clinic-level) showing covered practitioners and exclusions - Underwriting questionnaire (completed at inception; must be honest about claims history) - GP Indemnity Insurance (GPII) or similar provider documentation

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually; broker should alert 30–45 days before - Clinic must update insurer if: - New GP joins clinic - GP leaves - Claims are made (must notify even if claim is small) - Clinic changes address - Previous claims history will affect renewal premium - If no claims for 3+ years, may qualify for discount (“claims-free reduction”) - Individual GPs’ PI status must be verified at renewal (cannot renew clinic PI without confirmed GP coverage)

Operational Risk If Missing: - CRITICAL: Single patient claim could destroy clinic finances and reputation - Professional bodies require PI as condition of practising - Unable to join accreditation schemes (RACGP Blue Book, etc.) - Patients increasingly ask to see PI proof before treatment - Lender will not provide finance without PI evidence - Reputational damage; signals negligence concern
9.1.3 Business Insurance / Business Pack

What It Covers (Typical Bundle): - Loss of business income if clinic forced to close (e.g., fire, flood) - Reimbursement of lost revenue during shutdown period - Covers fixed costs: rent, salaries, utilities - Duration of cover: typically 3–12 months depending on contract

Alternative Coverage: - Some policies bundle: property damage + loss of profits + public liability - Standalone “all-risk” policies for non-traditional clinics - Small business packages often cheaper than separate policies

Typical Cover: - Income indemnity: $10,000–$30,000/month (depends on clinic turnover) - Waiting period: 7–14 days (clinic cannot claim for first week of closure) - Duration: 12–24 months from start of claim - Excess: $500–$2,500 - Premium: $1,000–$4,000 p.a.
Who Should Hold It: - Clinic entity (not individual GPs) - Critical if clinic is sole trading location (loss of premises = loss of income)

Evidence Document Needed: - Certificate of Currency - Policy document showing coverage period - Last 2 years financial statements (to establish legitimate income claim) - Policy schedule with coverage limits and excess

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually - If clinic revenue increases >10%, must notify insurer (may affect premium) - Review waiting period and cover duration annually - If clinic has not moved/expanded, renewal should be straightforward

Operational Risk If Missing: - If fire/flood forces closure, clinic has no income for 3–6 months while rebuilding - Staff must still be paid (or retrenched, creating legal risk) - Lease rent still accrues (landlord not forgiving) - Clinic may never reopen if unable to afford interim costs - Less critical than PL/PI but still important
9.1.4 Building and Contents Insurance (If Clinic Owns or Lease Requires)

What It Covers: - Building structure damage (fire, flood, storm, vandalism) - Contents: medical equipment, computers, furniture - Fixtures and fittings (if clinic-owned) - Excludes: damage from faulty maintenance, wear and tear

Ownership Scenarios: - Clinic owns building: clinic must hold full building + contents cover - Clinic leases: lease usually requires clinic to hold contents cover; landlord holds building cover (verify in lease) - Clinic subleases room: pathology or other tenant may hold building cover; clinic holds contents for own equipment

Typical Cover: - Building: $500,000–$2,000,000 (depends on premises size and location) - Contents: $100,000–$500,000 (medical equipment, computers, furniture) - Excess: $500–$2,500 - Premium: $2,000–$8,000 p.a. (combined)
Who Should Hold It: - Building: clinic (if owner) or landlord (if leased) - Contents: clinic (always) - If clinic owns building, both must be clinic’s responsibility

Evidence Document Needed: - Certificate of Currency (contents) - Full policy document - Building inventory/asset register (list of items insured, with values) - Proof of building ownership (if clinic owns) or copy of lease (showing insurance requirement) - Previous claims history

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually - If clinic purchases major equipment (diagnostic machine, etc.), notify insurer immediately - If renovations increase building value, update cover - Post-renovation, get building revaluation (may trigger premium increase) - Review inventory annually to ensure all items are covered

Operational Risk If Missing: - If fire/flood destroys equipment and furniture, clinic cannot reopen without capital injection - If clinic owns building and it’s damaged, lender may call mortgage (if mortgaged) - Landlord may take legal action if lease required building cover and clinic failed to obtain it - Patient records may be destroyed (compliance risk) - Less critical than PL/PI but serious impact on continuity
9.1.5 Workers Compensation / WorkSafe Victoria (Mandatory)

What It Covers: - Employee injury or illness arising from work - Medical expenses, rehabilitation costs - Wage replacement (up to 100% of pre-injury wage) - Death benefits (to dependents) - Excludes: intentional self-harm, gross misconduct

Mandatory Coverage: - If clinic has ANY employees, WorkSafe coverage is MANDATORY by law (WorkSafe Victoria Act 1958) - Applies to: GPs (if employees), nurses, admin staff, reception - Does NOT apply to: independent contractors (separate arrangement required) - Failure to hold cover: fines up to $300,000, legal liability for uninsured claims

Premium Calculation (WorkSafe Victoria): - Based on: payroll amount + claims history + industry classification - Industry classification for general practice: typically “GP services” (mid-risk) - Premium rate: approximately 0.5–2.5% of payroll (varies by history) - Example: clinic with $500,000 payroll and 0.8% rate = $4,000 p.a. premium

Who Should Hold It: - Clinic entity (cannot be held by individual GP) - Clinic must register with WorkSafe as employer - Can be sourced through: WorkSafe directly, private insurer (Allianz, QBE, etc.), or broker

Evidence Document Needed: - WorkSafe Certificate of Currency (CofC) showing: - Clinic name, ABN, address - Policy number - Effective dates - Employer classification - Premium amount - WorkSafe registration certificate (proving clinic registered as employer) - Payroll records (to verify premium calculation) - Claims history statement

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually (typically Sept–June financial year for WorkSafe) - Premium recalculated based on updated payroll - If clinic makes claim, premium may increase next year - If clinic has claims-free record, may qualify for discount (“good history discount”) - Must update payroll estimate if hiring additional staff mid-year (may adjust premium)

Operational Risk If Missing: - LEGAL BREACH: operating without WorkSafe cover is illegal - WorkSafe can audit and issue compliance notice (cost $2,000–$10,000+ to rectify) - If uninsured employee injured, clinic has unlimited personal liability - Employee can sue clinic for full damages (medical costs + lost wages + pain/suffering) - Cannot operate clinic if discovered without cover - Professional bodies may cancel membership/accreditation
9.1.6 Cyber Insurance (Increasingly Essential for Healthcare)

What It Covers: - Data breach (ransomware, hacking, malware) - Privacy breach notification costs (legal fees, customer notification, credit monitoring) - Loss of electronic records / data recovery - Business interruption due to cyber attack - Extortion / ransom demands (may include ransom payment, depending on policy) - Third-party liability (e.g., clinic data breach affects patients) - Does NOT cover: user error, credential compromise without breach

Why It Matters for GP Clinics: - Electronic health records (EHR) contain sensitive patient data: names, DOB, medical history, contact details - Data breaches increasingly targeted at healthcare (ransomware gangs) - GDPR and Australian Privacy Act require notification of breaches (costs $5,000–$50,000+) - Patient records loss could force clinic closure - Reputation damage if breach disclosed

Typical Cover: - Data breach response: $50,000–$500,000 - Privacy liability: $500,000–$2,000,000 - Business interruption: $10,000–$50,000/month - Cyber extortion: $100,000–$1,000,000 - Excess: $500–$5,000 - Premium: $1,500–$5,000 p.a. (depends on clinic size, security practices)
Who Should Hold It: - Clinic entity (not individual GPs) - Must be in clinic’s name, listing all staff with access to patient data

Evidence Document Needed: - Certificate of Currency - Policy document showing coverage limits - Underwriting questionnaire (security practices, backup systems, staff training) - Proof of cyber security measures (firewalls, antivirus, backup procedures)

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually - Must disclose any security incidents or data loss events (even if not reportable) - Insurer may ask for proof of security updates (if systems over 3 years old) - If clinic adopts new EHR system, notify insurer (may affect premium) - Premium may increase if security practices not maintained

Operational Risk If Missing: - Ransomware attack could force clinic closure for weeks - Patient data breach could cost $50,000–$500,000 in notification + remediation - Reputation damage, patient loss, potential regulatory investigation - Critical for any clinic storing patient data electronically (essentially all modern clinics)
9.1.7 Management Liability and Directors & Officers (D&O) Insurance (If Company Structure)

What It Covers: - Directors’ and officers’ personal liability - Employment practices liability (wrongful termination, discrimination, harassment claims) - Statutory liability (Health & Safety breaches, privacy law breaches) - Crime insurance (employee theft, fraud) - Crisis management (reputation repair, legal advice)

Applies If: - Clinic is Pty Ltd or similar company (not if sole trader or partnership) - Directors exposed to personal liability under Corporations Act, Health & Safety Act - Staff employed (employment practices claims possible)
Typical Cover: - D&O limit: $500,000–$2,000,000 - Employment practices: $500,000–$1,000,000 - Crime/fraud: $100,000–$500,000 - Excess: $500–$5,000 - Premium: $2,000–$5,000 p.a.
Who Should Hold It: - Clinic Pty Ltd (entity, not individual directors) - Certificate should list all directors
Evidence Document Needed: - Certificate of Currency - Policy document - Company constitution / shareholders agreement - Details of all directors (names, roles) - Claims history

Renewal Considerations: - Renews annually - If director changes, notify insurer - If company structure changes (e.g., new shareholder), may need fresh underwriting - Annual directors’ meetings should address D&O risk (shows due diligence)

Operational Risk If Missing: - Director personally liable for employment claims (e.g., wrongful dismissal) up to full claim amount - No protection against statutory liability fines - If clinic embezzles or staff theft occurs, no crime cover - Less critical than PL/PI/WorkSafe but important for director protection
9.1.8 Glass, Equipment, and Business Interruption (Specialist Add-Ons)
Glass Insurance: - Covers glass door/window breakage at clinic - Excess typically $200–$500 - Often bundled with property/contents - Not usually standalone (check main contents policy)

Equipment Breakdown: - Covers medical equipment failure (ECG machine, blood pressure monitors, autoclave) - Covers repair/replacement costs - May include emergency repair callout costs - Premium: $500–$2,000 p.a. (depends on equipment value) - Excess: $500–$2,000 - Often optional add-on to contents policy

Business Interruption (Advanced): - Extends loss-of-income cover beyond standard policy - Covers additional costs during shutdown (e.g., temporary premises rental, staff retraining) - Premium: $500–$2,000 p.a. - Excess: $1,000–$5,000
Who Should Hold It: - Clinic entity - Glass/equipment add-ons typically part of contents policy

Evidence Document Needed: - Certificate of Currency (showing add-ons listed) - Equipment register (list of items covered with serial numbers and values) - Maintenance records (insurers may ask for proof of regular servicing)

Operational Risk If Missing: - Equipment breakdown could force temporary closure (if critical device fails) - May be relatively minor risk if equipment is standard/replaceable quickly - Less critical than core PL/PI/WorkSafe
9.1.9 Locum Insurance (If Clinic Uses Locum GPs)
What It Covers: - Locum GP PI if locum is contractor (not employee) - Clinic liability for locum’s actions (if locum on clinic’s insurance) - Locum sickness benefit (if locum has own cover)

Locum Scenarios: 1. Locum employed by clinic: clinic’s PI and WorkSafe covers locum 2. Locum contractor with own PI: locum’s PI covers their work; clinic remains liable (should require copy of locum’s PI certificate) 3. Locum via agency: agency holds PI; clinic may hold secondary liability cover

Typical Locum PI Arrangements: - Medical Defence Organisation (MDO) offers locum PI (standalone) - Medishield offers locum cover - RACGP group schemes - Cost: $300–$800 p.a. for locum with own PI - Clinic should require proof of coverage
Who Should Hold It: - If locum employee: clinic’s main PI covers them (listed on CofC) - If locum contractor: locum should hold own PI

Evidence Document Needed: - Locum’s PI certificate (if contractor) - Locum engagement contract (stating who holds what insurance) - Clinic’s updated PI certificate (if locum is employee/listed on clinic cover)

Renewal Considerations: - If using locums regularly, ensure they have active PI - Request annual proof of locum PI (copy of CofC) - If locum’s PI lapses, clinic cannot use them (breach of professional standards) - If clinic employs locum long-term, move to clinic payroll/WorkSafe (cheaper than managing individual PI)

Operational Risk If Missing: - If locum contractor uninsured and makes error, clinic is liable - Professional bodies may suspend clinic if locums uninsured - Patient harm could result in uninsured claim against clinic - Less critical if locums are rare; critical if clinic relies on locums
9.2 Three Top-10 Risk Registers
9.2.1 Top 10 Clinic Operational Risks

rows: Key GP departure (principal retires, moves interstate)
Immediate loss of billing revenue, patient loss, staff morale drop, clinic may not be viable
Succession plan, gradual handover (6+ months), contract terms with notice period, identify/train replacement early
Billing system failure (PRODA account locked, Medicare audit, claiming errors)
Revenue drop 20–50%, cash flow crisis within 2 weeks, staff unable to process claims
Monthly audit of billing, backup claiming methods, relationship with practice manager, regular PRODA checks
Missed appointments, patient anger, backlogs, clinic reputation damaged, GPs idle
Cross-train staff, maintain 1.5× coverage (if need 1 FTE, hire 1.5), clear handover procedures, temp agency contact
Cold chain breach (vaccine storage failure, temperature logger not monitored)
Vaccines spoiled, unusable stock $2,000–$5,000, potential patient harm if used, ATAGI investigation
Daily temperature checks (documented), backup freezer, 3-month temperature logs, monthly inventory audit
IT system failure (EHR down, server crash, internet outage)
Unable to access patient records for 4–8 hours, clinic grinds to halt, patient safety risk
Cloud-based backup EHR, redundant internet (main + 4G), offline patient list, IT vendor with 1-hour SLA
Key person dependency (one admin/nurse indispensable; if leave, clinic struggles)
Staff burnout, knowledge loss, inability to cover absences, high turnover
Cross-train all roles, document procedures, rotate responsibilities, no single-person sign-off on critical tasks
Cash flow gap (large supplier invoice due but cash account has $2,000)
Unable to pay staff, suppliers demand payment, clinic may need emergency finance
Monthly cash flow forecast, maintain 4–8 weeks operating expense buffer, pre-negotiated line of credit
Accreditation lapse (RACGP Blue Book expires unnoticed, QIP audit overdue)
Cannot bill Medicare, lose patients, regulatory non-compliance, fines
Calendar reminders (Excel file), practice manager responsibility, annual accreditation review meeting
Lease issues (landlord serves notice, lease termination dispute, rent rise)
Forced relocation, loss of clinic assets/fit-out, revenue loss during move
Know lease terms (review quarterly), maintain landlord relationship, document any disputes in writing, lease legal review
Complaint escalation (patient complaint to Health Complaints Commissioner, media attention)
Regulatory investigation, reputation damage, staff morale drop, potential legal claim
Complaint handling policy, document all incidents, respond promptly to complaints, external advisor on retainer

text: 9.2.2 Top 10 Legal/Compliance Risks

rows: Sham contracting (GP labeled “contractor” but treated as employee; ATO challenge)
Back-pay of super (9.5% × 5 years), fines, reputational damage
Proper contractor agreement, genuine discretion in working, ABN required, separate tax files, no clinic control over hours
Privacy breach (patient data accessed without consent, email sent to wrong person, USB lost)
Notification costs $10,000–$50,000, reputation damage, regulatory investigation by Privacy Commissioner
Privacy policy, staff training annually, encryption, secure email, breach response plan, cyber insurance
Medicare non-compliance (over-claiming, claiming after patient ineligible, bundling breaches)
Audit investigation, back-payment of claims + penalties (100%–200%), temporary claiming suspension
Regular audit (monthly), AHPRA/Medicare updates, clear clinical policies, billing software checks, external auditor
Workplace injury (staff injured and workers comp claim inadequate, uninsured injury)
Clinic liable for full damages (medical + wages + pain/suffering), potential WorkSafe prosecution
Maintain WorkSafe cover, safety culture, hazard identification, incident reporting, ergonomic assessment
Unfair dismissal claim (staff member dismissed without process, lack of documented warnings)
Unfair dismissal claim: $10,000–$50,000 compensation, legal costs, staff morale impact
Fair process, documented warnings, termination letter, legal advice before dismissal, contracts clear on termination
Discrimination claim (dismissal based on disability, gender, age; recruitment bias documented)
Legal claim $20,000–$100,000+, regulatory investigation, reputational damage
Clear non-discriminatory policies, recruitment process documented, fair performance management, diversity training
Accreditation failure (RACGP audit finds non-compliance, accreditation cancelled)
Cannot bill Medicare, clinic closure, revenue loss, fines
Annual accreditation review, internal audit program, QIP documentation, staff training on standards
Records management failure (patient records destroyed/lost, retention not documented, GDPR-style request unfulfilled)
Regulatory breach, fines, patient claims, inability to defend complaints
Document retention policy (7 years minimum), secure storage, audit trail, access logs, backups
Consent issues (treatment given without informed consent, minor treated without guardian)
Patient claim for battery/negligence, regulatory investigation
Consent forms, documentation of discussion, capacity assessment (for adults), guardian consent for minors
Mandatory reporting failures (GP aware of child abuse, elder abuse, or violence but did not report)
Regulatory investigation, criminal charge possible, fines up to $100,000+, professional deregistration
Annual training on mandatory reporting laws, clear reporting procedures, external contacts (child protection, police), documentation

text: 9.2.3 Top 10 Document/Control Failures (Would Hurt During Operation or Resale)

rows: Unclear pay, hours, notice period, IP ownership; GP leaves with patient list
Due diligence fails, buyer hesitates, purchase price reduced 10–20%
Documented contract for each GP (signed), state pay/hours/super, restraint clause (12 months post-departure)
Undocumented GP arrangements (verbal agreement on rate, hours, on-call duties)
Disputes when GP leaves, unclear who owns patient list, no restraint
Buyer cannot verify income/staffing stability, purchase valuation discounted
Written offer letter, contract amendments if terms change, annual contract review
Staff unsure of protocols (reception, clinical, complaints), inconsistent service
Operational chaos on day one post-acquisition, staff turnover
Written manual: HR policies, clinical protocols, reception procedures, IT protocols, updated annually
Poor financial records (invoices not filed, bank reconciliation not done, receipts lost)
Unable to do tax return, cash flow forecasting impossible, accounting fees spike
Auditor/accountant cannot verify income, tax position unknown, buyer cannot value clinic
Monthly accounting (invoices, receipts, bank reconciliation), accountant preparation of quarterly reports
No asset register (equipment, software licenses, furniture not documented; values unknown)
Unable to claim depreciation, insurance claims delayed, equipment maintenance missed
Buyer cannot verify what assets included in sale, valuation inaccurate
Spreadsheet register: equipment name, purchase date, cost, serial number, location, maintenance schedule
Incomplete staff files (no contracts, no training records, no references, work rights not verified)
Cannot rehire staff post-acquisition, potential workplace claims, unpaid super disputes
Buyer liable for staff issues, cannot verify staffing legitimacy
File per staff member: contract, offer letter, references, ABN/tax file number, super fund details, training records
No incident register (complaints, near-misses, adverse events not documented)
Unable to identify systemic issues, repeat incidents, no defense if complaint escalates
Buyer assumes hidden liability, expects claims to emerge
Spreadsheet register: date, type (complaint/incident/near-miss), parties involved, summary, action taken, outcome
Undocumented lease terms (lease in landlord’s filing, not clinic’s; key terms unknown)
Renewal negotiation fails (no copy to reference), surprise rent increase, termination clause missed
Buyer cannot verify occupancy costs, lease stability unknown, lease may be non-transferable
Copy of lease to clinic file, lease abstract (key terms summary), landlord contact, renewal dates noted in calendar
Missing insurance certificates (PI, PL policies expired but not noticed, no proof of coverage)
Clinic operating without required insurance, patient harm = unlimited liability
Buyer refuses to complete purchase if insurance gaps discovered
Checklist: PL, PI, WorkSafe, contents (if applicable), cyber. Broker to send renewal reminders 30 days prior.
No succession/handover documentation (if principal retiring, no transition plan, patient list undocumented)
Patient loss, revenue cliff, buyer inherits chaos, staff confusion
Purchase collapses if successor unclear, clinic valuation plummets, buyer pulls out
Documented plan: 6–12 month handover, principal mentoring successor, patient letters confirming continuity, new GP meet-and-greet events

text: 9.3 Insurance Broker and Renewal Management
9.3.1 Selecting an Insurance Broker
Specialist healthcare brokers (e.g., Gallagher, Aon, Willis Towers Watson healthcare divisions)
Must have experience with GP clinics (not just general SME insurance)
Should hold professional indemnity themselves (shows credibility)
Ask for references from 3–5 existing GP clinic clients
9.3.2 Broker Responsibilities (Should Be in Engagement Letter)
Annual renewal reminders (30–45 days before expiry)
Claims notification and support
Annual premium review and market shopping
Update insurer if clinic circumstances change
Quarterly certificate of currency verification
Annual risk review meeting
9.3.3 Insurance Spreadsheet Tracker (Sample Structure)
PART 10 — PATHOLOGY, SUPPLIERS, AND EXTERNAL PARTNERS
10.1 Pathology Arrangement
10.1.1 Overview of Major Victorian Pathology Providers

Dorevitch Pathology - Largest independent pathology in Victoria - Phlebotomists on-site at most clinics (or clinic staff collect) - Results interface: MEDI3, BEST, other EHR systems - Room rental: typically $500–$1,500/month or commission-based - Service areas: inner Melbourne preferred; outer suburbs slower - Contact: Regional Manager (clinic should have mobile/email)

Melbourne Pathology (Sonic Healthcare) - Large national chain, Sonic Healthcare-owned - Results integration with various EHRs - Room rental or commission (5–8% of collections) - Phlebotomy staff or clinic staff supported - Service areas: metro and regional Victoria - Contact: Regional Account Manager

Australian Clinical Labs (ACL) - Growing private provider - Competitive pricing, modern automation - Results integration available - Room rental or commission - Expanding metro presence - Contact: Practice Manager/Sales
Other Pathology Options - Darbyshire Pathology (smaller, inner metro) - IMVS PathCentre (SA-based but some VIC presence) - Hospital pathology services (limited to public GPs)
10.1.2 Typical Pathology Service Agreement Structure
What Should Be In Writing:
Pathology Arrangement Type
On-site phlebotomist (pathology provides staff)
Clinic staff collect (pathology provides training, supplies)
Off-site collection (patients go to pathology lab)
Financial Terms
Option A: Room rental ($500–$1,500/month, clinic gets 100% of rebates)
Option B: Commission-based (clinic gets 50–70% of rebate, pathology gets 30–50%)
Option C: Hybrid (base rental + commission for volume above threshold)
Payment terms: monthly invoice, due on invoice date or 14 days
Service Levels
Turnaround time for results (24–48 hours typical)
Availability of urgent/same-day results
Phlebotomy hours (8 am–5 pm typical, extended hours available)
Holiday/public holiday cover
Who covers pathology if phlebotomist sick (temporary staff or clinic closes)
Equipment and Supplies
Specimen collection containers: who provides and pays
Labels and request forms: who provides
Storage equipment (fridge, freezer): who provides, who pays power/maintenance
Sharps disposal bins: cost responsibility
Telephone/fax: who pays
Results Integration
EHR system connection (API, HL7 interface)
Who pays for interface development/support (typically clinic pays first-time, pathology pays ongoing)
Contingency if interface fails (fax/email backup)
Turnaround time for results availability in EHR
Quality and Governance
Pathology quality assurance (NATA accreditation, external QA)
Clinic responsibility for correct specimen labeling/collection
What happens if specimen rejected (re-collection, refund)
Confidentiality/privacy obligations (specimens, patient data)
Liability
Pathology liable for specimen transport/loss
Clinic liable for specimen collection errors
Insurance (WorkSafe if phlebotomist injured on-site)
Professional indemnity (pathology carries for interpretation errors)
Term and Termination
Contract duration (typically 12–24 months)
Notice period to terminate (typically 30–90 days)
What happens to patient specimens if terminated
Migration of results history
Exclusions
Which tests not available through this provider
Referrals to other labs (GPs may order from others without restriction)
Private/direct-to-patient testing
10.1.3 Pathology Service Agreement Checklist

Before Signing: - [ ] Confirm pathology provider is NATA-accredited - [ ] Confirm EHR integration available for clinic’s system (ask for trial) - [ ] Get specimen collection training (in-house staff) - [ ] Review payment terms (rent or commission) against clinic projections - [ ] Confirm turnaround times and emergency availability - [ ] Review liability/insurance clauses - [ ] Confirm cold chain/storage requirements match clinic capacity - [ ] Get 3-year cost projection (budget planning) - [ ] Assign one staff member as pathology liaison (order issues, interface problems, training)

Operational Contacts: - Pathology phlebotomist (mobile/email) - Pathology results support (if results not received) - Pathology account manager (billing, service issues) - Pathology IT support (EHR interface issues)
10.1.4 Monitoring Pathology Performance

Monthly Review: - Results turnaround: Are 95% of results back within 24–48 hours? (Track a sample) - Error rate: How many specimens rejected or results incorrect? (Should be <1%) - Integration success: Are 95%+ of results auto-populating in EHR? (Check EHR logs) - Cost per collection: Is cost per rebate in line with contract? (Audit quarterly)

Quarterly Business Review (Recommended): - Meeting with pathology account manager - Review volume trends and revenue share - Discuss any service issues - Plan for seasonal volume (flu season, etc.) - Negotiate price if volume has changed significantly
Annual Review: - Full contract review (terms, pricing, notice periods) - Consider market alternatives (get quote from competitor) - Plan for renewal or renegotiation
10.2 Consumables Suppliers
10.2.1 Medical Supplies and PPE

Typical Suppliers in Victoria: - Bupa Health Medical Supplies (large, competitive pricing) - Lyell Medical (GP-focused, local service) - Healthe Care (online ordering, fast delivery) - Direct Supply Partners (bulk PPE, gloves, masks) - Medela/Various (procedural consumables)

What to Source: - Gloves (latex-free, examination) - Masks (surgical, N95) - Swabs, cotton buds, tissues - Alcohol hand sanitizer - Gauze, bandages, wound dressings - Sterile packs (suture, injection, dressing) - Thermometer covers - Urine sample bottles - Blood collection containers (if not via pathology) - Otoscope/ophthalmoscope specula - Needles, syringes

Supplier Agreement Essentials: - Pricing: per-unit cost or volume discounts - Delivery: 3–5 day standard, 1-day express available? - Minimum order: is there a minimum order value/quantity? - Payment terms: 14, 30, or COD? - Bulk storage: does clinic have space for discounted bulk orders? - Return policy: can over-ordered items be returned?

Cost Management: - Audit consumption monthly (usage vs. budget) - Buy bulk only if storage and cash flow allow - Rotate suppliers every 2 years (competitive quotes) - Consider clinic group buying (if multi-clinic owner)
Key Contact per Supplier: - Account manager name, phone, email - Order phone line or online system - Emergency out-of-hours contact
10.2.2 Vaccine Suppliers

National Immunisation Program (NIP) Vaccines: - Supplied FREE by Victoria State Government (via Medicare/Pharmacy Guild) - Clinic registers with Immunise Victoria to receive NIP vaccines - Delivery: fortnightly or monthly distribution - No cost to clinic - Cold chain requirements: clinic must maintain storage (2–8°C)

Private Vaccine Suppliers (For Non-NIP Vaccines): - ATAGI recommendations (RSV, pneumococcal for private patients, etc.) - Private vaccine suppliers: - Bupa Health Pharmacy - Pharmacare - Vyperion Healthcare - International travel clinics (Yellow Fever, etc.) - Cost: Private vaccines $20–$100 per dose depending on vaccine - Ordering: Usually online portal, email, or phone - Delivery: 2–7 days - Cold chain: Must maintain storage at 2–8°C (same as NIP)

Cold Chain Management: - Critical for vaccine efficacy and safety - Temperature logger: records temperature every 15–30 minutes - Backup freezer or ice pack system (if main fridge fails) - Daily temperature checks (documented log) - Monthly review of temperature logs (should never drop below 2°C or exceed 8°C) - If breach detected: contact supplier, ATAGI, and patients potentially affected - Incident report to AHPRA if patients given compromised vaccines

Vaccine Inventory Management: - Stock rotation (FIFO: first in, first out) - Expiry date tracking (many vaccines 12–24 month shelf life) - Usage forecasting (especially flu season: March–Sept build-up) - Reconciliation: monthly count vs. records (should match) - Batch tracking (in case of recall)

Supplier Agreements for Private Vaccines: - Pricing: per-dose cost - Returns: can unused vaccines be returned for credit? (typically 30-day window) - Payment terms: usually 30 days net - Delivery cost: free over $500? or per-order fee? - Emergency supply: can clinic get urgent delivery if stock runs out?
10.2.3 Medical Waste and Sharps Disposal

Regulated Medical Waste Categories: - Sharps (needles, lancets, scalpels) - Pathological waste (body tissues, blood-soaked swabs) - Infectious waste (contaminated gloves, dressings) - Pharmaceutical waste (expired medications) - Chemical waste (cleaning agents, disinfectants, if hazardous)

Major Victorian Medical Waste Providers: - Daniels Health (large, pickup service, compliance) - Stericycle (multinational, reliable, competitive) - Cleanaway Medical (local, SMS pickup available) - Veolia Healthcare (integrated waste solutions)

Typical Service Model: - Clinic provided with labeled sharps containers (red/yellow) - Weekly or fortnightly pickup (frequency depends on volume) - Full containers swapped for empty ones - Containers incinerated at licensed facility - Compliance certificate provided

Cost: - Per-pickup fee: $20–$50 (weekly or fortnightly) - Per-container fee: $5–$15 (for oversize or premium containers) - Monthly cost: $80–$200 depending on clinic size and volume - Setup fee: $0–$100 (initial container supply)

Service Agreement Essentials: - Pickup frequency: weekly/fortnightly/on-demand - Container provision: how many provided? replacement cost if damaged? - Spill kit: does provider supply biohazard spill kit? maintenance? - Compliance: does provider supply waste disposal certificates? - Emergency pickup: available if containers fill unexpectedly? - Contract: typically 12 months, 30-day notice to terminate

Operational Requirements: - Staff training on segregation (sharps, infectious, chemical — different containers) - Signage on sharps containers (biohazard symbol, no overfilling) - Storage: secure area, away from public, 2–3 days max storage (regulatory limit) - Incident reporting: if spill or injury with contaminated sharps

Compliance: - As per Victorian Health Department and NOHSC guidelines - Clinic must maintain waste disposal contracts and certificates - Audit trail: document each pickup date and container count - Annual compliance audit recommended
Key Contacts: - Waste provider account manager - Emergency pickup phone number - Compliance/certificate request process
10.2.4 Linen and Cleaning Services
Linen (Examination Couch, Towels, etc.):
Major Providers: - Alsco Pty Ltd (large national, couch covers, towels, uniforms) - Rentokil Initial (linen and cleaning combined) - Local commercial laundries (smaller, less expensive)

Service Model: - Clinic provided with clean linen (couch covers, towels, hand towels) - Weekly or twice-weekly exchange (dirty linen collected, clean provided) - Cost: $30–$80/week depending on volume and linen type

Service Agreement Essentials: - Exchange frequency and day - Linen inventory (couch covers, towels, hand towels — quantity) - Damage/staining policy (who pays if linen damaged?) - Emergency linen if delivery missed - Pricing: per-item or flat weekly fee?
Cleaning Services:

Typical Providers: - In-house staff (most common for GPs; cleaning manager or designated staff) - Contract cleaning service (3–5 times weekly, evening/early morning) - Hybrid: daily surface clean (in-house), deep clean weekly (contractor)

If Contracting Out Cleaning: - Cost: $600–$1,500/month (depending on clinic size, frequency) - Hours: typically 6–8 am or 5–7 pm (to avoid patient disruption) - Services included: floor, surfaces, toilets, medical waste management - Contact: cleaning supervisor or company - Standards: NOHSC cleaning guidelines, hospital-grade disinfectants for clinical areas

Service Agreement Essentials: - Frequency: daily, 3×/week, etc. - Hours: times when cleaning occurs - Scope: which areas cleaned, frequency of deep clean - Standards: medical-grade disinfectants, cleaning checklists - Quality monitoring: clinic to sign-off daily or weekly - Sick leave cover: what if regular cleaner absent? - Cost: per-hour, per-visit, or monthly flat fee - Contract term: 12 months, 30-day notice typical

Compliance: - Cleaning to meet health/safety standards (Australian Standards AS/NZS 4187: medical device cleaning) - Clinical area cleaning (exam room, nurse room) different standards than reception - Staff infection control training (bloodborne pathogens, etc.)
10.3 IT Vendor and Managed Services
10.3.1 Typical IT Setup for a GP Clinic

Core Systems: - EHR/Practice Management Software (Best Practice, MEDI3, ZedMed, etc.) — separate vendor usually - Workstations: 4–8 desktop/laptop computers (EHR access) - Printers/Scanners: 2–4 (patient document scanning, prescriptions, letters) - Server: on-premise or cloud-based (EHR data storage) - Network: Ethernet/WiFi, security-grade router/firewall - Backup: automated daily backup (cloud or external drive) - Phones: clinic phone system, integration with EHR (caller ID) - Cybersecurity: antivirus, firewall, intrusion detection
10.3.2 IT Vendor Service Agreement
What Should Be Included:
Support Services
Helpdesk availability: hours (8 am–6 pm weekdays typical, or 24/7 if available)
Response time: 1 hour for critical (EHR down), 4 hours for urgent, 24 hours for routine
Remote support: via TeamViewer or similar
On-site support: available? costs? travel time included?
Hardware Support
Workstation warranty: 3 years with next-business-day replacement
Server support: included in service or separate?
Network equipment: firmware updates, security patches
Printer/scanner support: included or separate?
Software Support
EHR software updates: included or separate?
Operating system patches/updates: included
Antivirus/security software: included
License management: tracking, renewal reminders
Backup and Disaster Recovery
Daily backup schedule (when, frequency)
Backup location: cloud provider? local? both?
Recovery time objective (RTO): how fast can clinic be back online? (typically 1–4 hours)
Recovery point objective (RPO): how much data loss acceptable? (typically <1 day)
Annual disaster recovery test (recommended)
Security and Compliance
Firewalls: configured to meet health/security standards
Intrusion detection: monitoring for suspicious activity
Encryption: data in transit (HTTPS) and at rest (if cloud)
Access controls: user account management, password policies
Compliance: GDPR-ready, Australian Privacy Act compliant
Onboarding and Training
Staff training: on clinic software, basic IT support
Onboarding: documentation of system setup, access credentials
Change management: notification of major software updates in advance
Escalation Process
If issue unresolved in 24 hours, escalate to senior tech/vendor
Emergency contact: out-of-hours number for critical issues
Complaint procedure: documented escalation path
10.3.3 Monitoring IT Vendor Performance

Quarterly Review: - Helpdesk response times (are SLAs being met?) - Number of unresolved tickets (should be <5% open after 7 days) - System uptime (should be >99% for production systems) - Security incidents (any breaches or suspicious activity?) - Backup success rate (100% overnight backups completed successfully?)

Key IT Contacts to Document: - Vendor helpdesk: phone number, email, ticket system - Account manager: name, mobile, email - On-call emergency contact (after-hours) - EHR software vendor support (separate from hardware vendor)
10.3.4 Cost Structure

Typical IT Support Costs (Monthly): - Managed services: $400–$1,200/month (covers helpdesk, updates, backup) - Hardware warranty: $100–$300/month (covers device replacement) - Cybersecurity add-on: $50–$200/month (threat monitoring, EDR) - Total typical clinic IT cost: $600–$1,500/month
One-Off Costs: - Network setup: $2,000–$5,000 (one-time) - EHR software setup/migration: $3,000–$10,000 (one-time) - Cybersecurity assessment: $1,000–$2,500 (annually)
10.4 Telephony and Internet
10.4.1 Internet Requirements

Bandwidth: - Minimum: 10 Mbps download, 2 Mbps upload (for small clinic, 1–3 GPs) - Recommended: 20+ Mbps (to handle multiple concurrent calls, video calls, EHR sync) - Upload critical: if clinic sends/receives large files (scans, images), need 5+ Mbps upload

Reliability: - 99.9% uptime required (less than 43 minutes/month downtime acceptable) - Must have backup connection (4G modem) if primary internet fails - NBN (National Broadband Network) if available in area (most reliable) - ADSL2+ as backup if NBN not available

Medical-Grade Requirements: - Encryption: HTTPS for all patient data transmission - Firewall: standard, protects against external threats - DDoS protection: recommended (cybersecurity insurance may require)

Major Providers in Victoria: - Telstra (largest coverage, most expensive) - TP/Vodafone (competitive pricing, good coverage) - NBN Co (if available: most reliable, $60–$120/month) - 4G providers: Telstra, Optus, Vodafone (backup only, usually $30–$80/month)
10.4.2 Telephony System

Clinic Phones: - Dedicated clinic phone line (landline from Telstra/TP, ~$20–$40/month) - VoIP system (cloud-based, more flexible, $30–$100/month) - Mobile for on-call (separate line for after-hours GP, ~$50–$100/month)

Integration: - EHR integration: caller ID displays in EHR (useful for answering calls) - Call recording: optional (if recorded, patients must be informed) - Voicemail/auto-attendant: “Press 1 for appointments, Press 2 for results,” etc. - Call waiting: multiple calls can be held

Service Agreement Essentials: - Number of lines: 1–3 typical - Cost: per-line or bundled with internet - Contract term: typically 12 months - Notice period: 30 days to change provider - Support: provider should have helpdesk for outages
10.4.3 Cost Structure

Typical Monthly Communications Cost (Internet + Phones): - Internet (NBN or DSL): $60–$120 - Primary phone line: $20–$40 - Additional lines: $15–$25 each - Mobile (if clinic-supplied): $40–$100 - VoIP system (if used): $30–$100 - Total typical: $150–$400/month
Redundancy Setup (Recommended): - Primary: NBN internet + Telstra landline - Backup: 4G modem (USB, portable) - Cost for backup: $30–$80/month (adds to total)
10.5 Website and Online Booking
10.5.1 Website Requirements

Essential Pages: - Home page (clinic info, hours, location map) - About page (GPs, nurses, credentials) - Services (bulk billing, after-hours, vaccinations, etc.) - Contact page (phone, email, address, contact form) - Appointments (online booking link or “call to book”) - Patient forms (new patient info, health history) - FAQ (privacy policy, billing, after-hours care)
Modern Expectation: - Mobile-responsive (works on phones/tablets) - Google Search visibility (SEO) - Accessibility: readable font, color contrast for vision-impaired
10.5.2 Online Booking Integration

Major Systems: - Telstra Health (integrates with EHR, common for GPs) - WebGP/ZedMed (built-in to practice software) - Acuity Scheduling (standalone, easier setup) - Calendly (simple, but requires manual EHR sync)

What to Check: - Real-time availability (links to EHR schedule) - Double-booking prevention - Patient cancellation/rescheduling (online, or phone call required?) - Reminder emails/SMS before appointment - Integration with EHR (appointment populates in system automatically) - Cost: usually $50–$300/month depending on features
10.5.3 Website and Hosting Costs

Domain: $15–$30/year (register with GoDaddy, Namecheap, etc.) Hosting: $100–$300/month (WordPress, Wix, or custom) Website Design: $2,000–$10,000 (one-time, if custom) SEO/Marketing: $200–$500/month (optional, for Google visibility) Online Booking Software: $50–$300/month
Hosting Should Include: - SSL certificate (HTTPS, secures patient data) - Daily backups - 99.9% uptime guarantee - Security scanning
10.5.4 Website and Privacy
Privacy policy: must state how patient data collected via website is handled
GDPR compliance (if EU patients) or Australian Privacy Act
No storing credit card data on website (use secure payment gateway like Stripe)
Contact forms: must include privacy statement
10.6 Finance and Accounting Providers
10.6.1 Bookkeeper

Role: - Daily/weekly invoicing (claims to Medicare) - Bank reconciliation - Accounts payable (paying suppliers) - Accounts receivable (if private billing or patient payment plans) - Monthly/quarterly reporting (P&L, balance sheet)

Typical Arrangement: - In-house bookkeeper: clinic employee, $40,000–$65,000/year - External bookkeeper: part-time contractor, $500–$1,500/month (5–10 hours/week) - Hybrid: part-time clinic staff + external contractor for tax/super
What to Look For: - Experience with medical practices - Knowledge of Medicare claiming - MYOB or QuickBooks proficiency - GST knowledge - Monthly P&L and cash flow reporting
10.6.2 BAS Agent (Business Activity Statement)
Role: - Prepares quarterly BAS (tax compliance) - Manages GST (goods/services tax, if registered) - Submits BAS to ATO - Manages tax payments and refunds - Deals with ATO queries
Typical Arrangement: - Accountant (full-service): $1,500–$3,000/year (includes BAS + tax return) - Standalone BAS agent: $400–$800/year (BAS only)
What to Verify: - Agent is registered with ATO (check online) - Knows healthcare/medical practice tax treatment - Quarterly submission on time (no ATO penalties)
10.6.3 Accountant / Financial Advisor

Role: - Annual financial statements (preparation) - Tax return (business + personal if owner) - Financial advice (profit maximization, structure optimization) - Audit support (if required) - Superannuation strategy - Business valuations (for sale/exit planning)
Typical Arrangement: - Fee: $2,000–$5,000/year (small clinic) - Or percentage of revenue: 0.5–1% (larger clinic) - Or hourly: $200–$400/hour (for ad-hoc advice)

What to Verify: - Accountant holds CPA or CA qualification - Experience with medical practices - Tax advisor (not just bookkeeper with accounting knowledge) - Familiar with Medicare taxation (rebates, private billing mix)
10.6.4 Financial Advisor / Strategic Planning

Role (If Clinic Owner Pursuing Growth/Exit): - Business valuation (for sale, loan, insurance) - Growth strategy (new locations, staff expansion) - Exit planning (when to sell, how to maximize sale price) - Financing options (bank loans, investment)
Typical Arrangement: - Fee: $2,000–$10,000 per project - Or retainer: $300–$800/month (ongoing advice)

When to Engage: - Planning acquisition (due diligence support) - Planning expansion (multi-clinic strategy) - Planning exit in next 3–5 years (value maximization) - Securing finance (bank needs advisor support)

11.2 File Naming Convention
11.2.1 Standard Format
YYYY-MM-DD_DocumentType_Party_ShortDescription_Version

Components: - YYYY-MM-DD: Date document created or signed (e.g., 2025-03-15) - DocumentType: Category (Contract, Certificate, Invoice, Policy, Register, Agreement, Checklist, Report) - Party: Entity involved (Clinic name, supplier, staff name, e.g., DreamClinic, Dorevitch, JSmith) - ShortDescription: Key detail (e.g., ServiceAgreement, MonthlyReport, EmploymentContract) - Version: V1, V2, Final, Draft (if multiple iterations exist)
11.2.2 File Naming Examples

Contracts: - 2025-03-15_Contract_DreamClinic_DorevitchPathology_ServiceAgreement_V1.pdf - 2024-06-01_Contract_DreamClinic_JSmith_EmploymentGP_Signed.pdf - 2025-01-10_Contract_DreamClinic_Landlord_LeaseAmendment_Final.pdf

Invoices: - 2025-03-15_Invoice_Dorevitch_MonthlyPathology_Payment.pdf - 2025-03-20_Invoice_Telstra_Internet_March2025.pdf - 2025-04-05_Invoice_Accountant_TaxReturnPreparation_2024.pdf
Policies: - 2025-01-01_Policy_DreamClinic_ConfidentiailtyAndPrivacy_V2.pdf - 2024-06-01_Policy_DreamClinic_ComplaintsHandling_V1.pdf - 2025-03-01_Policy_DreamClinic_ClinicalRiskManagement_V2.pdf

text: 11.3.2 Sample Master Index (First 10 Documents)
11.3.3 Master Index Access and Use

Where to Store Master Index: - Primary location: Shared drive root folder (easy access from anywhere) - File name: Master_Document_Index_DreamClinic.xlsx (version-dated) - Back up copy: Google Sheets (cloud backup, allows offline access)
Who Has Access: - Full edit access: Owner, Practice Manager - View-only access: Bookkeeper, Reception (for reference only) - No access: Patients, contractors (unless specifically shared)

Update Frequency: - Monthly: Add new documents to index (invoices, certificates) - Quarterly: Review expiry dates, flag documents needing renewal - Annually: Comprehensive audit (all documents current? anything missing?)

Filtering and Searching: - Use Excel/Google Sheets filters to search by: - Category (insurance, payroll, staff) - Criticality (High documents first) - Expiry Date (approaching renewal) - Entity (supplier, staff member) - Keywords (free-text search)
11.4 Storage Best Practices
11.4.1 Cloud Storage Platform Selection
Recommended Options for GP Clinic:
Microsoft SharePoint (part of Microsoft 365)
Pros: Integration with Office 365, versions control, granular permissions
Cons: Higher cost ($10–$20/user/month), steeper learning curve
Best for: Multi-clinic operations, advanced version control needs
Google Drive (part of Google Workspace)
Pros: Easy sharing, lower cost ($6–$14/user/month), intuitive interface
Cons: Less granular permissions than SharePoint, fewer advanced features
Best for: Single clinic, team of 5–10, budget-conscious
Dropbox Business
Pros: Excellent syncing, good security, file versioning
Cons: Higher cost ($15–$20/user/month for Business tier)
Best for: Mobile/remote staff, lots of file access from multiple locations
Recommendation for Single Clinic: Google Drive (lower cost, adequate features) Recommendation for Multi-Clinic: SharePoint (better control, scale-ability)
11.4.2 Folder Access and Permissions
Role-Based Access:

Sensitive Folder Restrictions: - Folder 01 (Governance): Owner + Practice Manager only - Folder 05 (Doctors): Owner + Practice Manager only (individual GP contracts private) - Folder 10 (Insurance): Owner + Bookkeeper (claims history sensitive) - Folder 16 (Due Diligence): Owner only until sale/exit planning
11.4.3 Document Scanning and OCR

What to Scan: - All signed contracts (original scanned + saved as PDF) - All insurance certificates (original stored in folder + electronic copy) - Lease agreements (original with landlord + scanned copy) - Financial documents (if received in paper, scanned) - Important supplier correspondence (invoices, notices)

OCR (Optical Character Recognition) Setup: - Use Google Drive native OCR: upload PDF → right-click → “Open with” → Google Docs → converts to searchable text - Or dedicated OCR tool: Adobe Acrobat (paid), free tools (ILovePDF.com) - Benefit: Documents become searchable by text content (not just filename)

Storage of Original Paper Documents: - Keep originals in secure lockable cabinet (clinic premises) - Retention period: 7 years minimum (per tax/compliance rules) - After 7 years: review for retention (some documents keep longer), then shred/destroy
PART 12 — INSTANT SEARCH AND RETRIEVAL SYSTEM
12.1 Search Logic: Finding Any Document in Under 30 Seconds
12.1.1 Three-Step Search Process

Step 1: Determine Document Type (5 seconds) - What kind of document? (Contract, Certificate, Invoice, Policy, Register, etc.) - Who is involved? (Supplier, staff member, external party) - What time period? (Current, historical, renewal-related)

Step 2: Navigate Folder Structure (10 seconds) - Use category/subcategory (e.g., “12_a Pathology Service Agreement”) - Scan folder for document by date or party name - Use Ctrl+F (browser/file explorer search) for quick scan
Step 3: Verify File Content (10 seconds) - Open PDF and verify content matches expectation - Check document date and version - Confirm it’s the current/correct version
Total Time: ~25 seconds for skilled user
12.1.2 Quick Reference Search Guide

rows: 12 Pathology and Suppliers → 12_h (search by supplier name and month)
04 HR and Staff → 04_b Individual Staff Folders
13 IT → 13_c (then check contact list in agreement)

text: 12.2 Tagging Logic: Consistent Tags Across All Documents
12.2.1 Tag System (For Cloud Storage)

How to Tag Documents: - In Google Drive: right-click document → “Add labels” → select relevant tags - In SharePoint: add “tags” column to document properties - In Dropbox: use “tags” feature (Business tier)
Standard Tag Set (Use Consistently Across All Documents):
By Document Type: - #Contract - #Certificate - #Invoice - #Policy - #Report - #Register - #Correspondence
By Criticality: - #Critical (insurance, contracts, accreditation) - #Important (financial, HR) - #Reference (policies, procedures)
By Renewal Status: - #NeedsRenewal (expiry within 90 days) - #Upcoming (expiry within 6 months) - #Current (valid for 6+ months) - #Expired (past expiry date)
By Entity: - #Dorevitch (pathology) - #Insurance - #Payroll - #Landlord - #ITVendor - #Staff-[Name] (e.g., #Staff-JSmith)
By Confidentiality: - #Public (can be shared) - #Internal (staff only) - #Confidential (owner/finance) - #Legal (legal privilege)
12.2.2 Tag Example: Pathology Service Agreement
Hypothetical document: 2025-03-15_Contract_Dorevitch_ServiceAgreement_V1.pdf
Tags applied: - #Contract - #Critical - #Current - #Dorevitch - #Pathology - #UpcomingRenewal (if renewal due within 6 months) - #Operational
Benefit: User can search for any tag (e.g., “#Dorevitch” or “#NeedsRenewal”) and instantly find all related documents
12.3 Register System: Spreadsheet-Based Master Index
12.3.1 Master Index in Action
Location: Root of cloud storage (easy access) File Name: Master_Document_Index_DreamClinic_2025.xlsx Software: Excel or Google Sheets (synced)
Primary Use Cases:
Finding a document quickly:
Ctrl+F in spreadsheet, search by keyword (supplier, date, doc type)
Filter by category or criticality
Locate file path immediately
Tracking renewals:
Sort by “Renewal/Review Date” column
Identify documents expiring within 90 days
Trigger action (contact supplier, renew insurance, etc.)
Audit trail:
Every document logged with date signed, effective date, expiry
Compliance proof: “Yes, we have current insurance” (show CofC in index)
Access control:
Note in index who has accessed document and when (optional column)
Useful for sensitive documents (insurance, staff files, legal)
12.3.2 Monthly Index Maintenance

Actions (1st of each month): 1. Open Master Index 2. Add any new documents from previous month (invoices, certificates, correspondence) 3. Check “Renewal/Review Date” column 4. Flag any documents expiring within 90 days as #NeedsRenewal 5. Update “Last Reviewed Date” for documents reviewed in past month 6. Save and sync to backup location

Quarterly Deep Audit (End of Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4): 1. Review entire index line-by-line 2. Verify each document still exists in cloud storage (check storage path) 3. Check for duplicate documents (consolidate if needed) 4. Verify file names match conventions 5. Update “Next Review Due” dates 6. Archive expired documents to folder 17
12.4 Single Source of Truth Dashboard
12.4.1 Dashboard Concept
What Is It? A high-level overview page (printable or digital) showing status of all critical documents at a glance. Use Google Sheets to create a dashboard that links to the Master Index.
12.4.2 Sample Dashboard Structure
Document Category Status Board (Spreadsheet Tab 2)
Color Coding: - ✓ Green: Current, no action needed - ⚠ Yellow: Expiring within 6 months, start renewal process - ✗ Red: Expired or critical action needed immediately
Dashboard Updates: - Update monthly (same time as Master Index update) - Print and post in staff office (visibility) - Email to owner/manager weekly (quick status check)
12.5 Expiry and Renewal Alerts System
12.5.1 Alert Trigger Process
Automated System (Using Google Sheets):
Master Index includes “Renewal/Review Date” column
Formula calculates 90 days before expiry
Example: Insurance expires March 1 → Renewal/Review Date = Dec 1 (90 days prior)
Monthly Alert Report
Use Google Forms + Script or Zapier to auto-email alerts
Subject: “Document Renewal Alert: [Document Name] expires [Date]”
Recipient: Owner, Practice Manager
Frequency: 1st of each month
Escalation:
90 days before expiry: “Please renew soon”
60 days before expiry: “Renewal required within 60 days”
30 days before expiry: “URGENT: Renewal due within 30 days”
Expiry date passed: “OVERDUE: [Document Name] has expired”
12.5.2 Manual Reminder System (If No Automation)
Low-Tech Alternative: Paper Calendar + Spreadsheet
Printable calendar posted in clinic office
Mark renewal dates in red (e.g., “WorkSafe Renewal Due”)
Spreadsheet (below) tracks renewal status
Renewal Tracking Spreadsheet:

12.6.1 Handover Documentation
What New Manager Must Have (In Writing):

12.6.2 Handover Template
DREAMCLINIC DOCUMENT MANAGEMENT HANDOVER
For: [New Manager Name] Date: [Date taking over] From: [Previous Manager Name]

1. CLOUD STORAGE LOCATION AND ACCESS - Platform: Google Drive (or SharePoint/Dropbox) - URL: [Link to DreamClinic shared drive] - Your login: [email address to use] - Password: [Reset password immediately upon takeover — don’t copy previous password]

2. MASTER DOCUMENT INDEX - Location: Root folder → Master_Document_Index_DreamClinic_2025.xlsx - This is your primary tool for finding any document - Update monthly with new documents - Flag expiring documents as #NeedsRenewal

3. FOLDER STRUCTURE OVERVIEW - 17 top-level folders (01 Governance through 17 Archived) - See Folder Structure Guide (Part 11.1) for what goes where - If unsure, ask or use Master Index to find similar documents
4. CRITICAL DOCUMENTS (HIGH PRIORITY - CHECK FIRST WEEK)

Insurance (Folder 10): - Public Liability: Check CofC for current coverage - Professional Indemnity: Verify all GPs have current PI certificates - WorkSafe: Verify current (mandatory if staff employed) - Check renewal dates — any expiring within 6 months?

Supplier Agreements (Folder 12): - Pathology: Verify current agreement with Dorevitch/Melbourne Pathology - Medical Waste: Check current provider and contract - IT Vendor: Verify support agreement active
Staff Contracts (Folder 05 & 04): - All GP contracts: review terms, notice periods - Staff files: verify all have current contracts
Governance (Folder 01 & 11): - Lease agreement: know renewal terms and date - Accreditation: know current status and review schedule

5. IMMEDIATE ACTIONS (FIRST WEEK) - [ ] Change cloud storage password (never use predecessor’s password) - [ ] Add yourself to all critical contact lists (insurance broker, IT vendor, pathology) - [ ] Review Master Index for any documents expiring within 90 days - [ ] Get briefing from owner on any sensitive/pending issues - [ ] Verify file naming convention (consistent naming across all folders) - [ ] Set up monthly calendar reminder: 1st of each month to update Master Index

6. MONTHLY TASKS (1ST OF EACH MONTH) - [ ] Open Master Index and add any new documents (invoices, certs, agreements) - [ ] Check renewal dates column → flag expiring within 90 days as #NeedsRenewal - [ ] Email renewal alerts to owner if any documents approaching expiry - [ ] Archive expired documents to Folder 17 - [ ] Verify all current insurance has CofC on file

7. QUARTERLY TASKS (END OF EACH QUARTER) - [ ] Deep audit of Master Index: verify each document still exists, file names correct - [ ] Review folder structure: any documents in wrong folder? Consolidate duplicates? - [ ] Verify all staff have updated files (no gaps, training current) - [ ] Check supplier performance: any issues to escalate?

8. ANNUAL TASKS (EACH DECEMBER/JANUARY) - [ ] Comprehensive audit: every document in index, nothing missing? - [ ] Archival: move expired documents to Folder 17 - [ ] Retention review: identify documents ready for shredding (>7 years) - [ ] Supplier contract review: any renegotiation needed? - [ ] Update handover documentation (this document) if anything changed
9. CONTACT LIST (KEEP CURRENT)

10. USEFUL PASSWORDS / CREDENTIALS (Store separately, never in email) - Cloud storage: [Secure password manager location] - EHR Admin account: [Secure password manager location] - Medicare PRODA: [Secure password manager location] - Master Index admin password: [If applicable]
11. COMMON ISSUES AND HOW TO RESOLVE
“I can’t find a document” → Use Master Index search function (Ctrl+F) → Search by supplier name, date, or document type → If still not found, check cloud storage directly in relevant folder

“Insurance is expiring soon” → Check renewal date in Master Index → Contact insurance broker 60 days before expiry → Request renewal quote; compare with alternative brokers → Once renewed, update Master Index with new CofC and expiry date

“Supplier asking about contract terms” → Go to Folder 12 (Pathology and Suppliers) → Find relevant supplier agreement → Check payment terms, notice period, renewal terms → If dispute, escalate to owner/lawyer
“Staff member asking about their contract” → Go to Folder 04 HR → 04_b Individual Staff Folders → Find staff member’s file, locate contract → Review together; if amendments needed, follow HR process

12.7 Practical Cloud Storage Setup Instructions
12.7.1 Step-by-Step Setup (Using Google Drive)

Step 1: Create Shared Drive (Team Drive) 1. Log into Google Drive (company account) 2. Click “New” → “Folder” → Select “Shared drive” (not regular folder) 3. Name: “DreamClinic-DocumentStorage” 4. Add members: Owner, Practice Manager, Bookkeeper (with appropriate permissions) 5. Note the link for easy access
Step 2: Create Folder Structure 1. In shared drive, create 17 top-level folders (01–17) 2. Within each, create subfolders per structure in Part 11.1 3. Use consistent naming (01_a, 01_b, etc.)

Step 3: Create Master Index 1. In Google Sheets, create new spreadsheet 2. Copy column headers from Part 11.3.1 3. Name file: Master_Document_Index_DreamClinic_2025.xlsx 4. Place in shared drive root folder 5. Set sharing: Owner (edit), Practice Manager (edit), all staff (view-only)

Step 4: Upload Documents 1. Begin with critical docs: insurance certificates, contracts, policies 2. Rename files per convention (Part 11.2) 3. Place in correct folder per structure 4. Add entry to Master Index 5. Apply tags (Step 5)

Step 5: Set Up Tagging 1. In Google Drive settings, enable “Labels” feature 2. Create label set: #Contract, #Certificate, #Critical, etc. 3. Apply labels to each document (select, right-click, “Add labels”) 4. Test search by label (click label name in sidebar, view all docs with that label)

Step 6: Set Up Sharing and Permissions 1. Right-click each sensitive folder (01, 05, 10, 16) 2. “Share” → Add specific users (not “anyone with link”) 3. Set permissions: Owner (edit), Practice Manager (edit), others (view-only) 4. Document in spreadsheet: who has access to which folder

Step 7: Set Up Backup 1. Subscribe to Google One (Backup & Sync) or use automated backup service 2. Set daily backup of shared drive to external hard drive or cloud (Dropbox, OneDrive) 3. Test restore capability (quarterly backup test)

Step 8: Create Dashboard 1. Create new Google Sheet tab in Master Index 2. Name: “Dashboard - Critical Documents Status” 3. Use formulas to auto-populate expiry dates and status 4. Set conditional formatting: red (expired), yellow (expiring soon), green (OK)

Step 9: Set Up Alerts 1. Option A (Automated): Use Google Forms + Zapier to email monthly renewal alerts 2. Option B (Manual): Set phone calendar reminder: 1st of month → “Update Document Index” 3. Test alert system with dummy data before going live

Step 10: Document This System 1. Create file: DocumentManagement_System_Setup_Guide.pdf 2. Include: folder structure overview, Master Index instructions, tagging guide, search tips 3. Place in Folder 01 (Governance) 4. Share with all staff

12.9 Compliance and Audit Trail
12.9.1 What to Document

For each critical document, record: 1. Date received/signed 2. Date filed in system 3. Who has accessed it (for sensitive docs) 4. Any amendments or updates 5. Date of last review 6. Renewal/expiry status

Example: Insurance CofC - Received from broker: Feb 28, 2025 - Filed in system: Feb 28, 2025 - Owner accessed: March 1, 2025 - Last reviewed: March 15, 2025 - Status: Current (expires Feb 28, 2026) - Next action: Review renewal Jan 15, 2026
12.9.2 Annual Compliance Audit
Checklist (Conduct December/January):
☐ Insurance: All policies current (PL, PI, WorkSafe, cyber, contents)?
☐ Supplier agreements: All valid, all in Master Index?
☐ Staff files: All contracts current, training records up-to-date?
☐ Accreditation: Current certification, QIP complete?
☐ Financial: All invoices filed, bank reconciliation current?
☐ Lease: Renewal terms known, no surprises?
☐ IT: All systems secure, backups working, licenses current?
☐ Governance: Directors minutes up-to-date, shareholders records current?
☐ Master Index: All documents present, naming consistent, no gaps?
☐ Archival: Old documents (>7 years) identified for shredding?
12.10 Summary: The Complete System in Action

Day 1 — Document Received (e.g., New Insurance Certificate): 1. Receive CofC from insurance broker (PDF email) 2. Rename file: 2025-02-28_Certificate_QBE_PublicLiability_2025.pdf 3. Place in Folder 10 → 10_a (Public Liability Insurance) 4. Open Master Index → Add row with document details 5. Apply tags: #Certificate, #Critical, #Insurance, #Current 6. Note expiry date (Feb 28, 2026) and renewal date (Jan 15, 2026)

Monthly (1st of Month): 1. Open Master Index 2. Add any new documents 3. Filter for documents expiring <90 days → Flag as #NeedsRenewal 4. Send email to owner: “Documents needing renewal this quarter: [List]”
Quarterly: 1. Deep audit: all documents present? Names correct? Folders accurate? 2. Review supplier performance 3. Update dashboard (status of all critical docs)
Annually (December): 1. Comprehensive audit: nothing missing? 2. Archive expired docs to Folder 17 3. Identify records >7 years old → Plan shredding 4. Update handover documentation

When Searching (Any Time): 1. Use Master Index (Ctrl+F) → search by supplier, date, doc type 2. Or navigate directly to folder (use folder structure guide) 3. Or search by tag in Google Drive (#Critical, #NeedsRenewal, etc.) 4. Find document in <30 seconds

If Manager Leaves (Succession): 1. Hand over: Master Index, folder access, contact list, handover document 2. New manager has all info needed to continue system 3. No knowledge loss; system is fully documented
FINAL SUMMARY
This comprehensive manual (Parts 9–12) provides:
Part 9: Complete insurance matrix, three top-10 risk registers, broker management
Part 10: Detailed supplier/external partner management (pathology, IT, waste, etc.)
Part 11: Robust folder structure (17 categories), file naming convention, master document index
Part 12: Instant search and retrieval system, tagging, alerts, handover procedures

The system is designed to: - Allow any document to be found in under 30 seconds - Ensure no critical document expires unnoticed - Provide full audit trail and compliance proof - Survive staff turnover (fully documented handover) - Scale from single clinic to multi-clinic operation - Require minimal training (structured, intuitive)

Critical success factors: 1. Consistent file naming (YYYY-MM-DD format) 2. Monthly Master Index updates (1st of month) 3. Tagging for searchability 4. Cloud storage with role-based access 5. Annual audit and archival
End of Parts 9–12 Total Word Count: ~18,500 words Australian/Victorian context throughout Fully practical, not theoretical

PART 13 — OWNER DASHBOARD
Overview

The Owner Dashboard is your single control point. This section defines what you must review, when, and why. All items should be in a shared dashboard tool (Excel, Notion, Monday.com, or Xero) that auto-updates where possible.
Key Principle: You cannot manage what you do not measure. The dashboard is your early-warning system.

rows: Review GP utilisation; check for billing errors

Escalate to IT provider; activate manual backup
Escalate to compliance lead; document response

Contact bookkeeper; review duplicate claims or reversals

13.3 MONTHLY DASHBOARD (Review by 5th of Following Month)
13.3.1 FINANCIAL P&L SNAPSHOT

rows: GP Fees (contractor % of billings or salary)
Verify current; alert 60 days before expiry
Target 15–25% net margin for GP clinics

text: What Success Looks Like: - Revenue within 5% of forecast - Net margin 15–25% (depending on model; equity-backed clinics aim higher) - All expenses within budget - No large one-off surprises

Red Flags: - Revenue <90% of target (3 months running) → potential patient loss or scheduling issue - Expenses >110% of budget (2+ months) → cost creep or unexpected event - Net margin <10% → unsustainable (unless temporary)
13.3.2 GP PAYMENT RECONCILIATION

Owner Action Each Month: 1. Match each GP’s invoice to their PMC billing statement 2. Verify percentage/rate per signed agreement 3. Confirm payment processed on agreed day 4. Flag any billing disputes immediately 5. Review if any GP’s billings trending down >10% (morale issue? patient loss?)
13.3.3 WAGES & SUPERANNUATION COMPLIANCE

Critical Checks: - Is actual super ≥11.5% of gross wages? - Are all amounts paid within 28 days of pay date? - Are all staff above NMW (National Minimum Wage)? - Is payroll tax being withheld correctly? - Is any staff in “genuine” contractor status or sham contracting risk?

rows: Practice Management (e.g., Best Practice, Medical Director)

Action: Follow up any 60+ day debtor; write off immaterial amounts (>$500) after 90 days; consider debt collection for 120+ days.
13.3.7 COMPLIANCE EXCEPTIONS & ACTIONS DUE

rows: Track per GP; alert 60 days before
Review Australian Privacy Principles; update if needed

Review all policies (medical indemnity, public liability, property, cyber); consolidate if possible; ensure all staff listed; maintain compliance
Is our rent fair? Any renewal/break clauses coming?
Check lease against market comparable; review outgoings; flag renewal/break dates 12 months in advance; consider refinancing if terms deteriorated
Are we compliant? Any opportunities to optimise?
Review payroll tax exposure; discuss with accountant; consider entity structure; ensure ATO/VRMA reporting on-time; assess quarterly BAS accuracy
How close were our forecasts? What did we learn?
Compare annual budget to actual P&L; calculate variances by line item; adjust next-year budget; document assumptions
Are our GPs happy? Any morale concerns?

Collect NPS (Net Promoter Score) or patient feedback; review Google/Healthgrades reviews; identify service gaps; plan improvements
Are we running lean? Any waste or inefficiency?

Is our financials clean and audit-ready?
Finalise annual P&L; reconcile all balance sheet items; file tax return if partnership/company; brief accountant on one-off items; plan next year
