PART 14 — FIRST 90 DAYS ACTION PLAN AFTER ACQUISITION
Overview

The first 90 days post-acquisition are critical. Your goal is stabilisation, not transformation. Do not attempt major operational changes in month 1; focus on securing access, understanding the business, and identifying gaps. Months 2–3 involve deeper analysis and preparation for longer-term optimisation.
Guiding Principle: Trust but verify. The seller may have misrepresented aspects of the business. Your job is to confirm everything is as represented—and to identify what was not disclosed.
14.1 DAYS 1–7: CRITICAL STABILISATION

rows: Cannot run clinic without access to banking, software, Medicare, email, domain
Request from seller agent: banking logins, PMC admin password, email domain access, Medicare portal credentials, HPOS/PRODA access, alarm codes, building keys, safe combination
Test each login immediately; verify you have admin rights; confirm 2FA is set up
Any account with seller still holding access; missing MFA
You can independently reset passwords; multi-user access confirmed
Meet Every Staff Member Individually (1:1 meetings, 15 min each)
Build rapport; understand any concerns; identify flight risks or cultural issues; learn informal processes
Schedule before clinic starts or at lunch; ask: “What’s working well?”, “What’s frustrating?”, “Any concerns about the transition?”, “Do you plan to stay?”
Body language; eye contact; hesitation on future plans; mention of undisclosed arrangements (cash payments, informal hours)
Any staff member expressing plans to leave; undisclosed cash arrangements; confusion about pay/hours
All staff express confidence; no surprise departures in month 1
Confirm All GP Contractor Agreements Are Signed & Current
Ensures you are the legal employer; understand payment obligations; identify verbal arrangements that could create liability
Collect signed contracts from practice records; review each for: term, termination clause, restraint clause, non-compete, IP ownership, payment % or FTE rate, notice period, dispute resolution
Look for: expiry dates, unsigned agreements, inconsistency between contract and actual billings, missing restraint clauses, unclear good faith payment obligations
Any unsigned agreement; verbal arrangement undisclosed; contract term expiring within 12 months without renewal language
All GPs have signed, current agreements; no surprises in payment obligations
Verify All Insurance Certificates Are Valid
Protect clinic from liability; comply with lease and professional requirements

Request from seller: medical indemnity certificate (verify practitioner names match), public liability certificate, professional indemnity (if applicable), property insurance, cyber liability (recommended)
Check: expiry dates, coverage amounts ($5M+ typical for medical indemnity), named insured matches your entity
Any certificate expiring within 30 days; low coverage limits (<$2M); GP names missing
All policies current; coverage adequate; certificates stored electronically
Ensures you have legal occupancy; avoids eviction risk; clarifies outgoings obligations
Request: executed lease assignment document from seller’s lawyer; landlord consent letter; copy of current lease with all amendments; latest outgoings reconciliation
Check: your name/entity as new tenant; landlord signature; any break clauses or renewal options; rent review clauses (CPI?); outgoings cap
Lease not yet assigned to you; landlord not formally notified; ambiguous outgoings language
Lease formally assigned; landlord acknowledgment email received
Ensure you control clinic finances; prevent fraud; understand cash position
Visit bank in person; request: list of all accounts (operating, savings, loan), current signatories, recent 6-month statement, any loan/facility agreements
Check: your name added as signatory; seller removed or in second position; account names match clinic entity; no unexplained large transfers
Seller still sole signatory; joint signatory without your consent; unexplained low balance (<1 week payroll)
You are primary signatory; statements auto-download to Xero; no other signatories without approval
Verify Payroll Is Set Up Correctly Under New Entity
Ensures employees are paid on-time; minimizes ATO issues; confirms no arrears or underpayments

Meet with payroll provider or bookkeeper; request: proof payroll is processing under your ABN/entity name, latest payslip examples, superannuation arrangement (fund name, account details), PAYG withholding settings
Check: employee names and amounts match staff records, super contributions ≥11.5%, tax file numbers on file, YTD figures reconcile to timesheets
Payroll still running under seller’s entity; super not being paid; missing TFNs
Payroll processes cleanly; staff all paid by day 5 of month; no ATO correspondence
Check Accreditation Status & Next Cycle Date
Know your compliance baseline; understand what you inherit; plan for next audit
Contact RACGP or accreditation body; request: copy of current accreditation certificate, last survey report, any non-conformances/corrective actions, planned next survey date
Read survey report carefully: what areas were flagged? Are corrective actions completed? When is next survey? What’s the deadline to be re-accredited?
Practice is not accredited; survey is overdue; critical non-conformances not addressed
Accreditation current; corrective actions clearly tracked; next survey planned 6+ months ahead
Secure Physical Keys, Alarm Codes, Safe Combinations
Cannot access clinic for operations; security liability; staff cannot enter/exit

Request from seller/landlord: set of clinic keys (front, back, server room, drug safe, filing cabinets), alarm system code, alarm system login/admin details, safe combination (if applicable), CCTV/security login
Test each key; disarm/re-arm alarm; change all codes/combinations immediately
Seller retains keys; alarm code unchanged; safe lock broken
New key set created; alarm reprogrammed; safe tested; old codes destroyed
Review Upcoming Roster for Next 4 Weeks
Identify scheduling issues, gaps, or overcommitment; prevent patient access problems
Request from practice manager: roster for next 28 days, any known leave or part-time patterns, any GPs on notice or planning change in hours
Look for: gaps (especially if multi-branch), clustering of GP absences, rostering inconsistencies with signed contracts, any sessions not yet confirmed
Any week with <FTE GPs rostered; no contingency for casual coverage; unconfirmed sessions
Roster complete and confirmed; all GPs have signed off; relief staff pre-arranged for known absences
Know what’s broken before it becomes a bigger issue; avoid surprise audits or patient harm

Conduct quick review: AHPRA registration of all GPs (online check), insurance certificates validity, cold chain compliance (fridge temps logged?), hand hygiene audit, incident register (any overdue follow-ups?), privacy policy currency (updated for Australian Privacy Principles?)
Document each finding in a checklist; flag as critical, high, or low risk
Any GP not registered; insurance lapsed; fridge temperature logs missing; incident register not current
Compliance checklist completed; all critical items assigned an owner and deadline
Set Up Your Own PRODA/HPOS Access
Direct access to Medicare rebate data; ability to review claiming independently; dispute resolution
Register for PRODA (Provider Digital Access) or HPOS (Health Professional Online Services); request access to clinic’s Medicare account
Verify you can log in; view recent claiming; access provider number details; set up delegate permissions
You cannot access clinic’s Medicare data; access denied for provider number
You can independently view clinic’s claiming history and balance owing
Ensures clinic receives rebate income; know collection/referral arrangements; avoid service interruptions
Request: signed pathology agreement (e.g., Healthscope, Sonic, local pathology), confirmation of transfer to new entity, commission/rebate rates, collection schedule
Check: your entity named as provider; agreement term and renewal dates; any performance minimums; weekly/monthly settlement timing
Pathology agreement still in seller’s name; unclear commission structure; no written agreement
Agreement transferred; commission reconciliation understood; collection samples flowing smoothly
DO NOT MAKE OPERATIONAL CHANGES YET
Premature changes cause staff uncertainty, patient disruption, and unintended consequences; need baseline data to decide what to change
Make a commitment: no staffing changes, pricing changes, service changes, or policy updates until day 31+
Monitor for the urge to “fix” things; document any issues for later review
Staff leaving due to uncertainty; patients complaining about unexpected changes
You have baseline metrics; staff feel stable; patient feedback is neutral

14.2 DAYS 8–30: UNDERSTANDING & DOCUMENTATION

rows: Understand the business you’ve bought; verify seller representations; identify trends; spot anomalies
Request from accountant/bookkeeper: 12-month P&L (month by month), balance sheet, bank reconciliations, cash flow statement, copy of last tax return or BAS submissions
Plot billings on a chart: is there a trend (growing/declining/seasonal)? Analyse expenses: are they in line with budgets? Any large one-off items? Compare each month’s net margin: 15–25% is healthy
Revenue declining >5% per month YoY; expenses increasing without corresponding revenue growth; large month-to-month variance unexplained; margin <10%
You can explain every revenue and expense line; have identified 2–3 improvement opportunities; understand cashflow cycle
Identify obligations, expiry dates, renewal opportunities, and hidden liabilities
Create a master contract index (spreadsheet): contract name, counterparty, date signed, term, renewal date, notice period, termination clauses, cost, key terms, location of original document
Examples: lease, GP agreements, pathology agreement, IT/software SLAs, supplier agreements (cleaning, waste, stationery), professional services (accountant, lawyer), landlord correspondence
Any contract missing from index; term unclear or verbal; expiry date within 12 months without renewal language; unfavourable terms vs market
Master contract index complete; all originals located; all key dates flagged in calendar; renewal process documented
Know where everything is; facilitate onboarding of new staff; prepare for compliance audits; enable faster decision-making

Create a single source of truth: folder structure (physical or digital) for: finance (invoices, statements), legal (contracts, leases), HR (staff files, award determinations), compliance (policies, audit reports, incident logs), clinical (protocols, accreditation, training records)
Information scattered across multiple locations; duplicate versions with unclear dates; key documents missing or in poor condition
All documents in one location; naming convention consistent; master index current (updated weekly); staff can find anything in <2 minutes
Verify All Staff Credentials & HR Files
Ensure no undisclosed employment gaps, credential issues, or HR liabilities; comply with Fair Work Act

Request from practice manager: personnel file for each staff member (employment contract, evidence of qualifications, police check, working with children check if applicable, reference checks, RoPA registration for nurses, vaccination proof, emergency contact, tax file number declaration)
Check dates: are credential checks recent (<3 years)? Employment contracts signed? Any gaps in employment history? Are vacation/leave policies documented?
Any staff without employment contract; police check >5 years old; missing credential verification; casual staff without proper SLA
All staff have signed contracts; credentials verified within last 3 years; personnel files complete
Understand how clinic operates; identify gaps or outdated procedures; ensure compliance with regulatory requirements

Request: copies of all policies (complaints, infection control, privacy, confidentiality, WHS, medication management, waste disposal, etc.); procedure manuals (front desk, appointment booking, clinical recording, etc.)
Red-flag policies: do they exist? Are they dated and reviewed? Do staff know about them? Any policies conflicting with award requirements or Australian Privacy Principles?
Policies missing for key areas (e.g., no complaints procedure); policies >2 years old without review date; staff unaware of policies
Policies complete, current, and reviewed annually; staff trained on key policies; version control in place
Build relationship; understand financial controls and compliance roadmap; clarify tax/superannuation obligations

Schedule 2–3 hour meeting: review last 12-month financials, discuss BAS preparation process, confirm payroll tax exposure, discuss entity structure and tax planning opportunities, review superannuation compliance, agree on reporting cadence (e.g., monthly P&L to owner)
Clarify: when is BAS due? Who prepares it? Is payroll tax a risk? What’s the best entity structure for your acquisition? Are there tax planning opportunities?
Accountant unable to explain significant revenue/expense variances; unclear on BAS/payroll tax obligations; no documented reporting process
You have monthly P&L email by 5th of following month; clear calendar of tax dates; agreed tax strategy
Understand system landscape; ensure no undisclosed vulnerabilities or dependencies; plan for security and uptime

Schedule call: review all systems (PMC, pathology, email, internet, backup, security), SLAs (what’s covered? response time? uptime guarantee?), backup and disaster recovery plan, security measures (firewalls, antivirus, MFA), any known issues or planned upgrades
Ask: if main server fails, how long until clinic back online? Who has admin access to key systems? When was last security audit?
No formal IT SLA; backup plan unclear or untested; MFA not enabled on critical systems; IT provider difficult to reach
All systems documented; formal SLAs in place; backup tested monthly; security posture assessed
Establish relationship; confirm lease terms; identify any hidden issues or neighbourhood/property changes; discuss future flexibility

Schedule call/meeting: introduce yourself as new tenant, confirm you’ve received lease assignment, discuss any upcoming maintenance/renovations, ask about foot traffic/neighbourhood trends, clarify outgoings process, discuss any lease break/renewal options
Topics: Is the building secure? Any planned renovations that could disrupt clinic? Parking adequate? Landlord’s expectations for tenant behaviour?
Landlord unfamiliar with transition; lease assignment not yet processed; hidden plans for building changes; outgoings disputes
Landlord relationship established; lease terms clarified; no surprises in maintenance schedule
Ensure clinic is claiming correctly; identify any billing errors or missed rebate opportunities; verify no overpayment or clawback risk
Ask practice manager/GP: how are items coded? Who bulk-bills vs gap-charges? How are rebates tracked? Any rejected claims? Ask accountant: how are rebates reconciled to deposits?
Chart rebate lag (days between service and receipt); identify any bulk-bill ratio changes; check for any adjustment notices; verify rebate deposits match expected amounts
Rebates arriving >14 days late; unexplained drop in rebate deposits; adjustment notices not understood; coding inconsistency across GPs
Rebates reconcile cleanly to deposits; coding is consistent; no unexplained gaps or variances
Know what you’re paying for; understand which systems are critical; plan for future consolidation or upgrades
Create system inventory: system name, provider, cost, admin login, integrations, criticality (essential/important/nice-to-have), renewal date, SLA
Include: PMC, pathology portal, email, website, online appointments, VoIP, accounting, backup, security, staff scheduling, time tracking
Any system login unknown; integration not working; critical system on non-standard setup
All systems documented; admin access confirmed; integrations mapped; cost optimization opportunities identified
Understand safety culture; identify any systemic issues; ensure compliance with accreditation requirements
Request: complaints log (last 2 years), incident register (last 2 years), any formal complaints to RACGP or health complaints body, any litigation or legal disputes
Analyse: what’s the complaint rate? Common themes? How are complaints resolved? Are follow-ups documented? Are GPs involved in any disputes?
No formal complaint/incident register; complaints logged inconsistently; unresolved complaints >30 days old; any regulatory complaint
Log complete and current; complaints resolved promptly; no patterns or systemic issues identified
Vaccine storage is a regulatory and clinical requirement; failure = lost accreditation and potential patient harm
Inspect fridge in person: check temperature log (daily readings?), look for expired vaccines, check max/min thermometer, verify backup power/alarm, review fridge servicing records
Check: are temps consistently 2–8°C? Any days outside range? Last service date? Backup power in place?
Temp log missing or irregular; any temps outside range; fridge never serviced; no backup power
Temp log consistent and current; all readings in range; fridge serviced last 12 months; backup alarm in place
Protect staff and patients; avoid WorkCover claims; ensure regulatory compliance
Request: WHS policy, incident/injury register, staff induction checklist, hazard register, first aid kit inventory, MSDS for chemicals, evacuation plan
Walk around clinic: are hazards identified? Are spill kits accessible? Are first aid kits stocked? Is emergency contact info posted?
WHS policy missing or >2 years old; no incident register; staff untrained on hazards
WHS policy current; all staff inducted; hazard register complete; emergency procedures clear
Start building the case for operational changes; understand where to focus effort in months 2–3

Based on your review so far, what stands out as inefficient or suboptimal? Examples: billing codes could be optimized, staff scheduling is wasteful, consumable waste is high, appointment utilisation could improve, reception workflow is inefficient
Quantify opportunity: what’s the financial impact if improved? How much effort to fix?
Opportunities identified but not quantified; no clear priority; no owner assigned
3 opportunities identified, ranked by ROI and effort, with rough timeline for implementation
Prepare for future accreditation audits; enable faster onboarding; reduce information loss
Set up: physical filing cabinets for HR/contracts; cloud storage (Google Drive, OneDrive, or Dropbox) for compliance/clinical docs; password manager (LastPass, 1Password) for system credentials
Implement: folder structure aligned to accreditation requirements (e.g., RACGP Blue Book sections); version control for policies; access controls (who can see what?)
Documents scattered; no folder structure; no access controls; sensitive data not protected
All documents backed up to cloud; version control in place; access controls set; staff trained on system

14.3 DAYS 31–60: OPTIMISATION & CONTROL

rows: Establish rhythm for data-driven decision-making; catch problems early
Set recurring Friday meeting (30 min): review dashboard metrics (billings, staffing, complaints, incidents), discuss variances, assign actions to practice manager, plan for next week
You receive dashboard email every Friday; weekly meeting produces 2–3 actionable decisions
Clean data enables better decisions; faster month-end close; easier tax/audit
Work with accountant/bookkeeper: agree on monthly P&L format, reconciliation checklist, BAS tracking, invoice approval process, expense categorisation
Monthly P&L arrives by 5th of following month; all variances explained; no surprises at tax time
Review & Update GP Payment Reconciliation Process
Ensure accuracy, fairness, and compliance with signed agreements
Verify each GP’s monthly invoice matches their billings; confirm agreement percentages/rates; test payment process (bank transfer, frequency); flag any disputes immediately
All GP payments processed on agreed schedule; no disputes; invoicing transparent and auditable
Close risks; ensure all key agreements are documented and current
Review master contract index; identify gaps (e.g., no written pathology agreement, no formal IT SLA); draft and execute missing agreements; schedule renewals as needed
All critical agreements documented; renewals tracked; no verbal-only arrangements
Modernise operations; ensure regulatory compliance; clarify expectations for staff
Schedule policy review: prioritise key policies (clinical, WHS, privacy); update based on feedback from staff and accreditation requirements; brief team on changes
Key policies updated and reviewed annually; staff trained; version control current
Never miss an insurance renewal, accreditation survey, or contract renewal

Create master renewal calendar: insurance (medical indemnity, public liability, property), accreditation (RACGP), GP registrations (AHPRA), contract renewals (lease, pathology, IT), credential checks (police, working with children, CPD)
Calendar shared with team; automatic reminders 60/30/14 days before each renewal; 100% on-time renewal rate
Identify overstaffing (cost drag) or understaffing (morale/burnout); align to clinic capacity
Analyse: staff cost as % of revenue (should be 25–35% for GP clinics); utilisation by role (nurses, admin, reception); turnover rate; any contractors who could be permanent (or vice versa)
You can explain staffing costs; identify any overstaffing or retention risks; have plan for next hire
Low-hanging fruit to improve revenue without service changes
Review item coding: are GPs using highest-value codes consistently? Any under-coding? Analyse gap fees: are they competitive? Could you increase bulk-bill ratio for some services?
Billing codes audited; gap fees reviewed; revenue per consultation increased 5–10% if opportunity exists
Communicate priorities; celebrate wins; address issues promptly; build culture
Schedule: weekly 30-min all-staff briefing (Monday morning), monthly 60-min team meeting (address concerns, policy updates), quarterly 90-min full clinic meeting (strategic updates, celebration)
All staff attend; meetings are productive; action items tracked; culture is transparent and inclusive
Address Any WHS or Compliance Gaps
Avoid workplace incidents; maintain accreditation; protect staff
Action items from WHS audit: train staff on hazard identification, stock first aid kits, post emergency contact info, schedule equipment checks, update incident register
All WHS items closed; no outstanding compliance gaps; staff feel safe
Reduce costs; improve service; consolidate vendors
Analyse: consumable suppliers (gloves, swabs, syringes—any price increases?), waste disposal (frequency, cost, competition?), cleaning (quality and cost), courier/delivery services
You’ve negotiated 5–10% savings on 1–2 suppliers, or consolidated to fewer vendors with better terms

14.4 DAYS 61–90: GROWTH FOUNDATION

rows: Reduce legal/compliance risks; negotiate favourable terms before renewal

Final review of any contracts still pending; identify 2–3 renewal opportunities for next 12 months; brief negotiation strategy for each (e.g., landlord—ask for 3-year extension at CPI only, no step increases)
All contracts reviewed and understood; renewal strategy documented; no surprises on renewal dates
Enable fast, confident access to any document; audit trail for compliance; succession planning
Migrate all documents to cloud system with version control; set access controls; train all staff; test retrieval speed; schedule quarterly cleanup/archiving
All documents centrally accessible; staff can find anything in <2 minutes; audit trail complete
Define priorities for next year; align team; measure progress
Based on top 3 improvement opportunities + financial review + team feedback, draft plan: specific initiatives, timelines, responsibilities, success metrics, budget
Team understands priorities; monthly progress updates conducted; quarterly reviews show progress on plan
Plan ahead for natural attrition; ensure clinic capacity; improve work-life balance

Analyse: are any GPs approaching retirement? Any part-time who might leave? Clinic utilisation (are we at capacity?). If recruitment needed: start process early (6-month lead time typical); draft job description; engage recruitment agency; plan onboarding
If recruiting: process begun; candidates identified by end of day 90. If not: plan documented for future reference
Understand clinic trajectory; identify any patient loss; plan marketing if needed

Analyse: new patient numbers month-by-month (trending up/down?); patient retention (how many return?); age/demographic mix; any patterns (e.g., losing patients after first visit?). Benchmark against industry (typical GP clinic: 5–10% new patient growth annually)
You understand patient growth trajectory; identified any retention issues; have plan to improve if needed
Grow revenue or patient base; improve community value

Ask GPs: what services do patients request that we don’t offer? Examples: mental health (GPMI billing), chronic disease management (TCAs), minor procedures (sutures, skin tag removal), allied health partnerships (physio, psychology)
1–2 service expansion opportunities identified and scoped (e.g., mental health clinic 1x weekly; cost and revenue models calculated)
Establish KPIs for All Key Areas
Measure performance; drive accountability; identify trends early

Define KPIs by area: Financial (billings/FTE, net margin), Clinical (patient satisfaction NPS, safety incidents), Operational (appointment utilisation, staff turnover, customer wait time), Compliance (accreditation score, incident resolution time)
KPIs dashboard created; baselines established; targets set; monthly tracking underway
Reduce wait time; improve patient experience; free up admin time

Observe reception for 1–2 days; map appointment booking process, payment collection, patient sign-in, enquiry resolution; identify bottlenecks; test improvements (e.g., pre-check-in via text, online payment)
Patient wait time reduced 20–30%; reception team morale improved; staff feel empowered to improve workflows
Assess progress; celebrate wins; reset for next quarter
Formal meeting: review acquisition objectives vs actual performance, discuss what’s working and what isn’t, update financial forecast, set priorities for Q2, brief stakeholders/investors
Quarterly summary document produced; team alignment confirmed; investor confidence high
Maintain compliance; avoid surprise audit failures; prepare team

Check timeline: when is next accreditation survey due? If >18 months away, plan for full preparation. If <18 months, begin now. Identify any gaps from accreditation feedback; assign responsibility for remediation; schedule mock audit 3 months before survey
Accreditation readiness plan drafted; no critical gaps remain; team is confident in survey outcome
