PART 15 — RED FLAGS & MISSING ITEMS
Overview

This section lists the critical risks and gaps in a typical GP acquisition. Since you’ve been provided no documents by the seller, these are the items you must obtain and verify before or immediately after settlement.

Ranking system: - CRITICAL = Acquisition cannot proceed without this; compliance/legal/safety risk if missing - HIGH = Should be resolved within 30 days; creates operational or financial risk - MODERATE = Should be resolved within 90 days; efficiency or compliance concern
15.1 CRITICAL MISSING DOCUMENTS

rows: Written contract between clinic and each GP; specifies terms, payment, termination, restraints, IP ownership
Determines employment status (contractor vs employee), payment obligations, notice period, ability to enforce non-compete
Request from seller: original signed agreements for all GPs. Review for: term, renewal clause, payment %, notice period, restraint clause, dispute resolution. Must be dated within last 3 years
Any GP working on verbal arrangement only; agreement unsigned; contract term expiring within 12 months without renewal language; payment clause unclear (% of billings vs fixed fee?)
All GPs have signed agreements current for ≥12 months; payment terms match actual billings; restraint clauses enforceable
Agreement between landlord and clinic for occupancy of premises
Confirms you have legal right to occupy the space; defines rent, outgoings, term, break clauses, renewal options, maintenance obligations

Request from seller/landlord: original signed lease with all amendments and schedules, evidence that assignment to your entity has been consented by landlord, latest outgoings reconciliation, any correspondence re: disputes or maintenance
Lease still in seller’s name; assignment to you not yet executed; rent/outgoings terms unclear; any break clause within 12 months without discussion with landlord; outgoings disputes
Lease formally assigned to your entity; landlord written consent obtained; all terms clear and documented
Current professional indemnity insurance covering clinic and named GPs
Regulatory requirement (RACGP accreditation); protects clinic from malpractice claims; covers all practitioners
Request from seller: original certificate of currency; verify: coverage limits ≥$5M, all GP names listed, expiry >60 days away, no significant exclusions
Certificate expiring <60 days; GP names missing from policy; coverage limits <$3M; significant exclusions (e.g., cosmetic surgery)
Current certificate in your name/entity; all GPs listed; expiry >12 months; coverage ≥$5M
Access to all clinic bank accounts; understanding of cash position; control of finances
Without access, cannot pay staff, suppliers, or GPs; cannot verify balance or investigate fraud; cannot receive rebates
Request from seller: list of all accounts (operating, savings), latest 6-month statements, proof of transfer of signatories to your entity
Seller remains sole signatory; you don’t have independent access; unexplained large transfers out of clinic account pre-settlement; low cash balance (<1 week payroll)
You are primary signatory; seller removed; statements auto-download to Xero; cash balance sufficient for 2+ weeks operations
Proof that clinic meets RACGP standards; copy of last survey findings and non-conformances
Tells you compliance baseline; any non-conformances you inherit; when next survey due; what you need to fix
Request from seller/RACGP: current certificate, full last survey report, any follow-up correspondence re: corrective actions
Practice not accredited (huge red flag); survey report shows critical non-conformances not yet addressed; next survey overdue
Current accreditation; survey report shows minor findings only; corrective actions documented and on track; next survey 6+ months away
Proof of staff wages, super contributions, tax withholdings
Identifies any underpayment, non-compliance, or arrears; confirms amount you inherit as liability
Request from seller/bookkeeper: payroll register (all staff, all months YTD), evidence of super payments (bank statements or fund statements), latest payslips, ATO PAYG reconciliation
Payroll records incomplete or missing; super not paid for multiple months; staff underpaid below NMW; no evidence of tax withholding
All payroll records complete; super paid 100% on time; staff paid at/above NMW; tax withholding correct
Medicare Provider Agreement & Provider Number Documentation
Proof that clinic is registered as Medicare service provider; copy of provider agreement
Cannot claim Medicare rebates without valid provider number; agreement outlines billing obligations and compliance requirements
Request from seller: provider agreement (can be downloaded from Services Australia), provider number(s) assigned, any correspondence from Medicare re: billing compliance or disputes
Provider agreement missing or outdated; provider number unclear; Medicare compliance letters/penalties not disclosed
Current provider agreement; provider number clear; no outstanding compliance issues with Medicare
Signed employment contract for every staff member (nurses, admin, reception)
Defines employment terms, award classification, hours, pay, notice period, IP ownership, restraints; protects against claims of unfair dismissal
Request from practice manager: signed employment contract for every staff member (not casual); verify: award classification correct, hours match roster, notice periods defined, any restraint clauses
Any staff without contract; contracts undated or unsigned; unclear award classification; staff underpaid below NMW
All staff have signed current contracts; award classification correct; contract terms match actual employment
Agreement between clinic and pathology provider (e.g., Healthscope, Sonic) for specimen collection, rebates, commission
Ensures clinic receives pathology rebates; clarifies commission structure; defines collection schedule and compliance obligations
Request from seller: signed pathology agreement, commission/rebate rates, evidence that agreement transferred to your entity, recent statements showing rebate reconciliation
Agreement still in seller’s name; commission structure unclear; no written agreement (verbal arrangement); pathology not transferring specimens to correct provider
Agreement formally transferred to you; commission/rebate process clear and reconciling cleanly
Master document showing all regulatory deadlines: RACGP survey, AHPRA registrations, insurance renewals, police checks, etc.
Prevents missed deadlines that could cost accreditation, licenses, or staff
Request from seller/practice manager: list of all compliance deadlines (insurance, accreditation, staff credentials); verify dates against original documents
No master list of compliance dates; missing renewal dates; any deadline within 30 days not yet actioned
Master compliance calendar created; all renewals flagged 60/30/14 days before; 100% on-time completion
Current public liability, property, cyber, workers’ compensation (if applicable) insurance
Protects clinic assets, staff, and patients; may be lease requirement; demonstrates due diligence
Request from seller: certificates for all policies (public liability, property, cyber, workers’ comp if applicable), verify: coverage adequate, expiry >60 days, all named insured match your entity
Any policy expired or expiring <30 days; coverage limits inadequate; cyber insurance missing (increasingly required); workers’ comp only if have employees
All policies current; coverage adequate; renewal dates flagged; stored electronically and with insurers
Temperature log and service records for vaccine fridge
Accreditation requirement; non-compliance = vaccine wastage + lost accreditation + patient safety risk
Request from seller: temperature logs (last 12 months—should show daily readings 2–8°C), fridge service records (within last 12 months), backup power/alarm documentation
Temp logs missing or irregular; any readings outside 2–8°C range not documented; fridge never serviced; no backup power
Temp logs current and consistent; readings always in range; fridge serviced last 12 months; backup alarm in place
All operational policies (complaints, infection control, privacy, WHS, medication management, clinical protocols)
Ensures staff understand expectations; compliance requirement for accreditation; liability protection

Request from practice manager: copies of all policies (at minimum: complaints, privacy, WHS, infection control, medication); verify: all dated within last 2 years, reviewed and approved, staff have acknowledged reading
Policies missing for key areas; policies >2 years old without review date; no evidence staff have read/understood
All policies current and reviewed annually; staff acknowledgment of reading on file; policies easy to find and update
Log of all patient complaints, staff incidents, near-misses, patient safety events
Shows safety culture; identifies trends (e.g., recurring complaint themes); demonstrates proactive risk management to accreditors
Request from practice manager: full complaint/incident log (dates, summary, resolution, follow-up), formal complaints made to RACGP or health complaints body, any legal actions
No formal register; complaints logged inconsistently; unresolved complaints >30 days old; any formal complaint to regulator not disclosed
Register current and organised; complaints resolved promptly; no systemic issues; any regulatory complaints fully disclosed and addressed
Patient Health Records - Retention & Destruction Policy
Written policy on how long records are kept, when destroyed, method of secure destruction
Compliance requirement (National Health Records Act); minimum 7 years after last contact (or longer if patient <18); secure destruction required
Request from practice manager: written policy on health record retention and destruction; evidence of implementation (e.g., annual destruction records)
No written policy; records kept >10 years unnecessarily (storage cost); no evidence of secure destruction; records of minors destroyed <7 years after last contact
Written policy current; destruction schedule maintained; evidence of secure destruction; compliance with retention periods
RACGP Accreditation Action Plan (If Non-Conformances Exist)
If last survey found non-conformances, document showing corrective actions and timeline
Failing to address non-conformances = loss of accreditation at next survey
Request from seller: copy of survey report + any follow-up correspondence from RACGP, written action plan for each non-conformance, evidence of completion
Non-conformances noted in survey but no action plan; corrective actions overdue; no evidence of remediation
Action plan complete and tracked; all non-conformances closed before your ownership; next survey will be clean
Proof that patient data is backed up regularly, systems are secure (firewalls, antivirus, MFA enabled)
Patient data is critical; loss = accreditation violation + patient privacy breach + financial loss
Request from IT provider: backup schedule and last successful backup date, security audit report, MFA implementation status, any known vulnerabilities
No documented backup; backup last performed >30 days ago; no security audit in last 2 years; MFA not enabled on critical systems
Daily backups verified; last backup current (<24 hours); security audit completed recently; MFA enabled on all systems

text: 15.2 CRITICAL MISSING REGISTRATIONS / IDENTIFIERS / ACCESS

rows: Individual medical registration for each GP
Must have valid registration to practise; regulatory requirement; flag if GP has conditions or suspended
Visit AHPRA website (ahpra.gov.au); search each GP by name; verify “registered” status and any conditions
Any GP not registered; registration lapsed; conditions on registration not disclosed (e.g., required to work under supervision)
All GPs registered with no conditions; registration active until ≥12 months in future
Unique identifier for clinic at that address; needed to claim Medicare rebates
Cannot bill Medicare without valid provider number; number is location-specific (if you move, need new number)
Log in to Medicare Provider Digital Access (PRODA) or Health Professional Online Services (HPOS); verify provider number(s) assigned to your clinic address; check claiming history
Provider number not visible or multiple numbers for same location; no claiming activity for >30 days (broken setup?)
Provider number clear and active; claiming activity regular and matching expected volume
Australian Business Number; if operating as sole trader or partnership, business name must be registered
Required for tax reporting, employment, and operating legally
Check ABR (Australian Business Register) for ABN; verify registered business name and ABN status
ABN status not active; registered to wrong entity or person; lapsed registration
ABN current and active; registered to correct entity/person; no changes in ABN status
Your personal login to Medicare portal (Health Professional Online Services or Provider Digital Access)
Allows independent verification of clinic’s Medicare claiming and balance; required for dispute resolution
Register at HPOS/PRODA; verify you receive email confirmation and can log in; test by viewing provider details
Cannot gain access or access denied; system shows clinic to different owner; no email confirmation
You have active HPOS/PRODA access; can view clinic’s Medicare data independently
If clinic operated as proprietary company or partnership, ASIC registration required
Confirms legal entity, directors, company status
Visit ASIC website (asic.gov.au); search company name; verify: status (active), directors match records, no ASIC compliance issues
Company not registered with ASIC (red flag); status “deregistered” or “in liquidation”; directors incorrect
Company registered and active; directors/stakeholders match records; no ASIC compliance issues
Required if you have employees (nurses, admin); coverage issued by Victorian WorkCover
Covers staff injuries; compliance requirement; affects payroll tax
Contact Victorian WorkCover Authority (workcoverwa.gov.au equivalent for Victoria); verify clinic has active policy for all employees
No policy; policy lapsed; employees not all covered
Current policy in place; all employees covered; policy number on file
Some GPs may have individual professional indemnity (above clinic’s group cover)
Clarifies layers of coverage; individual GP protection
Request from each GP: proof of individual indemnity if applicable; verify coverage is in addition to clinic’s group cover
Any GP without either individual or clinic cover; coverage gap identified
All GPs covered via clinic policy or individual policy; total coverage ≥$5M per practitioner

text: 15.3 HIGH-RISK CONTRACTUAL ISSUES

rows: Unsigned or Undocumented GP Contractor Agreements
GP working on handshake deal only; no written contract
Cannot enforce payment terms, notice periods, or restraint clauses; dispute resolution is nightmare; if GP leaves, can demand money for goodwill lost
Any GP without dated, signed contract; verbal arrangement mentioned by staff or GP; inconsistency between contract and actual billings
Insist on signed agreement before paying any GP invoice; agreement should specify: payment %, term, renewal, notice period, restraint clause, dispute resolution; have lawyer review if material GP
Lease term ending within 12 months and no renewal language; risk of eviction
Clinic operations disrupted; forced to relocate; lose patient base; cannot refinance/resell business easily
Review lease end date carefully; check for: renewal options (right to extend?), break clauses (can landlord terminate early?), rent review mechanism (CPI escalation only, or step increases?)
Negotiate renewal/extension ASAP if within 12 months; ideally secure 3–5 year term; confirm in writing from landlord
Unclear Goodwill Allocation in Purchase Price
If you purchased “goodwill” as part of acquisition, contract should specify what goodwill means (patient list? contracts? systems?) and any conditions for claw-back
Tax implications; IP ownership issues; if goodwill isn’t as represented, difficult to recover
Review purchase agreement (if you have one); does it specify: what goodwill is included? Any warranties from seller? Any claw-back provisions if patient retention drops?
If goodwill ambiguous, document in writing (email to seller’s agent) what you understood goodwill to include; seek indemnity/warranty if not as represented
IP Ownership of Patient Records / Systems
Who owns the patient health records? The clinic software? Custom systems or integrations built by prior owner?
If IP not owned by you, cannot claim ownership of patient data; if forced to leave premises or sell, lose key assets; potential breach of Privacy Act if ownership unclear
Review: clinic contracts for PMC (do they own data or clinic?), any custom systems or add-ons developed (who owns the code?), supplier agreements (any restriction on data export?)
Clarify in writing with sellers and key vendors: clinic owns all patient health records and data; right to export if vendor relationship ends; any IP created by/for clinic owned by clinic
Restraint clause in GP agreement is too narrow (e.g., only 2 km radius, 12-month term) or missing entirely
GP can leave, open competing clinic next door, and poach patients/staff

Check each GP contract: does it include restraint clause? Is it: reasonable distance (5+ km typical), reasonable duration (2–3 years typical), reasonable scope (competing GP services)? Can it be enforced?
If weak or missing: negotiate stronger clause (legal advice recommended); if GP won’t agree, consider whether risk is acceptable (is GP flight risk?)
No clarity on: who bills Medicare (clinic or individual GPs)? How are rebates allocated? Any clawback risk?
If billing arrangement is wrong, clinic could be liable for fraudulent claiming; misallocation of rebates = accreditation violation
Request from seller: explanation of Medicare claiming process, evidence of services being billed correctly (sample of claims), any correspondence from Medicare re: compliance
Any Medicare compliance letters or clawbacks not disclosed; claiming process unclear; rebates not being allocated correctly
Pathology collection/ rebate arrangement is verbal only; no agreement with pathology provider
If pathology provider changes or disputes arise, no documentation to back up arrangement; risk of rebate loss
Request written pathology agreement; if only verbal, ask seller to provide written confirmation from pathology provider, or draft one for signature
Pathology agreement clearly documented and transferred to your entity; commission/rebate process clear
GP Payment Model Creates Morale Risk
GP is paid on pure commission (% of own billings) with no guaranteed minimum; if billings drop, GP leaves
Clinic loses GP during recruitment/stabilisation period; expensive to replace; other GPs lose confidence in stable employment
Review each GP’s contract: is there a guaranteed minimum if clinic is slow? How is payment if on leave/sick? Is payment model competitive vs market?
Consider hybrid model (minimum + percentage) if pure commission seems unfair; ensure payment is predictable month-to-month
Landlord Approval for Clinic Use Not Documented
Lease may restrict use (e.g., “medical professional only” but not specified as GP clinic); landlord hasn’t formally consented to clinic operation
Landlord could claim use violation; request change of use; threaten eviction
Review lease for: use clause (is GP clinic permitted?), landlord’s written consent to clinic operation, any conditions (e.g., noise limits, hours of operation)
Confirm in writing with landlord that GP clinic use is permitted; address any conditions (e.g., noise from patient volume, after-hours access); ensure assignment to you signed
Key Person Dependency on Seller or Old Owner
Clinic revenue/operations heavily dependent on one person (e.g., seller is main GP; staff all report to seller; key processes known only to seller)
If key person leaves, clinic falters; difficulty transitioning; loss of patient loyalty
Assess: what if the main GP left tomorrow? Could clinic continue? Are processes documented? Have you met all staff?
Document all key processes; cross-train staff; ensure key staff report to you (not seller); conduct knowledge-transfer sessions with seller
Supplier arrangement involves family member or friend (e.g., pathology referral fee paid to supplier who is related to seller); undisclosed commissions or kickbacks
Potential fraud; supplier receives unfair advantage; pricing may not be competitive
Ask seller directly: any family/friend relationships with suppliers? Any undisclosed commissions? Review supplier agreements for transparency
Get written declaration from seller on any supplier conflicts; audit supplier pricing for competitiveness; ensure all supplier relationships arms-length
No Written Service Level Agreements (SLAs) with Key Vendors
IT provider, accountant, bookkeeper, pathology—no written SLA defining service expectations, response times, or penalty clauses
If service provider fails (e.g., IT down for 2 days, accountant misses BAS deadline), no contractual remedy; dispute becomes difficult
Request copies of all vendor contracts/agreements; if missing, ask vendor for SLA
Develop written SLA with each key vendor; ensure expectations are clear; penalty clauses for material breaches (e.g., IT uptime >99.5%, support response within 2 hours)
Insurance Policies Not Assigned to New Entity
Insurance policies still in seller’s name; not yet transferred to your entity
If claim arises, insurer may deny coverage (insurable interest); you have no coverage; policy could be cancelled if seller fails to renew
Contact each insurer (medical indemnity, public liability, property, cyber); confirm: policies assigned to your entity, premium payment transferred to your accounts
All policies formally reassigned to your entity/ABN; confirmation in writing from each insurer; premium payment responsibility clear
Unsure if staff are paid correctly according to relevant award (e.g., nursing award, healthcare assistant award); may be underpaid or miscategorised
Backpay liability; FWA (Fair Work Ombudsman) compliance issue; staff morale risk if underpaid
Request from practice manager: job descriptions for each staff role, evidence they’re classified correctly under relevant award, pay rate reconciliation showing compliance
All staff correctly classified under relevant award; pay rates verified to be ≥minimum; no backpay exposure

text: 15.4 STAFFING / PAYROLL / HR RISKS

rows: Staff classified as “independent contractors” but actually performing work as employees; no contract or casual arrangement documentation
Employment obligations not met (no super, no paid leave, no notice period compliance, no Award rates); FWA liability; potential backpay; staff claims unfair dismissal

Ask practice manager: are any staff truly independent or genuinely casual? Interview staff about: hours worked, flexibility, control by clinic, integration with clinic. Look for: regular hours, clinic-provided equipment, sole income source
Reclassify any sham contractors as employees; ensure contracts, super, leave, and award compliance from date of reclassification; document decision in writing
Staff working without signed employment contract; terms of employment unclear
No legal basis for employment; disputes over notice period, hours, pay, leave entitlements; termination becomes difficult; backpay claims
Request from practice manager: signed employment contract for every staff member (not casuals); if missing: get signed immediately
All staff (except genuine casuals) have signed current employment contracts before you own the clinic
Employer not paying superannuation; underpaying; not paying on time; wrong fund; employee contributions deducted but not remitted
Statutory breach; ATO penalty; employee claims against clinic; backpay liability; accreditation concern

Request from bookkeeper: evidence of super payments (bank statements to super fund, fund statements), reconcile to payroll register (11.5% minimum of gross wages), verify timing (must be within 28 days of pay date)
All superannuation paid correctly from date you own clinic; no arrears; consider seeking legal indemnity from seller for any pre-ownership super breaches
Staff are covered by industrial award or registered agreement that you’re not aware of (e.g., nursing award, health services agreement); wages may not comply
Backpay liability; compliance breach; potential FWA intervention
Ask practice manager: what award covers each staff member? Request: copies of any registered agreements, NES (National Employment Standards) compliance confirmation, pay slip analysis
All staff correctly classified under relevant award/agreement; wage rates meet minimum; all entitlements (leave, penalties, loadings) correctly applied
Missing Police Checks or Background Verification
Staff (especially those with patient contact or access to drugs) don’t have current police checks or Working With Children checks
Accreditation violation; patient safety risk; liability if staff member causes harm and check was overdue
Request from practice manager: police check and/or WWCC for all clinical staff and anyone with patient contact; verify dates (should be within last 3 years); check status (must be “clear”)
All staff have current police checks (or WWCC where applicable); dates recent (<3 years); status “clear” or “no disqualifying events”
Clinical staff (nurses, allied health) credentials not verified; registrations expired (e.g., NMBA nursing registration, REPS fitness professional); CPD not current
Patient safety risk; accreditation concern; staff not legally permitted to perform role
Request: proof of registration for all clinical staff (NMBA for nurses, AHPRA if applicable, relevant professional body), CPD records, evidence of currency
All clinical staff have current registrations and CPD; verify online via NMBA, AHPRA, or relevant body
Clinic operations heavily reliant on one staff member (e.g., practice manager is only person who knows scheduling, billing, supplier relationships); no cross-training or documentation
If key person leaves, clinic falters; critical processes fail; patients displaced
Assess: can clinic function if main practice manager left tomorrow? Are key processes documented? Have you met all staff?
Document all key processes in writing; train backup staff; ensure key staff feel valued and secure; plan for succession
Unclear or Conflicted Casual Staff Arrangements
Casual staff hours and pay not clearly documented; hours vary inconsistently; payment arrangement unclear; potential for under-reporting or misclassification
Compliance uncertainty; potential underpayment; FWA risk

Request from practice manager: roster for casual staff (last 3 months showing hours worked), pay records showing rates, any written agreement with casuals about: minimum hours, pay rates, notice for cancellation
All casual staff have written agreement specifying: hourly rate, notice for cancellation, availability expectations; hours and pay tracked consistently
Staff with Undisclosed Conflicts of Interest
Staff member has family relationship with GP or other staff; supplier-related staff; unclear impartiality
Potential bias in hiring, scheduling, referrals, payments; accreditation concern; staff morale risk
Ask practice manager and review HR files: any family relationships, conflicts in hiring/promotion, undisclosed financial interests
All conflicts of interest documented; policies clarify how conflicts are managed (recusal from decisions, supervision, disclosure)
GP or staff roster is inconsistent, unfair, or creates fatigue; no clear rostering policy; GPs rostered beyond contracted hours
Staff morale/burnout; GPs leaving; accreditation concern re: fatigue and patient safety
Review roster for past 3 months: are hours consistent with contracts? Is any GP significantly over/under hours? Is rostering fair?
Establish clear rostering policy; ensure roster aligns with contracts; distribute workload fairly; address any over/under rostering immediately
Unclear if payroll tax is applicable; no evidence of registration or payment; potential under-reporting
ATO penalty; backpay liability; compliance breach
Contact VRMA (Victorian Revenue Management) or state payroll tax authority; verify: payroll tax registration status, wage bill threshold (FY2026: $672,500 in Victoria), recent payments
Payroll tax registration confirmed (or exemption documented); payments on-time if applicable; BAS items correct
Staff Turnover Not Tracked or Investigated
High staff turnover (e.g., >30% annually) with no exit interviews or investigation into reasons
Morale issues, burnout, unfair management, or unaddressed problems; accreditation concern
Ask practice manager: turnover rate in past 12 months, reasons for departures (exit interviews conducted?), any patterns (e.g., staff leaving after specific manager?)
Turnover baseline established; exit interviews conducted; any patterns or systemic issues identified and addressed

text: 15.5 OPERATIONAL RISKS AFFECTING CASHFLOW, COMPLIANCE, OR RESALE VALUE

rows: Clinic revenue heavily dependent on one or few patients (e.g., one corporate health check contract for 30% of revenue, or one GP billing 50% of total)
Loss of key patient/GP = significant revenue drop; clinic becomes unsustainable; hard to sell
Analyse revenue by source: top 5 patients/GPs as % of total (should be <20% each); any bulk-bill contracts tied to specific conditions; any sole-source contracts
Diversify revenue; renegotiate key contracts to be less dependent on one party; document patient list ownership clearly
Clinic has pattern of billing errors; any clawback notices from Medicare; billing codes consistently wrong or suboptimal
Clawback = immediate revenue impact; accreditation concern; potential fraud allegations if pattern is systematic
Request from seller: any Medicare compliance letters or clawback notices, sample of claims submitted (randomise by date), billing codes used by each GP
Audit billing codes; ensure all claims are compliant; address any systemic errors; seek professional billing advice if needed
Appointment utilisation <80% (too many empty slots); clinic has capacity but not filling it
Revenue is being left on the table; overhead costs fixed but revenue is low; hard to achieve profitability
Analyse 6-week appointment book: % of slots booked vs available, breakdown by GP, any days/times consistently empty, cancellation rate
Investigate root cause: low patient demand? Poor marketing? Scheduling issues? Implement plan to improve utilisation (e.g., online booking, increased marketing, staggered hours to match demand)
Lease Renewal or Break Clause Risk
Lease break clause within 12 months (without discussion); renewal clause missing (risk of eviction after term expires)
Clinic could lose premises; forced relocation; loss of patient base; business becomes valueless without real estate security
Review lease: end date, renewal options, break clauses, rent review mechanism, landlord reputation, any notices from landlord
Negotiate early renewal or extension (3–5 years) if break/renewal imminent; confirm in writing from landlord; consider lease buyout if terms deteriorated
Vaccines stored incorrectly (wrong temperature, expired, not logged); potential patient harm if expired vaccine given
Loss of accreditation; patient safety incident; regulatory investigation; litigation risk if patient harmed
Inspect fridge: temperature log current and consistent (2–8°C daily), no expired vaccines, backup power/alarm in place, service records current
Correct storage immediately; review vaccination records to identify any at-risk patients; potentially offer revaccination; document incident; report to accreditor if required
Known safety incident (wrong medication, missed diagnosis, patient harm) not reported or investigated; accreditation or regulatory body unaware
Accreditation violation; regulatory investigation if discovered later; patient litigation; reputational damage
Ask practice manager: any known incidents or near-misses that weren’t reported? Review incident register: are all entries documented and resolved?
Investigate immediately; report to accreditor (if required), regulatory body (if harm occurred), insurer (medical indemnity); document remediation; brief team on lessons learned
GPs consistently under-billing (using lower-value item codes than appropriate); potential lost revenue
Immediate revenue impact; difficult to correct without patient impact or GP resistance
Audit billing codes for sample of consultations: are codes appropriate for service? Any systematic under-coding? Compare to industry benchmarks
Review with GPs; ensure codes are appropriate for complexity; consider billing audit or professional coding review; implement monthly billing audit process
Clinic rostered as if 5 FTE GPs, but realistically only have 4 FTE available; vacancies not yet filled; appointment capacity constrained
Revenue below forecast; patient access issues; other GPs burnout from overwork; clinic becomes unattractive to join
Verify: number of GPs contracted vs actual availability, vacancies, timeline to fill, any GPs on notice planning to leave
Accelerate recruitment if vacancies; reduce appointment capacity if cannot fill; consider temporary relief GPs; plan succession for any GPs leaving
Supplier Agreements Not Transferred or in Writing
Suppliers (cleaning, waste, courier, consumables) arrangements are verbal; not transferred to new owner; pricing/terms unclear
Supplier relationship could be terminated; pricing could increase; service standards unclear; no SLA if service fails
Request from seller: list of all suppliers, written agreements/contracts, evidence of transfer to new owner, any pricing agreements
Confirm all supplier relationships in writing; establish SLAs; negotiate pricing; ensure agreements transferred to your entity/ABN
Poor Quality or Incomplete Medical Records
Patient health records are incomplete, poorly organised, or don’t meet accreditation standards (e.g., illegible handwriting, missing progress notes, no medication reconciliation)
Accreditation violation; patient safety risk; difficult to defend against complaints; hard to sell as quality business
Sample 10–20 patient records: are notes legible and complete? Are medication lists current? Are consultation notes adequate?
Implement medical record audit; identify training needs for staff; implement record quality standards; plan record improvement project; consider electronic records upgrade if paper-based
Pathology tests being claimed for billing but rebates not being received; duplicate claims or tests not properly linked to patients
Lost revenue (rebates should go to clinic); potential clawback if duplicates discovered
Audit recent pathology billings: are all tests properly coded and linked to patient records? Are rebates being received and reconciling to billing?
Reconcile pathology billings to rebates monthly; correct any errors; audit sample of tests for proper coding; consider pathology audit if systematic issues
Medical indemnity limits too low (<$2M for GP clinic); public liability inadequate; cyber insurance missing; any exclusions that could apply to clinic’s services
If claim arises, clinic underinsured; forced to pay out-of-pocket; potential bankruptcy if large claim; accreditation concern
Review all policies: coverage limits, exclusions, any gaps, any services not covered
Increase coverage if inadequate; add cyber insurance (increasingly required); ensure all GPs listed on medical indemnity; annual insurance review with broker
Clinic heavily dependent on one software system (e.g., practice management software); no backup if system fails; vendor lock-in (expensive to switch)
System outage = clinic cannot function; vendor increases prices/changes terms; data export difficult if want to switch
Review: all software used, backup plans if system down, vendor SLAs, cost of switching, data portability
Test backup/disaster recovery plan quarterly; ensure daily backups occur; document process to recover if main system fails; negotiate data export rights with vendors
Lease Outgoings Disputes or Hidden Costs
Outgoings reconciliation not transparent; landlord increases outgoings unexpectedly; no cap on outgoings escalation
Unexpected cost increases; inability to forecast expenses; lease becomes uneconomical
Review outgoings reconciliation: last 12 months, breakdown (water, waste, common area), any disputed items, any escalation clause in lease (is there a cap?)
Request detailed outgoings breakdown; query any unusual increases; ensure lease has CPI cap on escalation (not unilateral landlord increase)
Consumables (gloves, syringes, dressings, medications) regularly out of stock or wasted; no inventory management system; staff ordering inconsistently
Higher costs (emergency orders), patient delays (out of stock), potential safety issues if critical items unavailable
Physical inventory check: are consumables in stock and within date? Review ordering: is it regular and planned or ad hoc? Any waste/expiry?
Implement inventory management system; establish par levels for each consumable; assign ordering responsibility; conduct monthly inventory audits
Appointment system is outdated (manual or paper-based); no online booking; scheduling creates frequent double-bookings or no-shows
Patient frustration; revenue loss (no-shows); staff time wasted fixing booking errors
Review appointment system: manual or online? Cancellation/no-show rate? Overbooking frequency? Staff time spent on scheduling
Implement online appointment system (if not already); set no-show policy; monitor overbooking; consider reminders (SMS/email) to reduce no-shows
Last accreditation survey identified non-conformances; corrective actions overdue; no documented remediation
Next survey could result in loss of accreditation; regulatory escalation
Request from seller: accreditation survey report, documented corrective actions, evidence of completion
Complete all non-conformances before next survey; document evidence of remediation; conduct mock audit 3 months before survey
Patient complaints are discouraged or not actively sought; no NPS tracking or patient satisfaction survey; online reviews are negative
Patient dissatisfaction may lead to loss of patients and poor word-of-mouth; accreditation concern re: patient-centred care
Review: complaint process, any formal patient satisfaction surveys, Google/Healthgrades reviews, NPS data if available
Implement patient satisfaction survey (quarterly); monitor online reviews; encourage feedback; respond to complaints promptly; brief team on patient satisfaction goals

text: PART 16 — FINAL OUTPUT: WEEKLY REVIEWS, DELEGATION & DUE DILIGENCE REQUESTS
16.1 “WHAT I MUST KNOW EVERY WEEK” — Critical Weekly Checks
These are the 20 most important weekly metrics for an owner to monitor. Every Friday, you should have answers to all of these questions. If any answer is “red flag,” investigate immediately.

rows: Total Week’s Billings (Yesterday’s Total by 9 AM Daily)
Xero dashboard or daily PMC report
Call practice manager; check for billing errors, unusual claims, or utilisation issues
Check scheduling, cancellations, no-shows; discuss with GP if concerning pattern
Sufficient for 2+ weeks payroll + 1 week expenses
<1 week payroll or unexpected low balance
Investigate transfers; check for clawback or unexpected expense
Contact Medicare; check HPOS for clawback or adjustment notices
Any unplanned absence or late arrival
Contact staff member; arrange relief if needed; debrief practice manager
Complaints register or practice manager report
Escalate to resolution; brief complainant; document response
IT provider status dashboard or manual check
Escalate to IT provider; activate manual backup process
<10 min average wait; no booking delays
Queue at reception; patients waiting >15 min; calls going to voicemail
Add reception staff; review booking efficiency; reduce appointment length if needed
Incident report or practice manager alert
Investigate immediately; brief team; report to accreditor if required
Any staff injury or workers comp claim
Investigate cause; complete incident form; notify WorkCover/insurer; debrief team on prevention
Investigate GP satisfaction, scheduling issues, patient access problems
Review claim; understand reason; correct if error; dispute if disagree
Pay immediately; review supplier terms; assess if negotiation needed
Any staff expressing concerns or morale issues
Have one-on-one with staff member; understand issue; take action if needed
All critical items in stock; no expiries
Any item out of stock; any expiries due
Emergency order; review supplier lead time; prevent future stock-outs
Any urgent issue requiring emergency response
Document; ensure appropriate response; debrief team; update protocols if needed
Practice manager anecdote or feedback system
Investigate morale; remind team of patient appreciation
Take action immediately; assign responsibility; track to completion
Any GP dispute about payment; late payment
Resolve immediately; verify against agreement; ensure timely payment
Unexpected Emails or Calls from Authorities
Letter from Medicare, AHPRA, regulator, or lawyer
Take seriously; involve accountant/lawyer; understand issue; respond promptly

text: Weekly Dashboard Review Meeting (Friday 2–3 PM, 30 Min):

Attendees: Practice Manager + Owner
Topics: Run through the 20 metrics above; discuss any red flags; assign actions; plan for next week
Format:

WEEKLY DASHBOARD REVIEW — [DATE]

METRICS REVIEW:
[ ] Billings on track?
[ ] Consults normal?
[ ] Bank balance healthy?
[ ] Medicare deposits received?
[ ] Staff all present?
[ ] No complaints/incidents?
[ ] Systems online?
[ ] Reception workflow OK?
[ ] No urgent issues?
[ ] Payables current?

RED FLAGS IDENTIFIED:
[List any metrics that deviated from target]

ACTIONS FROM THIS WEEK:
[List specific actions; assign owner; set deadline]

NEXT WEEK PRIORITIES:
[What to focus on next week]

Notes:
[Any other items to follow up]
16.2 “WHAT I CAN SAFELY DELEGATE” — Responsibilities by Role

As the owner, you should not be doing day-to-day operational tasks. Delegate these to your practice manager, bookkeeper, and reception lead. You focus on strategy, compliance, and financial oversight.
16.2.1 PRACTICE MANAGER (Full-Time, Reports to Owner)
Responsibilities:

rows: All staff present; any absences covered; roster confirmed for next week
You’re managing roster; staff scheduling is chaotic
Appointments booked smoothly; patients getting appointments within 2 days (non-urgent); wait times <10 min
You’re taking appointment calls; patients complaining about access
Cash counted and banked; receipts recorded in system; reconciliation to PMC
You’re counting cash or reconciling daily
Complaints logged and resolved within 7 days; patient kept informed; no escalation to owner unless serious
Complaints reaching you without manager attempting resolution
Staff performance issues identified early; minor issues addressed with staff; major issues escalated to owner with documentation
Stock levels maintained; ordering done proactively; no emergency orders; suppliers managed
You’re ordering stock or dealing with out-of-stock issues
Equipment serviced on schedule; repairs requested and tracked; no unexpected breakdowns
You’re arranging repairs or equipment servicing
Dashboard completed by Friday 12 PM; all metrics up-to-date; summary emailed to owner
Meetings held on schedule; agendas planned; notes taken; action items tracked
Incident register maintained; patient feedback collected; any safety concerns flagged immediately
Roster confirmed with GPs; sessions covered; relief staff arranged for gaps; no double-bookings
Hazards identified and addressed; first aid kits stocked; evacuation plan current; staff trained
Documents stored and organised; expiry dates tracked; corrective actions logged

text: Practice Manager Qualifications/Experience: - Healthcare management experience (5+ years preferred) - Experience in general practice or medical clinic - Strong admin/organisational skills - People management skills - Ability to use practice management software - Knowledge of Medicare/health billing basics
16.2.2 BOOKKEEPER (Part-Time, Can Be Outsourced)
Responsibilities:

rows: Cash and cheques deposited daily; reconciliation to bank statement; no unexplained gaps
Invoices received, coded, and entered into system; ready for approval; paid on time
Payroll runs on schedule; all staff paid on agreed day; timesheets approved; super calculated and paid within 28 days
P&L generated by 5th of month; variances explained; provided to owner; reconciles to bank account
Invoices paid on time; no overdue invoices; payment runs processed; AP aging report available
You’re managing which invoices to pay
Patient out-of-pocket debts tracked; follow-up letters sent; aged AR <5% of monthly revenue
BAS items tracked monthly; BAS prepared within 2 weeks of quarter-end; ready for accountant/submission
Each GP’s billings reconciled to payment; invoice matches billings; payment processed on time
Expenses coded correctly for tax and reporting; consistent with chart of accounts
You’re deciding how to categorise expenses
Super liability calculated and tracked; payments made within 28 days; records maintained
Invoices filed by supplier; pricing verified; any anomalies flagged
Simple 12-week cash forecast prepared; major upcoming expenses flagged; owner briefed on cash position

text: Bookkeeper Qualifications: - Accounting qualification (Cert IV Bookkeeping or equivalent) - Practice management/medical clinic experience preferred - Proficiency in Xero or similar accounting software - Attention to detail - Can be part-time (10–15 hrs/week) or outsourced to accountant’s staff
16.2.3 RECEPTION LEAD (Full-Time, Reports to Practice Manager)
Responsibilities:

rows: Appointments booked smoothly; average wait 2–5 days; online booking working; no voicemail overflow
You’re taking calls or managing bookings
Patients checked in quickly; payments collected; records updated; wait time <5 min
All calls answered promptly; voicemails returned; patient enquiries resolved; hold times <2 min
Patient files prepared for GPs; returned after consulting; no missing files
Stationery/forms stocked; appointment cards/forms available; ordered in advance
Reception area clean and professional; no clutter; magazines current; bathroom supplies stocked
New patient details entered into system; contact details current; no duplicate records
Patient complaints logged; initial empathetic response; escalated to manager as needed
Pathology specimens collected and sent correctly; tracking numbers noted; results logged
Voicemail updated; after-hours number on message; urgent messages routed appropriately
Reminders sent (SMS/email) day before; response tracked; no-show follow-up completed

text: Reception Lead Qualifications: - Customer service experience - General practice reception experience preferred - Proficiency with appointment software - Basic computer skills - Patient-focused, empathetic demeanor - Problem-solving skills
16.2.4 WHAT YOU (OWNER) SHOULD STILL DO

rows: Only you know the long-term direction
Accountable for profitability and financial decisions
GPs report to owner; payment disputes, contract issues need owner authority
Lease renewal, major capex, service expansion, pricing changes
Update board/investors on progress, risks, opportunities
Only owner can make major financial decisions, sign cheques for large amounts
Only owner can engage/brief lawyers and insurers
You set the annual budget and strategic direction
30-min meeting to stay across key metrics

text: 16.3 “WHAT TO REQUEST IMMEDIATELY FOR DUE DILIGENCE” — Top 20 Due Diligence Items

Issue this list to the seller’s agent/lawyer immediately upon signing the purchase agreement. Request all items within 14 days of settlement (use settlement as the hard deadline). Many of these should ideally be obtained before settlement so you can do full due diligence and negotiate indemnities.

rows: Establishes payment terms, notice period, restraint clauses; any gaps = verbal arrangements you’ll need to formalise
Executed Lease Agreement & Landlord Assignment Consent
Confirms you have legal right to occupy; assignment must be in place; no surprises at settlement
Verify seller’s representations about clinic profitability; understand revenue trends; identify any red flags
Confirms clinic’s Medicare registration; needed to set up rebate receiving
All Insurance Certificates (Medical Indemnity, Public Liability, Property)
Verify current coverage; identify any gaps; arrange transfer before settlement
Verify cash position; understand payment patterns; identify any large unexplained transfers or clawbacks
Ensure all GPs are currently registered; no conditions or suspensions; can practise in Australia
RACGP Accreditation Certificate & Last Survey Report
Know baseline compliance; identify any non-conformances to address; plan next survey
Verify all staff are under contracts (not casual or verbal); understand terms, notice periods, restraint clauses
Verify no super arrears; check staff are paid correctly; identify any backpay liability
Master List of All Contracts & Agreements
Don’t want to discover undisclosed contracts post-settlement; ensure complete picture
Cold Chain / Vaccine Fridge Temperature Logs (Last 12 Months)
Verify fridge is compliant; identify any out-of-range temperatures requiring investigation
Identify any systemic issues; understand clinic’s safety culture; any patterns of complaints
Understand all systems in use; confirm you’ll have access post-settlement; identify costs
Confirm pathology relationship transferring to you; understand commission/rebate structure; verify written agreement
All Policies & Procedures (Clinical, WHS, Privacy, Complaints)
Understand operational framework; identify any gaps or outdated policies
Review quality of medical record-keeping; identify any gaps or deficiencies; assess training needs
Understand clinic’s billing pattern; identify any errors or clawback risk; verify rebate deposits match claims
Understand current staffing; identify any gaps or key person dependency; plan for transition
Copies of All Supplier Agreements (IT, Cleaning, Waste, Consumables)
Understand all supplier relationships; verify they’ll transfer to you; identify any cost opportunities

text: Due Diligence Request Template (to send to seller’s agent/lawyer)

URGENT: DUE DILIGENCE REQUEST — [PRACTICE NAME] ACQUISITION

Settlement Date: [DATE]

To complete due diligence and facilitate smooth settlement, please provide the following 
documents within 14 days of this request:

CRITICAL ITEMS (Required before settlement):
[ ] Signed GP Contractor Agreement(s) [all GPs, current and dated within 3 years]
[ ] Executed Lease Agreement with Schedule of Amendments
[ ] Landlord's Written Consent to Assignment of Lease
[ ] Medical Indemnity Insurance Certificate of Currency
[ ] Public Liability Insurance Certificate of Currency
[ ] 12-month P&L (month-by-month, actual vs budget)
[ ] Bank Account Statements (last 12 months, all accounts)
[ ] Copy of Medicare Provider Agreement
[ ] AHPRA Registration Status for all GPs (screenshot or print)
[ ] RACGP Accreditation Certificate
[ ] Last RACGP Survey Report (with findings)
[ ] All Signed Employment Contracts (staff)
[ ] Payroll Register (last 12 months, all staff)
[ ] Evidence of Superannuation Payments (statements or bank transfers)
[ ] Master List of All Contracts/Agreements (with location of originals)
[ ] Cold Chain Temperature Log (last 12 months, vaccine fridge)
[ ] Complaint Register (last 24 months)
[ ] Incident Register (last 24 months)

HIGH PRIORITY ITEMS (Within 14 days of settlement):
[ ] All Software Licenses & IT Agreements
[ ] Pathology Collection Agreement
[ ] All Clinic Policies & Procedures
[ ] Sample Patient Health Records (10–20 for quality review)
[ ] Staffing Structure Chart & Current Job Descriptions
[ ] All Supplier Agreements (IT, Cleaning, Waste, Consumables, Stationery)
[ ] List of All Passwords/Access Credentials (banking, software, systems)
[ ] Medicare Claiming History (last 6 months, sample of claims)
[ ] Accreditation Compliance Calendar (next survey date, any outstanding items)

Please note: We will require full access to practice management software, banking, 
and other systems within 48 hours of settlement to ensure business continuity.

If any items are unavailable or require clarification, please advise in writing with 
an explanation. We may require indemnities for any missing documents or undisclosed 
items.

Due Diligence Officer: [Your name]
Contact: [Your email / phone]
16.4 CRITICAL QUESTIONS TO ASK SELLER (In Writing)

Beyond the document list above, ask the seller to answer these questions in writing (you may want to use these as a statutory declaration). Get answers before settlement if possible; if after settlement, within 7 days.

rows: Are all GPs currently working under signed contractor agreements? Any verbal arrangements or letter agreements only?
Identifies unsigned arrangements that could create disputes
If any unsigned: insist on signed agreement before paying that GP
Have any GPs given notice to leave or expressed intent to leave in the past 12 months?
Identifies flight risk; allows you to plan recruitment or re-negotiate
If flight risk identified: engage GP early; understand concerns; offer retention if key
Are there any undisclosed financial arrangements with GPs (e.g., loans, advances, personal guarantees for equipment)?
Identifies hidden liabilities you may inherit
If any: document in settlement; negotiate indemnity if liability-bearing
Are all staff correctly classified under relevant awards (nursing award, health services award)? Have pay rates been verified as compliant?
Request evidence of award compliance review; obtain indemnity if risk
Have any staff been paid below NMW or without correct super contributions? Any backpay owing?
Identifies wage theft or non-compliance liability
If any: document; obtain indemnity from seller; rectify immediately
Are there any outstanding workers’ compensation claims or WHS incidents that haven’t been resolved?
Request details; obtain indemnity; notify your insurer
Has the clinic received any compliance letters from Medicare, AHPRA, RACGP, health complaints body, or VRMA?
If any: obtain full correspondence; understand resolution; obtain indemnity if unresolved
Are there any disputes, complaints, or legal claims against the clinic or GPs (patient litigation, FWA claims, landlord disputes, supplier disputes)?
Request details; obtain indemnity; brief your lawyer
Does the lease have any unusual clauses, break options, or renewal restrictions the buyer should know about?
Clarify all key terms; negotiate extension if break/renewal imminent
Has the landlord agreed to the assignment of the lease to the new owner, or is assignment still pending?
Confirms you’ll have legal occupancy post-settlement
Confirm in writing from landlord before settlement
Are there any planned building renovations, maintenance, or landlord disputes that could disrupt clinic operations?
Plan for contingencies; maintain normal operations if renovation underway
Has the pathology relationship been confirmed in writing as transferring to the new owner? Are commission rates and rebate arrangements clear?
Confirms pathology supply and revenue flow
Confirm transfer in writing from pathology provider; verify agreement transferred
Are there any undisclosed debts, loans, finance agreements, or personal guarantees the clinic is liable for?
Identifies financial liability you may inherit
Request full disclosure; obtain indemnity; arrange refinancing if needed
Has the clinic ever been subject to an ATO audit, payroll tax audit, or other regulatory investigation?
Identifies audit risk or compliance issues
Request details; obtain indemnity; brief your accountant
Are there any patient safety incidents or near-misses in the last 12 months that haven’t been formally reported or investigated?
Identifies unreported incidents or systemic issues
Request incident summary; investigate if concerning; report to accreditor if required
Are there any key staff members who might leave soon, or any retention concerns you should know about?
Engage key staff early; understand concerns; offer retention if needed
What is the typical patient profile (demographics, growth rate, retention rate)? Any loss of major patient groups in the past 12 months?
Analyse patient data; plan for growth or retention if needed
Are there any supplier relationships or arrangements that could be affected by the change of ownership? Any commitments you haven’t disclosed?
Confirm all supplier relationships transfer smoothly; renegotiate if terms unfavourable
Are there any undisclosed verbal arrangements with staff, GPs, or suppliers that you consider important?
Identifies informal arrangements you need to formalise
Formalise all arrangements in writing post-settlement
If I find undisclosed items or misrepresentations post-settlement, how will disputes be resolved?
Ensure indemnity language is clear; agree on dispute resolution mechanism in purchase agreement

text: D. RED FLAG EARLY WARNING CHECKLIST
If any of these occur, investigate immediately and escalate:
☐ Any unplanned GP absence without coverage arranged
☐ Any Medicare compliance letter or clawback notice received
☐ Any patient complaint unresolved >7 days
☐ Any staff resignation or notice to leave
☐ Any system outage lasting >1 hour
☐ Any bank balance drop >20% month-to-month
☐ Any weekly billings <80% of target for 2+ weeks
☐ Any workers’ compensation claim or workplace incident
☐ Any feedback from landlord about property or lease issues
☐ Any message from insurer re: coverage gaps or renewal issues
☐ Any accreditation compliance alert
☐ Any patient safety incident
☐ Any undisclosed financial liability discovered
☐ Any staff expressing morale/burnout concerns
☐ Any supplier relationship disruption
CONCLUSION & NEXT STEPS
Summary
This 90-day action plan and dashboard framework provides a comprehensive roadmap for a new GP clinic owner to:
Secure the business (days 1–7): Gain access and control
Understand the business (days 8–30): Deep analysis and documentation
Optimise operations (days 31–60): Implement systems and controls
Build for growth (days 61–90): Strategy and foundation for long-term success

The owner dashboard ensures you stay across critical metrics every week. The delegation framework ensures you focus on strategy and compliance, not day-to-day operations. The red flag section identifies the 20+ highest-risk areas in a typical GP acquisition and provides mitigation strategies.
Key Principles
Trust but verify: Seller representations should be validated against independent data
Document everything: Legal clarity on all major arrangements
Delegate ruthlessly: Practice manager, bookkeeper, and reception lead handle day-to-day
Measure relentlessly: Dashboard discipline enables early intervention
Comply rigorously: RACGP accreditation and regulatory requirements are non-negotiable
Communicate clearly: Weekly staff meetings, monthly investor updates, transparent decision-making
Post-Day 90

Once the first 90 days are complete, you’ll have: - Secured all access and documentation - Identified the top 3–5 improvement opportunities - Established financial control and weekly metrics - Built a capable team with clear roles - Planned for the next 12 months - Positioned the clinic for sustainable growth
The next phase (months 4–12) focuses on execution of the 12-month operational plan, continuous improvement, and preparing for the first annual review as new owner.
End of Part 13–16

Document prepared for general practice acquisitions in Victoria, Australia. All recommendations based on RACGP accreditation standards, National Health Records Act, Fair Work Act, and standard GP clinic management practices. Consult with your lawyer, accountant, and insurance broker for entity-specific advice.
